19. Flashcards

  • Q: Cryoglobulinaemia Type II cause?

A: HCV (80–90%)

  • Q: Cryoglobulinaemia Type I association?

A: Lymphoproliferative malignancy

  • Q: Cryoglobulinaemia Type III association?

A: SLE, RA, Sjögren's

  • Q: Hallmark complement?

A: Low C4 (classical pathway)

  • Q: RF in cryoglobulinaemia?

A: High (IgM monoclonal has RF activity)

  • Q: EM finding?

A: Microtubules 30–50nm (hollow)

  • Q: Sample handling?

A: WARM tube (37°C)

  • Q: Meltzer's triad?

A: Purpura, arthralgia, weakness

  • Q: Type II mild Rx?

A: DAAs

  • Q: Severe Rx?

A: PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids

  • Q: Monoclonal IgM κ = ?

A: Type II

  • Q: Microtubules = ?

A: Cryoglobulin precipitates (pathognomonic)

  • Q: ANCA in cryo?

A: Usually negative

  • Q: Cryoglobulins tube?

A: WARM (37°C)

20. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — HCV accounts for 80–90% of Type II (mixed) cryoglobulinaemia.
  2. B — Classical pathway consumption → Low C4 (hallmark); C3 low/normal.
  3. B — Type I = monoclonal = lymphoproliferative (Waldenström, myeloma, CLL, lymphoma).
  4. C — 30–50nm hollow microtubules (cryoglobulin precipitates) = pathognomonic.
  5. B — Cryoglobulins precipitate at <37°C; if tube cools, they precipitate in tube → false negative. Must send warm (37°C).
  6. B — Meltzer's triad: palpable purpura, arthralgia, weakness/fatigue.
  7. C — Mild HCV cryo: DAAs alone (cure HCV → cryoglobulins clear in 90%).
  8. B — Severe (RPGN, nephrotic, alveolar haemorrhage): PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids acutely, then DAAs.
  9. B — Monoclonal IgM has rheumatoid factor activity → High RF.
  10. B — ANCA usually negative in cryoglobulinaemia; positive in ANCA vasculitis.

SBAs

  1. B — Moderate-severe HCV cryo (proteinuria 2g): DAAs + Rituximab. DAAs alone for <1g proteinuria.
  2. B — Type I = malignancy-driven → treat underlying clone (rituximab-chemo).
  3. B — SLE cryoglobulinaemia: standard lupus nephritis protocol (MMF + steroids).
  4. B — RPGN + alveolar haemorrhage = life-threatening vasculitis → PLEX + Rituximab + IV steroids.
  5. B — Cold sample → cryoglobulins precipitate in tube → false negative. Repeat in warm tube.
  6. B — Persistent cryoglobulins post-HCV cure = persistent B-cell clone → Rituximab targets RF-producing clone.
  7. B — Microtubules 30–50nm hollow = cryoglobulin EM signature.
  8. B — Monoclonal cryoglobulin = screen for lymphoproliferative (myeloma, Waldenström, lymphoma).
  9. B — Mononeuritis multiplex = severe vasculitis → PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids.
  10. B — Comprehensive monitoring: HCV SVR12, cryoglobulin levels, C4, RF, renal function, proteinuria.

21. Summary

Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis is a Must Know 🔴 immune-complex GN topic.

Classification: Type I (monoclonal, malignancy), Type II (mixed monoclonal+polyclonal, HCV 80–90%), Type III (polyclonal, SLE/RA).

Labs: Low C4 (hallmark), High RF, Cryoglobulins+ (send warm tube), HCV RNA+ (Type II).

Renal: MPGN pattern, subendothelial microtubules (30–50nm) on EM.

Clinical: Meltzer's triad (purpura, arthralgia, weakness) + neuropathy, skin ulcers.

Management: Type II (HCV) — DAAs first; add rituximab if moderate/severe; PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids for RPGN/severe. Type I — treat malignancy. Type III — treat autoimmune.

PLEX indications: RPGN, severe neuropathy, hyperviscosity, alveolar haemorrhage.

Exam focus: Three types, Low C4/High RF, microtubules EM, warm tube, DAAs for HCV, PLEX+Rituximab for severe, Meltzer's triad.

topics.title: Post Streptococcal Gn Psgn
topics.html:
Topic: Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN)]], [[Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome)]], [[Infective Endocarditis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
PSGN = immune-complex GN following group A β-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS) infection (pharyngitis/impetigo). Peak 1–3 weeks post-infection. Children 5–15yr. Nephritic syndrome: haematuria (cola-coloured urine), RBC casts, hypertension, oliguria, periorbital oedema. Low C3, normal C4. ASO titre ↑. Subepithelial "humps" on EM. Self-limited (95% childhood recovery). Adults: worse prognosis, ↑ CKD risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise classic post-strep presentation (nephritic syndrome 1–3 wks post-GAS)
  • Interpret serology (ASO, anti-DNase B, C3, C4)
  • Differentiate from other nephritic causes (IgA, anti-GBM, ANCA, lupus, C3G)
  • Manage supportively (BP control, volume, antibiotics for residual infection)
  • Identify indications for biopsy (atypical features, adult, persistent)
  • Counsel on prognosis (childhood vs adult)

2. Epidemiology & Aetiology

ParameterDetail
OrganismGroup A β-haemolytic Streptococcus (GAS) — M-types 1, 2, 4, 12, 25, 49 (nephritogenic)
Infection sitePharyngitis (most common) or Impetigo (skin) — M-types 49, 55, 57, 59, 60
Latency1–3 weeks post-pharyngitis; 3–6 weeks post-impetigo
AgeChildren 5–15 years (peak); rare <3yr, >40yr
SexMale = Female
GeographyWorldwide; ↓ in developed (antibiotics), still common in developing

3. Pathogenesis

  • Immune complexes (streptococcal antigen + antibody) deposit in subepithelial space of GBM
  • Antigens: Streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxin B (SpeB), nephritis-associated plasmin receptor (NAPlr), streptococcal DNAase B
  • Complement: Alternative pathway activation → Low C3, Normal C4
  • EM: Subepithelial "humps" (large, dome-shaped deposits on epithelial side of GBM)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTypical (Child)Atypical / Adult
Gross haematuriaCoca-cola / tea-coloured urine (90%)Microscopic may predominate
Periorbital oedemaMorning, improves by eveningMay be anasarca
Hypertension60–80% (volume + RAAS)Often severe
Oliguria/AKIMild-moderate (20–30%)More severe, dialysis may be needed
Flank painRarePossible
FeverLow-grade (if recent infection)Uncommon
ASO titre↑ (peaks 3–5 wks)May be negative (impetigo)

5. Laboratory Findings

TestPSGN TypicalDifferential Clues
UrineHaematuria (RBC >50/HPF), RBC casts, proteinuria (subnephrotic 0.5–3g/day)RBC casts = glomerular
Serum C3Low (85–90%) — normalises 6–8 wksPersistently low → C3G, MPGN, SLE, endocarditis
Serum C4NormalLow C4 = classical pathway (SLE, cryo, MPGN)
ASO titre↑ >200 IU/mL (or rising) — peaks 3–5 wksImpetigo: ASO may be low → Anti-DNase B better
Anti-DNase B↑ (more sensitive for skin infection)Use if ASO negative, impetigo history
Anti-streptolysin O (ASO)Not streptolysin O specific—
Throat/skin cultureMay be negative (latent phase)Confirms recent GAS if positive
CBCNormal or leukocytosis—
C3 normalisation by 8 weeks = typical PSGN. Persistent low C3 >12 weeks → consider C3G, MPGN, SLE, infective endocarditis.

6. Differential Diagnosis (Nephritic Syndrome)

ConditionC3C4Key Differentiator
PSGNLowNormalRecent GAS, ASO↑, humps EM, self-limited
IgA NephropathyNormalNormalCoincident with URTI (not 1–3 wks after), no ASO rise
Anti-GBMNormal/LowNormalLinear IgG, anti-GBM+, pulmonary bleed
ANCA VasculitisNormalNormalp-ANCA/c-ANCA, pauci-immune, no ASO
Lupus NephritisLowLowFull-house IF, ANA/dsDNA+, systemic features
C3G (DDD/C3GN)LowNormalC3 dominant IF, C3NeF+, persistent low C3 >12 wks
Infective EndocarditisLowLow/NormalBlood cultures +, murmur, embolic phenomena
CryoglobulinaemiaLowLowCryoglobulins+, HCV+, low C4

7. Indications for Renal Biopsy

IndicationReason
Adult >40 yearsHigher malignancy/vasculitis risk; PSGN rare
Atypical featuresNephrotic-range proteinuria, severe AKI, persistent low C3 >12 wks
Systemic symptomsFever, weight loss, rash, arthralgia (suggest SLE, vasculitis, IE)
Persistent proteinuria/haematuria>6 months (risk of chronic GN)
Uncertain diagnosisDifferentiate from IgA, C3G, lupus, anti-GBM
Children with classic presentation: Biopsy NOT routinely needed.

8. Management

Supportive (Mainstay)

IssueManagement
HypertensionACEi/ARB (if no hyperkalaemia/AKI contraindication), CCB, diuretics; target <130/80
Volume overloadLoop diuretic (furosemide) ± fluid restriction
AKIConservative — avoid nephrotoxins, monitor electrolytes, dialysis if severe (rare in kids)
Residual GAS infectionPenicillin V 500mg QID × 10 days (or Amoxicillin) — eradicates carriage, prevents spread
Contact prophylaxisHousehold contacts: throat culture → treat if GAS+

Immunosuppression

SituationApproach
Typical childhood PSGNNOT INDICATED — self-limited
Adult with severe diseaseConsider steroids ± cyclophosphamide if crescentic >50% / rapidly progressive
Persistent low C3 + proteinuria >3.5gEvaluate for C3G/MPGN — treat accordingly

9. Prognosis

Children (95% complete recovery)

OutcomeFrequency
Complete resolution95% (proteinuria/haematuria resolve by 1–2 yrs)
Persistent microscopic haematuria5–10% (benign)
Proteinuria >1yr2–5%
CKD/ESRD<1%

Adults (Worse)

OutcomeFrequency
Complete resolution50–60%
Persistent proteinuria30–40%
CKD/ESRD10–20%
RecurrenceRare (but may indicate C3G/mutation)
Risk factors for poor outcome: Age >40, nephrotic syndrome, severe AKI (dialysis), crescents >50%, persistent low C3 >12 wks.

10. Complications

ComplicationManagement
Hypertensive encephalopathyIV labetalol/nicardipine, ICU
Acute pulmonary oedemaIV furosemide, nitrates, NIPPV
AKI requiring dialysisTemporary (usually) — supportive
Post-strep reactive arthritisNSAIDs, self-limited
Rheumatic feverSeparate entity (Jones criteria) — not caused by PSGN

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- PSGN: Nephritic syndrome 1–3 wks post-GAS pharyngitis/impetigo. Children 5–15yr.
- Classic: Cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema, hypertension, RBC casts.
- Labs: Low C3, Normal C4, ASO titre ↑ (peaks 3–5 wks), Anti-DNase B for impetigo.
- EM: Subepithelial "humps" (pathognomonic).
- C3 normalises by 8 wks — persistent low C3 >12 wks = NOT PSGN (think C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE).
- Management: Supportive (BP, volume, antibiotics for carriage). No steroids in typical childhood PSGN.
- Prognosis: Children 95% recovery; Adults 10–20% ESRD.
- Biopsy NOT needed in classic childhood presentation.
- Differential: IgA (synpharyngitic), anti-GBM, ANCA, lupus, C3G, IE.

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
PSGN = immediate post-infectionLatency 1–3 wks (pharyngitis), 3–6 wks (impetigo)
ASO always positiveImpetigo: ASO often negative → use Anti-DNase B
Low C3 + Low C4 = PSGNPSGN = Low C3 + Normal C4. Low C4 = SLE, cryo, IE
Steroids help childhood PSGNNo steroids — self-limited, supportive only
C3 stays low forever in PSGNC3 normalises by 6–8 wks — persistent low C3 = C3G/MPGN/SLE/IE
Adults recover like childrenAdults: 10–20% ESRD, higher persistent proteinuria
Biopsy all nephritic syndromeChildhood classic PSGN: NO biopsy needed
PSGN causes rheumatic feverSeparate entity — PSGN ≠ rheumatic fever (different M-types, different immune response)
Impetigo PSGN = throat PSGNDifferent M-types (49, 55–60), longer latency, ASO may be negative
ASO titre = single value diagnosticRising titre (convalescent > acute) more diagnostic than single value

13. Mnemonics

  • PSGN triad: Haematuria + Hypertension + Oedema = HHO
  • PSGN complement: C3 Low, C4 Normal = C3L C4N
  • PSGN latency: Pharyngitis 1–3 wks; Impetigo 3–6 wks = PHARYNGITIS SHORT, IMPETIGO LONG
  • PSGN EM: Humps = Huge Under Membrane Subepithelial
  • ASO alternative: Anti-DNase B for Body (skin/impetigo)
  • Prognosis: Kids = 95% OK; Adults = 20% ESRD = K95 A20

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Post-Strep GN))

Aetiology

Organism[Group A Strep (GAS)]

Types[M1,2,4,12,25,49 pharyngitis; M49,55-60 impetigo]

Latency[1-3 wks pharyngitis; 3-6 wks impetigo]

Clinical

Age[Children 5-15yr peak]

Presentation[Cola urine, Periorbital oedema, HTN, RBC casts]

AKI[20-30% mild-moderate]

Labs

Urine[Haematuria, RBC casts, Proteinuria <3.5g]

Complement[Low C3, Normal C4 → normalises 8 wks]

Serology[ASO↑ (peaks 3-5 wks), Anti-DNase B (impetigo)]

EM[Subepithelial HUMPS]

Differential

IgA[Synpharyngitic, no ASO rise]

Anti-GBM[Linear IgG, pulmonary]

ANCA[Pauci-immune, ANCA+]

SLE[Low C3+C4, Full-house]

C3G[Persistent low C3, C3 dominant]

IE[Blood cultures+, murmur]

Management

Supportive[BP control, Diuretics, Antibiotics for carriage]

No_Steroids[Childhood typical]

Adult_Severe[Consider if crescents>50%/RPGN]

Prognosis

Children[95% recovery]

Adults[10-20% ESRD]

C3_Course[Normalises 8 wks → if not = C3G/MPGN/SLE/IE]

```

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Latency period (pharyngitis vs impetigo)
  2. Classic triad (haematuria, HTN, oedema)
  3. Complement profile (Low C3, Normal C4)
  4. ASO vs Anti-DNase B indication
  5. EM finding (subepithelial humps)
  6. C3 normalisation timeline
  7. When to biopsy
  8. Childhood vs adult prognosis

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Classic presentation, latency, Low C3/Normal C4, ASO/anti-DNase B, humps EM, supportive Rx, no steroids in kids, C3 normalises 8 wks
Should Know 🟡Impetigo vs pharyngitis differences, biopsy indications, adult prognosis, differential diagnosis table, contact prophylaxis
Nice to Know 🟢Nephritogenic M-types, SpeB/NAPlr antigens, genetic susceptibility, long-term follow-up studies

18. MCQs (10)

  1. Typical latency between streptococcal pharyngitis and PSGN:

A. 1–3 days

B. 1–3 weeks

C. 1–3 months

D. 3–6 weeks

E. Immediate

  1. Characteristic complement profile in PSGN:

A. Low C3, Low C4

B. Low C3, Normal C4

C. Normal C3, Low C4

D. Normal C3, Normal C4

E. Low C3, Low C4, Low CH50

  1. EM hallmark of PSGN:

A. Subendothelial deposits

B. Mesangial deposits

C. Subepithelial "humps"

D. Intramembranous dense deposits

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. ASO titre interpretation — most diagnostic:

A. Single value >200 IU/mL

B. Rising titre (convalescent > acute)

C. Falling titre

D. Any positive value

  1. Best serology for post-impetigo PSGN:

A. ASO titre

B. Anti-DNase B

C. Anti-hyaluronidase

C. Anti-streptokinase

E. Anti-NADase

  1. C3 normalisation in typical childhood PSGN:

A. 1–2 weeks

B. 6–8 weeks

C. 3–4 months

C. 6 months

  1. Management of typical childhood PSGN:

A. High-dose steroids

B. Supportive (BP, volume, antibiotics for carriage) — NO steroids

C. Cyclophosphamide

D. Plasma exchange

E. Rituximab

  1. Prognosis in children with PSGN:

A. 50% recovery

B. 95% complete recovery

C. 70% recovery

D. 80% recovery

E. 60% recovery

  1. Indication for renal biopsy in PSGN:

C. All adults

A. Normal recovery

B. C3G, MPGN, SLE, or Infective Endocarditis

19. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 8-year-old boy, 2 weeks post-strep throat, cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema, BP 130/85. Urine: RBC casts, proteinuria 1.2g/day. C3 low, C4 normal, ASO 400. Management:

A. Prednisolone 1mg/kg

B. Supportive: ACEi/ARB for BP, furosemide for oedema, penicillin V × 10 days

C. IV methylprednisolone 500mg × 3 days

D. Cyclophosphamide 500mg/m²

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 10-year-old girl, post-impetigo PSGN. ASO negative. Best confirmatory serology:

A. Repeat ASO in 2 weeks

B. Anti-DNase B titre

C. Anti-hyaluronidase

D. Anti-streptokinase

E. Throat culture

  1. 35-year-old man, PSGN presentation (cola urine, HTN, RBC casts), C3 low, C4 normal, ASO 800. Biopsy: 60% crescents. Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Steroids ± cyclophosphamide (RPGN protocol)

C. No treatment needed

D. Plasma exchange

E. Rituximab

  1. Child with PSGN, C3 still low at 14 weeks. Next step:

A. Repeat ASO

B. Renal biopsy (exclude C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE)

C. Start steroids

C. Increase penicillin dose

E. Observe — will normalise eventually

  1. Adult with PSGN — risk factor for poor prognosis:

A. Age <30

B. Age >40, nephrotic proteinuria, severe AKI, crescents >50%

C. Male sex

D. High ASO titre

E. Short latency

  1. Prophylaxis for household contacts of PSGN patient:

A. All get penicillin × 10 days

B. Throat culture → treat if GAS positive

C. Azithromycin × 5 days for all

D. No prophylaxis needed

E. Benzathine penicillin IM for all

  1. Differentiating PSGN from IgA nephropathy:

A. PSGN: synpharyngitic haematuria

B. PSGN: 1–3 wks POST-infection; IgA: DURING/COINCIDENT with URTI

C. IgA: Low C3

D. PSGN: No RBC casts

E. IgA: ASO positive

  1. Impetigo-associated PSGN vs Pharyngitis-associated:

A. Shorter latency

B. Longer latency (3–6 wks), different M-types (49,55-60), ASO often negative

C. Higher C3

D. No hypertension

E. No RBC casts

  1. 95% recovery statistic in PSGN refers to:

A. Adults

B. Children (5–15yr)

C. All ages

D. Impetigo-only

E. Pharyngitis-only

  1. Monitoring post-PSGN — persistent proteinuria at 1 year:

A. Normal

B. Investigate for C3G, MPGN, IgA, secondary causes

C. Start steroids

D. Repeat ASO

E. Increase ACEi dose only

20. Flashcards

  • Q: PSGN latency pharyngitis?

A: 1–3 weeks

  • Q: PSGN latency impetigo?

A: 3–6 weeks

  • Q: PSGN classic triad?

A: Haematuria, HTN, Periorbital oedema

  • Q: PSGN complement?

A: Low C3, Normal C4 (normalises 8 wks)

  • Q: PSGN EM?

A: Subepithelial humps

  • Q: ASO peak?

A: 3–5 weeks post-infection

  • Q: Impetigo PSGN serology?

A: Anti-DNase B (ASO often negative)

  • Q: PSGN management kids?

A: Supportive only — NO steroids

  • Q: PSGN prognosis kids?

A: 95% complete recovery

  • Q: PSGN prognosis adults?

A: 10–20% ESRD

  • Q: C3 normalisation?

A: 6–8 weeks

  • Q: Persistent low C3 >12 wks?

A: Think C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE

  • Q: Biopsy indication?

A: Adult >40, atypical, persistent low C3, nephrotic

  • Q: PSGN vs IgA timing?

A: PSGN = POST-infection (1–3 wks); IgA = DURING URTI

  • Q: Contact prophylaxis?

A: Throat culture → treat if GAS+

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Latency 1–3 weeks after pharyngitis; 3–6 weeks after impetigo.
  2. B — Classic PSGN: Alternative pathway activation → Low C3, Normal C4.
  3. C — Subepithelial "humps" = large dome-shaped deposits on epithelial side of GBM.
  4. B — Rising titre (convalescent > acute) more diagnostic than single value.
  5. B — Anti-DNase B more sensitive for skin/impetigo strains (ASO often negative).
  6. B — C3 normalises by 6–8 weeks in typical PSGN.
  7. B — Childhood PSGN: supportive only (BP, volume, abx for carriage). No immunosuppression.
  8. B — 95% of children recover completely.
  9. B — Biopsy if adult >40, atypical, persistent low C3 >12 wks, nephrotic syndrome, systemic symptoms.
  10. B — Persistent low C3 >12 wks = NOT typical PSGN; consider C3G, MPGN, SLE, infective endocarditis.

SBAs

  1. B — Typical childhood PSGN: supportive management only. Penicillin V eradicates carriage.
  2. B — Post-impetigo PSGN: ASO often negative; Anti-DNase B is the sensitive test.
  3. B — Adult with crescentic RPGN (60% crescents): consider immunosuppression (steroids ± CyC).
  4. B — Persistent low C3 at 14 weeks = atypical → biopsy to exclude C3G, MPGN, SLE, IE.
  5. B — Poor prognosis: age >40, nephrotic proteinuria, severe AKI, crescents >50%.
  6. B — Household contacts: culture first, treat only if GAS positive (avoid unnecessary antibiotics).
  7. B — Key timing difference: PSGN = 1–3 wks POST-infection; IgA = DURING/COINCIDENT with URTI.
  8. B — Impetigo PSGN: longer latency, M-types 49/55–60, ASO often negative.
  9. B — 95% recovery refers specifically to children (5–15yr). Adults: 10–20% ESRD.
  10. B — Persistent proteinuria >1yr = investigate for underlying chronic GN (C3G, MPGN, IgA).

22. Summary

Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Aetiology: GAS infection (pharyngitis/impetigo) → immune complexes (subepithelial) → nephritic syndrome after 1–3 wks (pharyngitis) or 3–6 wks (impetigo).

Clinical: Cola-coloured urine, periorbital oedema, hypertension, RBC casts. Children 5–15yr.

Labs: Low C3, Normal C4 (alternative pathway), ASO ↑ (peaks 3–5 wks), Anti-DNase B for impetigo. EM: Subepithelial humps.

C3 normalises by 8 weeks — persistent low C3 >12 wks = C3G/MPGN/SLE/IE.

Management: Supportive (BP control, diuretics, Penicillin V × 10 days for carriage). NO steroids in typical childhood PSGN.

Prognosis: Children 95% recovery; Adults 10–20% ESRD (worse if crescents >50%, nephrotic, severe AKI).

Biopsy: Not routine in classic childhood; indicated if adult >40, atypical, persistent low C3, nephrotic.

Exam focus: Latency, complement profile, ASO/anti-DNase B, humps EM, supportive Rx, C3 timeline, child vs adult prognosis, biopsy indications.

topics.title: Rapidly Progressive Gn Rpgn
topics.html:
Topic: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome)]], [[MPGN and C3 Glomerulopathy (DDD, C3GN)]], [[Cryoglobulinaemic Glomerulonephritis]], [[ANCA-Associated Vasculitis (GPA, MPA, EGPA)]], [[Lupus Nephritis]], [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
RPGN = clinical syndrome of rapid loss of renal function (days–weeks) with glomerular disease. Pathology: Crescentic GN (>50% crescents). Three aetiological categories: Type I (Anti-GBM), Type II (Immune-complex), Type III (Pauci-immune/ANCA). Emergency: treat based on serology (Anti-GBM, ANCA, ANA, Cryo, Complement). Pulse steroids + cyclophosphamide/rituximab ± plasma exchange.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define RPGN (rapid AKI + active sediment + crescents)
  • Classify by aetiology (Anti-GBM, Immune-complex, Pauci-immune)
  • Recognise clinical presentations and serological patterns
  • Apply urgent diagnostic workup (serology, biopsy)
  • Manage emergently based on category (pulse steroids + CyC/rituximab ± PLEX)
  • Differentiate from other causes of rapid AKI
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP emergency nephrology vignettes

2. Definition & Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetail
DefinitionRapid loss of GFR (50% decline in days–weeks) + active urinary sediment (haematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria)
PathologyCrescentic GN — cellular crescents in >50% glomeruli
TimecourseDays to weeks (vs CKD = months–years)
PresentationAKI, haematuria (microscopic/macroscopic), proteinuria (subnephrotic), hypertension, oedema, ± pulmonary haemorrhage

3. Aetiological Classification (Crucial for Management)

CategoryMechanismSerologyExamples
Type I: Anti-GBMAnti-α3(IV)NC1 collagen IgGAnti-GBM+, ANCA−Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture's if pulmonary)
Type II: Immune-ComplexImmune complex depositionLow C3/C4, ANA/dsDNA+, Cryo+, ANCA−Lupus nephritis (III/IV), Post-strep, Cryoglobulinaemia, IE, IgA (rare), MPGN
Type III: Pauci-immuneANCA-mediated (neutrophil activation)ANCA+ (p-ANCA/MPO or c-ANCA/PR3), C3/C4 normalGPA, MPA, EGPA, Renal-limited vasculitis
Double-positive (Anti-GBM + ANCA) = 20–30% of Type I — treat as both.

4. Serological Workup (Order STAT)

TestType IType IIType III
Anti-GBMPositiveNegativeNegative
ANCA (MPO/PR3)Negative (± double-positive)NegativePositive
C3, C4Normal/Low C3Low C3 ± Low C4Normal
ANA, dsDNANegativePositive (SLE)Negative
CryoglobulinsNegativePositive (Type II/III)Negative
Hepatitis B/C, HIVNegativePositive (HCV, HBV)Negative

5. Clinical Differentiation

FeatureAnti-GBM (Type I)Immune-Complex (Type II)Pauci-immune/ANCA (Type III)
AgeBimodal (20–30, 60–70)Variable (SLE: young ♀; Post-strep: children)Older (>50)
Pulmonary haemorrhageCommon (40–60%)Rare (SLE, cryo)Common in GPA/MPA
Anti-GBMPositiveNegativeNegative
ANCANegative*NegativePositive
ComplementNormalLow C3/C4Normal
Biopsy IFLinear IgGGranular ("full-house" SLE)Pauci-immune (scant)
Double-positive20–30% ANCA+—20–30% Anti-GBM+
Always check both Anti-GBM and ANCA in RPGN — double-positive changes management.

6. Emergency Management (Start IMMEDIATELY)

General Principles

  • Do NOT wait for biopsy if clinical + serology clear
  • Pulse IV Methylprednisolone 500–1000mg × 3 days → Oral Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day
  • Cyclophosphamide (IV 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo OR oral 2mg/kg/day × 3mo)
  • Plasma Exchange (PLEX) based on category

Category-Specific Regimens

Type I: Anti-GBM

ComponentRegimen
PLEXDaily × 14 days (or until anti-GBM negative) — MANDATORY
CyclophosphamideIV 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo
SteroidsPulse → Oral taper 3–6mo
RituximabAlternative to CyC (especially young women — fertility)
Anti-GBM: PLEX = life-saving (removes antibody). Dialysis-dependent at presentation → <10% renal recovery.

Type II: Immune-Complex (SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE)

AetiologyRegimen
SLE (Class III/IV)Pulse steroids → MMF 2–3g/day OR IV CyC 500mg/m² q2wk + steroids
CryoglobulinaemiaPLEX + Rituximab + Steroids (if severe/RPGN)
Post-strepUsually self-limited; supportive; steroids if crescentic >50%
Infective EndocarditisAntibiotics (surgery if needed); immunosuppression contraindicated
IgA NephropathyRarely RPGN; if so: steroids ± CyC
Identify and treat infection first (IE, post-strep) — immunosuppression can worsen.

Type III: Pauci-immune (ANCA Vasculitis — GPA, MPA, EGPA)

ComponentRegimen
PLEXIf Cr >500 μmol/L or pulmonary haemorrhage (PEXIVAS trial: PLEX not routine for all)
InductionRituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4 (preferred) OR IV CyC 500mg/m² q2wk × 3mo
SteroidsPulse → Oral taper (RAVE/PEXIVAS: lower cumulative steroid better)
MaintenanceRituximab q6mo OR Azathioprine/MMf (MAINRITSAN: rituximab superior)
ANCA RPGN: Rituximab non-inferior to CyC (RAVE); preferred for relapsing/young women.

7. PLEX Indications Summary

CategoryPLEX Indication
Anti-GBMALWAYS (daily × 14d)
ANCA (Type III)Cr >500 (5.7 mg/dL) OR Pulmonary haemorrhage
CryoglobulinaemiaRPGN, alveolar haemorrhage, severe neuropathy
SLENot routine (consider if TMA, catastrophic APS)
TMA/HUSDaily (see TMA topic)

8. Prognosis by Category

CategoryRenal Recovery (if treated early)Relapse Risk
Anti-GBMCr <300: 70–80%; Dialysis-dependent: <10%Rare (<5%) once Ab cleared
SLEGood with early tx (MMF/CyC)Moderate (30–40%)
ANCACr <300: 70–80%High (30–50%) — maintenance needed
CryoglobulinaemiaVariable (HCV cure improves)High if HCV persists

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- RPGN = rapid AKI + active sediment + crescents >50%.
- Three categories: Anti-GBM (Type I), Immune-complex (Type II), Pauci-immune/ANCA (Type III).
- Emergency workup: Anti-GBM, ANCA (MPO/PR3), ANA/dsDNA, C3/C4, Cryoglobulins, Hepatitis.
- Anti-GBM: Linear IgG, pulmonary haemorrhage common, PLEX mandatory × 14d.
- ANCA vasculitis: Rituximab preferred induction (non-inferior to CyC, fertility-sparing).
- Double-positive (Anti-GBM + ANCA): 20–30% — treat as both.
- PLEX: Anti-GBM = always; ANCA = if Cr >500 or pulmonary bleed.
- SLE RPGN: MMF or CyC + steroids (not PLEX routine).
- Prognosis: Dialysis-dependent Anti-GBM → <10% recovery; ANCA high relapse → maintenance.

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
RPGN = always ANCAOnly Type III = ANCA; Type I = Anti-GBM; Type II = Immune-complex
PLEX for all RPGNAnti-GBM = always; ANCA = only if Cr >500 or pulmonary bleed
Rituximab for allAnti-GBM: CyC standard (rituximab alternative); ANCA: rituximab preferred; SLE: MMF/CyC
Crescents = RPGN>50% crescents = RPGN; <50% = crescentic GN but not RPGN clinically
Anti-GBM = no ANCA20–30% double-positive — worse prognosis, ↑ relapse
Wait for biopsy before treatmentNO — treat emergently on serology; biopsy confirms
SLE RPGN = PLEXPLEX not routine for SLE; MMF/CyC + steroids first
All pulmonary haemorrhage = Goodpasture'sAlso ANCA vasculitis, SLE, cryoglobulinaemia, catastrophic APS
ANCA typesp-ANCA/MPO = MPA/EGPA/renal-limited; c-ANCA/PR3 = GPA
Post-strep = RPGN rarePost-strep usually self-limited; RPGN rare, steroids if crescents >50%

11. Mnemonics

  • RPGN Types: I = Immune Anti-GBM; II = Immune Complex; III = Pauci-immune ANCA = ICAP
  • Anti-GBM: Linear IgG + Pulmonary = LIP
  • ANCA: PR3 = PR3 GPA; MPO = MPA/EGPA
  • PLEX: Anti-GBM = Always; ANCA = Always if Cr>500/Pulmonary
  • Double-positive: Anti-GBM + ANCA = Awful (worse prognosis)

12. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((RPGN))

Definition[Rapid AKI + Active sediment + Crescents >50%]

Type_I[Anti-GBM (Type I)]

Serology[Anti-GBM+]

IF[Linear IgG]

Pulmonary[Common 40-60%]

Rx[PLEX x14d + CyC + Steroids]

Prognosis[Dialysis-dep <10% recovery]

Type_II[Immune-Complex (Type II)]

Causes[SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE, IgA]

Serology[Low C3/C4, ANA+, Cryo+]

IF[Granular (Full-house SLE)]

Rx[Treat cause: SLE=MMF/CyC; Cryo=PLEX+Rituximab; IE=Abx]

Type_III[Pauci-immune/ANCA (Type III)]

Causes[GPA, MPA, EGPA, Renal-limited]

Serology[ANCA+ (PR3/MPO)]

IF[Pauci-immune]

Rx[Rituximab preferred + Steroids; PLEX if Cr>500/Pulm bleed]

Relapse[High - maintenance Rituximab/AZA]

Double_Positive[Anti-GBM + ANCA (20-30%)]

Rx[Both protocols]

Prognosis[Worse, high relapse]

```

13. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. RPGN definition (rapid AKI + crescents >50%)
  2. Three aetiological types (Anti-GBM, Immune-complex, Pauci-immune)
  3. Key serology for each (Anti-GBM, ANCA, C3/C4/ANA/Cryo)
  4. Anti-GBM: Linear IgG, PLEX mandatory, pulmonary bleed
  5. ANCA: Rituximab preferred, PLEX if Cr>500/pulmonary
  6. Double-positive (20–30%)
  7. SLE RPGN: MMF/CyC (not PLEX routine)
  8. Prognosis by category (dialysis-dep Anti-GBM <10%, ANCA high relapse)

14. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴RPGN definition, 3 types, serology workup, Anti-GBM PLEX mandatory, ANCA rituximab preferred, PLEX indications, double-positive
Should Know 🟡SLE RPGN management (MMF/CyC), cryoglobulinaemia RPGN, post-strep RPGN, PEXIVAS/RAVE/MAINRITSAN trials, prognosis by category
Nice to Know 🟢Novel ANCA therapies (avacopan, narsoplimab), anti-GBM antigen-specific therapy, complement inhibition in IC-RPGN, biomarkers for relapse prediction

16. MCQs (10)

  1. RPGN histopathological hallmark:

A. Crescents in >50% glomeruli

B. Wire-loop lesions

C. Spike and dome

D. Tram-tracking

E. Foot process effacement

  1. Anti-GBM disease — immunofluorescence pattern:

A. Granular IgG + C3

B. Linear IgG along GBM

C. Mesangial IgA

D. Full-house

E. C3 dominant

  1. ANCA vasculitis — preferred induction agent (RAVE trial):

A. Cyclophosphamide

B. Rituximab

C. Mycophenolate

C. Azathioprine

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Plasma exchange in ANCA RPGN — indication (PEXIVAS):

A. All cases

B. Serum creatinine >500 μmol/L OR pulmonary haemorrhage

C. Only pulmonary haemorrhage

D. Creatinine >300

E. Never indicated

  1. Double-positive Anti-GBM + ANCA prevalence:

A. <5%

B. 20–30%

C. 50%

D. 70%

E. 90%

  1. Anti-GBM RPGN — dialysis-dependent at presentation, renal recovery:

A. 50%

B. 30%

C. <10%

D. 80%

E. 100%

  1. SLE Class III/IV RPGN — first-line induction:

A. Plasma exchange

B. MMF 2–3g/day OR IV Cyclophosphamide + Steroids

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange + Rituximab

E. Azathioprine

  1. ANCA type in Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA):

A. c-ANCA/PR3

B. p-ANCA/MPO

C. Both equally

D. Atypical ANCA

E. Anti-GBM

  1. Maintenance therapy post-ANCA induction (MAINRITSAN):

A. Azathioprine

B. Rituximab q6mo (superior to AZA)

C. MMF

D. Low-dose cyclophosphamide

E. Steroids alone

  1. Anti-GBM disease — PLEX duration:

A. 7 days

B. 14 days (or until anti-GBM negative)

C. 21 days

D. 28 days

E. 3 days

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, AKI (Cr 450), haematuria, RBC casts, pulmonary haemorrhage. Anti-GBM+, ANCA−. Immediate management:

A. IV Methylprednisolone only

B. PLEX daily × 14d + IV Cyclophosphamide + IV Methylprednisolone

C. Rituximab + steroids

D. Plasma exchange × 7 days

E. Supportive only

  1. 65-year-old woman, AKI (Cr 620), haematuria, proteinuria, no pulmonary symptoms. p-ANCA/MPO+, Anti-GBM−, C3/C4 normal. PLEX indicated?

A. Yes (Cr >500)

B. Yes (Cr >500 — PEXIVAS criterion)

C. No (only if pulmonary bleed)

D. No (PLEX never in ANCA)

E. Only if dialysis-dependent

  1. 25-year-old woman, SLE, AKI (Cr 380), active sediment, biopsy: Class IV lupus nephritis with crescents. Management:

A. PLEX + Rituximab

B. MMF 3g/day + Steroids (or IV CyC + steroids)

C. Rituximab alone

D. Plasma exchange

E. Cyclophosphamide alone

  1. 30-year-old man, GPA (c-ANCA/PR3+), RPGN (Cr 550), no pulmonary bleed. Biopsy: pauci-immune crescentic GN. Induction:

A. Cyclophosphamide only

B. Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4 + Steroids

C. MMF + steroids

D. PLEX + Cyclophosphamide

E. Azathioprine + steroids

  1. Anti-GBM + p-ANCA double-positive patient. Compared to pure Anti-GBM:

A. Better prognosis

B. Worse renal survival, higher relapse risk

C. No difference

D. Lower PLEX requirement

E. No maintenance needed

  1. ANCA vasculitis patient, post-induction (rituximab), remission. Maintenance per MAINRITSAN:

A. Azathioprine 2mg/kg

B. Rituximab 500mg q6mo × 2 years

C. MMF 1g BD

D. Low-dose prednisolone only

E. No maintenance

  1. Patient with RPGN, both Anti-GBM and p-ANCA positive. PLEX duration:

A. 7 days

B. 14 days (Anti-GBM protocol) + ANCA immunosuppression

C. 21 days

D. Until ANCA negative

E. 3 days

  1. ANCA vasculitis with pulmonary haemorrhage. PLEX protocol:

A. Alternate days × 7

B. Daily × 7–14 days (as per Anti-GBM protocol for pulmonary bleed)

C. 3 sessions only

E. Not indicated

  1. RPGN differential — pauci-immune crescentic GN with NO extrarenal features. Most likely:

A. GPA

B. Renal-limited ANCA vasculitis

C. MPA

D. EGPA

E. Anti-GBM

  1. Post-strep GN with crescents >50% (RPGN). Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Steroids (if crescents >50% and progressive)

C. PLEX

D. Cyclophosphamide

E. Rituximab

18. Flashcards

  • Q: RPGN crescent threshold?

A: >50% glomeruli

  • Q: RPGN Type I = ?

A: Anti-GBM (Linear IgG)

  • Q: RPGN Type II = ?

A: Immune-complex (SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE)

  • Q: RPGN Type III = ?

A: Pauci-immune / ANCA (GPA, MPA, EGPA)

  • Q: Anti-GBM PLEX?

A: Daily × 14d (mandatory)

  • Q: ANCA PLEX indication?

A: Cr >500 OR pulmonary haemorrhage

  • Q: ANCA induction preferred?

A: Rituximab

  • Q: ANCA maintenance?

A: Rituximab q6mo (superior to AZA)

  • Q: Double-positive prevalence?

A: 20–30%

  • Q: Anti-GBM dialysis recovery?

A: <10%

  • Q: SLE RPGN Rx?

A: MMF or CyC + steroids (NO routine PLEX)

  • Q: c-ANCA = ?

A: PR3 = GPA

  • Q: p-ANCA = ?

A: MPO = MPA/EGPA

  • Q: Post-strep RPGN Rx?

A: Steroids if progressive crescentic

  • Q: PEXIVAS trial?

A: PLEX not routine for all ANCA; only Cr>500/pulm bleed

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A — Crescentic GN = crescents >50% glomeruli = RPGN histology.
  2. B — Anti-GBM = linear IgG along GBM (pathognomonic).
  3. B — Rituximab non-inferior to CyC (RAVE), preferred for relapsing/fertility.
  4. B — PEXIVAS: PLEX only if Cr >500 μmol/L (5.7 mg/dL) OR pulmonary haemorrhage.
  5. B — 20–30% of Anti-GBM have concurrent ANCA (usually p-ANCA/MPO).
  6. C — Dialysis-dependent Anti-GBM at presentation → <10% renal recovery.
  7. B — SLE RPGN: MMF (preferred) or IV CyC + steroids; PLEX not routine.
  8. B — MPA/EGPA/renal-limited = p-ANCA/MPO; GPA = c-ANCA/PR3.
  9. B — MAINRITSAN: Rituximab 500mg q6mo × 2yr superior to AZA for relapse prevention.
  10. B — Anti-GBM PLEX: daily × 14 days or until antibody negative.

SBAs

  1. B — Anti-GBM + pulmonary bleed = emergency PLEX + CyC + steroids.
  2. B — Cr 620 (>500) = PLEX indicated per PEXIVAS even without pulmonary bleed.
  3. B — SLE Class IV RPGN: MMF 3g/day or IV CyC + steroids; PLEX not standard.
  4. B — Rituximab preferred induction for ANCA vasculitis (RAVE).
  5. B — Double-positive: worse renal survival, higher relapse (ANCA component).
  6. B — MAINRITSAN: Rituximab 500mg q6mo × 2yr = superior maintenance.
  7. B — Double-positive: PLEX per Anti-GBM protocol (14d) + full ANCA immunosuppression.
  8. B — Pulmonary haemorrhage in ANCA = PLEX daily × 7–14d (same as Anti-GBM protocol).
  9. B — Pauci-immune crescentic GN without extrarenal = renal-limited ANCA vasculitis.
  10. B — Post-strep RPGN rare; if progressive crescentic >50% → steroids (not PLEX/CyC).

20. Summary

Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN) is a Must Know 🔴 emergency nephrology topic.

Definition: Rapid AKI (days–weeks) + active sediment + crescents >50%.

Three Categories: Type I = Anti-GBM (linear IgG); Type II = Immune-complex (SLE, Cryo, Post-strep, IE); Type III = Pauci-immune/ANCA (GPA, MPA, EGPA).

Emergency Workup: Anti-GBM, ANCA (MPO/PR3), ANA/dsDNA, C3/C4, Cryoglobulins, Hepatitis.

Management: Anti-GBM: PLEX daily × 14d + CyC + Steroids (mandatory). ANCA: Rituximab preferred + Steroids; PLEX if Cr >500 or pulmonary bleed (PEXIVAS). SLE: MMF/CyC + steroids (PLEX not routine). Cryo: PLEX + Rituximab + Steroids. Double-positive (20–30%): Treat as both.

Prognosis: Dialysis-dependent Anti-GBM <10% recovery; ANCA high relapse → maintenance rituximab.

Exam focus: 3 types, serology, PLEX indications, rituximab vs CyC, double-positive, SLE vs ANCA management, crescent threshold.

topics.title: Iga Nephropathy Bergers Disease
topics.html:
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
IgA Nephropathy = commonest primary GN worldwide. Aberrant IgA1 glycosylation → immune complexes → mesangial deposition. Classic: macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI. Diagnosis: renal biopsy (dominant mesangial IgA on IF). Management: supportive (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i) ± immunosuppression if high-risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise clinical presentations (synpharyngitic haematuria, asymptomatic, nephrotic, RPGN)
  • Interpret biopsy findings (LM, IF, EM) and Oxford MEST-C classification
  • Apply risk stratification and treatment algorithm
  • Differentiate from HSP nephritis, post-strep GN, IgA-dominant infection-related GN

2. Epidemiology & Pathogenesis

FeatureDetail
EpidemiologyCommonest primary GN worldwide; young adults (20–30s); M>F; high prevalence in East Asia
PathogenesisAberrant IgA1 O-glycosylation → galactose-deficient IgA1 → anti-glycan IgG/IgA autoantibodies → immune complexes → mesangial deposition → complement activation (alternative pathway) → inflammation
GeneticsGWAS: HLA region, complement regulators (CFH, C3), mucosal immunity loci; familial clustering
AssociationsCeliac disease, liver disease, IBD, HIV, Hank's disease

3. Clinical Presentation

TypeFeatures
Classic (Synpharyngitic)Episodic macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI (often with loin pain); self-limiting, recurs
AsymptomaticMicroscopic haematuria ± proteinuria (incidental on screening); most common presentation
Nephrotic Syndrome<5%; heavy proteinuria, oedema, hypoalbuminaemia, hyperlipidaemia
Rapidly Progressive GN (RPGN)Rare; crescentic transformation (>50% crescents); acute kidney injury
Chronic ProgressiveGradual eGFR decline, hypertension, proteinuria

4. Histopathology

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyMesangial hypercellularity, matrix expansion; segmental/global sclerosis in chronic; crescents if RPGN
ImmunofluorescenceDominant mesangial IgA (+ C3, IgG, IgM); no IgG dominance (distinguishes from infection-related)
Electron MicroscopyMesangial electron-dense deposits; no subepithelial/subendothelial deposits (if present = overlap)

5. Oxford MEST-C Classification (Prognostic)

LesionScoreDefinitionPrognostic Significance
M (Mesangial hypercellularity)M0/M1M0: <50% glomeruli; M1: ≥50%M1 → worse renal survival
E (Endocapillary hypercellularity)E0/E1E1: hypercellularity in any glomerulusE1 → worse prognosis; responds to immunosuppression
S (Segmental glomerulosclerosis)S0/S1S1: adhesion/sclerosis in any glomerulusS1 → worse renal survival
T (Tubular atrophy/Interstitial fibrosis)T0/T1/T2T0: <25%; T1: 25–50%; T2: >50%Strongest predictor of progression
C (Crescents)C0/C1/C2C0: none; C1: <25%; C2: ≥25%C1/C2 → worse outcome; indicates immunosuppression
[!key]
Oxford MEST-C: M1, E1, S1, T1/2, C1/2 = adverse features. T score is strongest predictor of progression.

6. Risk Stratification & Management Algorithm

Risk CategoryCriteriaManagement
Low RiskProteinuria <0.5g/day, eGFR >60, normal BP, no MEST-C adverse featuresSupportive only: ACEi/ARB (max tolerated), SGLT2i, BP <130/80, statin, weight loss, smoking cessation
Intermediate RiskProteinuria 0.5–1g/day, or eGFR 45–60, or isolated MEST-C E1/S1Supportive + consider 6mo steroids if persistent proteinuria >0.75g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB (TESTING trial)
High RiskProteinuria >1g/day, or eGFR <45, or crescents (C1/C2), or T1/T2Immunosuppression: 6mo steroids ± cyclophosphamide (if crescentic); consider MMF/rituximab
RPGN>50% crescents, rapidly rising CrPulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3d → oral pred 1mg/kg + cyclophosphamide 0.5g/m² IV monthly × 6mo (or rituximab)

7. Supportive Care (All Patients)

InterventionTarget / Dose
ACEi/ARBMax tolerated dose; target BP <130/80; monitor K+/Cr
SGLT2 InhibitorDapagliflozin/empagliflozin (if eGFR ≥20); reduces progression by 35–40%
Blood Pressure<130/80 (KDIGO 2021)
LipidsStatin if >50yo or CVD risk
DietLow salt (<2g Na/day), moderate protein (0.8g/kg)
Weight/SmokingBMI <25, smoking cessation
MonitoringUPCR, eGFR, BP every 3–6mo

8. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Feature
Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) NephritisSystemic vasculitis (palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis); same pathology (IgA) but systemic
Post-Streptococcal GNASO titre ↑, C3 low, subepithelial "humps" on EM; follows strep by 1–3 weeks
IgA-Dominant Infection-Related GNStaph infection, IgG co-dominance on IF, older patients, systemic infection
C3 GlomerulopathyC3 dominant on IF, IgA absent/minimal; alternative pathway dysregulation
Lupus Nephritis Class IIFull-house IF (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q), positive ANA/anti-dsDNA

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Commonest primary GN worldwide → IgA Nephropathy
- Classic: Macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI (synpharyngitic)
- Biopsy: Mesangial IgA dominant on IF; mesangial deposits on EM
- Oxford MEST-C: T score = strongest predictor of progression
- Supportive: ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP control (KDIGO 2021)
- Immunosuppression: Persistent proteinuria >1g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB → 6mo steroids (STOP-IgAN, TESTING trials)
- RPGN with crescents: Pulse steroids + cyclophosphamide/rituximab
- HSP vs IgA: HSP = systemic (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis); same renal pathology

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
IgA = always benign30–40% progress to ESRD at 20 years
All need immunosuppressionOnly high-risk (>1g proteinuria, crescents, declining eGFR); low-risk = supportive
Post-strep = IgA dominantPost-strep = IgG dominant, C3 low, subepithelial humps
HSP = different diseaseHSP nephritis = systemic IgA vasculitis; same renal pathology
Steroids always harmful6mo steroids reduce ESRD risk in high-risk (TESTING trial; lower dose safer)
MEST-C all equalT score (tubular atrophy/fibrosis) is strongest predictor

11. Mnemonics

  • IgA classic: Synpharyngitic Haematuria post-URTI = SHU
  • Oxford MEST-C: Mesangial, Endocapillary, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy, Crescents = MEST-C
  • Biopsy: Mesangial IgA Deposits = MID
  • Risk: Proteinuria >1g, EGFR <45, Crescents, Tubular atrophy = PECT

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest primary GN worldwide?

A: IgA Nephropathy

  • Q: IgA classic presentation?

A: Macroscopic haematuria 1–2 days post-URTI (synpharyngitic)

  • Q: IF hallmark of IgA nephropathy?

A: Dominant mesangial IgA (+C3), no IgG dominance

  • Q: EM finding in IgA?

A: Mesangial electron-dense deposits

  • Q: Oxford MEST-C components?

A: Mesangial, Endocapillary, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy/fibrosis, Crescents

  • Q: Oxford MEST-C strongest predictor?

A: T score (Tubular atrophy/Interstitial fibrosis)

  • Q: Supportive Rx for all IgA?

A: ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP <130/80 + statin + lifestyle

  • Q: When to immunosupress?

A: Proteinuria >1g/day despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB, or crescents, or eGFR <45

  • Q: RPGN IgA management?

A: Pulse methylpred + CYC/rituximab

  • Q: HSP vs IgA?

A: HSP = systemic (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis); same renal pathology

  • Q: Post-strep vs IgA?

A: Post-strep = ASO↑, C3 low, subepithelial humps, IgG dominant

  • Q: IgA-dominant infection-related GN?

A: Staph infection, older, IgG co-dominance, systemic infection

  • Q: Complement pathway in IgA?

A: Alternative pathway

  • Q: Oxford E1 significance?

A: Endocapillary hypercellularity; responds to immunosuppression

  • Q: TESTING trial result?

A: 6mo steroids reduce ESRD but increase infections; lower dose safer

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — IgA nephropathy is commonest primary GN globally
  2. B — Classic = synpharyngitic macroscopic haematuria 1–2d post-URTI
  3. B — Dominant mesangial IgA on IF (pathognomonic)
  4. D — T score (tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis) = strongest predictor
  5. B — Supportive: ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP control is first-line for all
  6. B — KDIGO: immunosuppression if proteinuria >1g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB
  7. B — TESTING: 6mo methylpred reduced ESRD but increased adverse events (infections)
  8. B — HSP = systemic IgA vasculitis (purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis)
  9. A — Post-strep: ASO↑, C3↓, subepithelial "humps", IgG dominant
  10. B — E = endocapillary hypercellularity
  11. B — Crescentic RPGN: pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab

SBAs

  1. B — Young adult + post-URTI haematuria + mesangial IgA = IgA nephropathy
  2. C — Proteinuria 0.8g, eGFR 75, M1 only → low/intermediate risk → supportive
  3. B — Proteinuria >1g, eGFR 45, adverse MEST-C (E1, S1, T1, C1) → immunosuppression
  4. B — HSP = systemic vasculitis (palpable purpura, abdominal pain, arthritis) + IgA nephritis
  5. B — Infection-related GN: IgA/IgG co-dominance, subepithelial deposits, active infection
  6. D — T2 = >50% tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis (worst prognosis)
  7. C — Alternative pathway (C3 deposition, no C1q/C4)
  8. B — KDIGO 2024: BP <130/80 for proteinuric CKD
  9. B — DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEY: SGLT2i reduces progression ~35–40% in CKD incl. IgA (non-diabetic)
  10. C — RPGN with >50% crescents: pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab

20. Summary

IgA Nephropathy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest primary GN. Classic = synpharyngitic macroscopic haematuria. Biopsy = dominant mesangial IgA on IF, mesangial deposits on EM. Oxford MEST-C for prognosis (T score strongest). Supportive = ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i + BP <130/80. Immunosuppression = proteinuria >1g despite 3–6mo ACEi/ARB → 6mo steroids (TESTING). RPGN = pulse steroids + CYC/rituximab. HSP = systemic vasculitis + same renal pathology.

Exam focus: Clinical presentations, biopsy interpretation, Oxford MEST-C, supportive vs immunosuppressive thresholds, HSP distinction, TESTING trial.

Clinical relevance: Most common GN; early recognition + ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i prevents progression.

topics.title: Membranous Nephropathy
topics.html:
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Membranous Nephropathy = commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults (non-diabetic). Subepithelial immune complexes → complement-mediated podocyte injury. Primary (anti-PLA2R/THSD7A) vs Secondary (SLE, malignancy, infections, drugs). Spontaneous remission ~30%. Management: supportive (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i) + immunosuppression if high-risk.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Distinguish primary vs secondary membranous nephropathy
  • Interpret anti-PLA2R/THSD7A serology and biopsy stages
  • Apply KDIGO risk stratification (low/medium/high) for immunosuppression
  • Manage nephrotic complications (thrombosis, infection, lipids)

2. Epidemiology & Classification

FeatureDetail
EpidemiologyCommonest cause of adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic); peak 50–60yo; M>F
Primary (Idiopathic)~70–80%; anti-PLA2R+ (70–80%), anti-THSD7A+ (2–5%), double-negative (15–20%)
Secondary~20–30%; SLE (Class V), malignancy (solid tumours), infections (HBV, HCV, syphilis), drugs (NSAIDs, gold, penicillamine), sarcoidosis

3. Pathogenesis

MechanismDetail
PrimaryPodocyte antigens: PLA2R (phospholipase A2 receptor), THSD7A (thrombospondin type-1 domain-containing 7A) → autoantibodies (IgG4 dominant) → in situ immune complex formation in subepithelial space
SecondaryExogenous antigens (malignancy, drugs, infections) or immune dysregulation (SLE)
ComplementMembrane attack complex (C5b-9) → podocyte injury → proteinuria
IgG SubclassIgG4 dominant in primary MN (vs IgG1/3 in secondary)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetail
Nephrotic Syndrome~80%: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, hyperlipidaemia
Asymptomatic ProteinuriaIncidental finding; subnephrotic range
Renal Impairment~20–30% at presentation (usually chronic)
Thrombotic ComplicationsRenal vein thrombosis (20–40%), PE, DVT — hypercoagulable state
Extrarenal (Secondary)SLE features, malignancy symptoms, infection signs

5. Histopathology (Biopsy)

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyDiffuse GBM thickening (spikes on Jones/PAS); no hypercellularity; advanced = sclerosis
ImmunofluorescenceGranular capillary wall deposits: IgG (dominant), C3; IgG4 predominant in primary
Electron MicroscopySubepithelial electron-dense deposits (subepithelial "humps"); corresponding GBM spikes; podocyte foot process effacement

6. Staging (Ehrenreich-Churg)

StageEM FindingsPrognosis
Stage ISmall, sparse subepithelial deposits; no spikesBest
Stage IILarger, confluent deposits; early spikesGood
Stage IIIDeposits incorporated into GBM; prominent spikesIntermediate
Stage IVAdvanced GBM thickening, sclerosis, few depositsWorst (chronic)
[!key]
Stage I–II better response to immunosuppression; Stage III–IV more chronic, less responsive.

7. Serology & Biomarkers

MarkerSignificance
Anti-PLA2R AbHighly specific (90–95%) for primary MN; titre correlates with disease activity; serial monitoring guides therapy
Anti-THSD7A Ab~2–5% primary MN; associated with malignancy
Complement (C3, C4)Usually normal (vs low in SLE, post-infectious, C3G)
ANA, anti-dsDNARule out SLE (Class V lupus)
Malignancy screenCT chest/abdomen/pelvis if >60yo, anti-THSD7A+, refractory

8. KDIGO Risk Stratification & Management

RiskCriteria5-yr ESRD RiskManagement
LowPersistent proteinuria <4g/day, normal eGFR<5%Supportive only: ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, statin, anticoagulation if indicated, monitor q3–6mo
MediumProteinuria 4–8g/day OR eGFR <605–15%Consider immunosuppression if persistent >6mo supportive; shared decision-making
HighProteinuria >8g/day OR eGFR <40>15%Immunosuppression recommended (see below)

9. Immunosuppression Regimens (Primary MN)

RegimenInductionMaintenanceNotes
Rituximab (Preferred)375mg/m² weekly × 4 (or 1g × 2 doses 2wks apart)Repeat if relapse (B-cell driven); monitor CD19+MENTOR trial: non-inferior to CYC, safer; first-line per KDIGO 2024
Cyclophosphamide + Steroids (Ponticelli)CYC 0.5–1g/m² IV monthly × 6mo (alt monthly oral) + pred 0.5mg/kg alt daysNone (6mo total)Classic regimen; effective but higher toxicity (gonadal, bladder, infection)
CNIs (Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine) + SteroidsTac 0.05–0.1mg/kg/day + predTaper to lowest effective doseHigh relapse rate after stopping; nephrotoxicity risk
Anti-PLA2R-guidedTreat to anti-PLA2R negativityMonitor titres q3mo; retreat if risingPersonalised approach; correlate with proteinuria

10. Supportive Care (All Patients)

InterventionTarget
ACEi/ARBMax tolerated; target proteinuria reduction >50%
SGLT2 InhibitorDapagliflozin/empagliflozin (eGFR ≥20); reduces progression
BP Control<130/80
StatinsAll nephrotic adults (CVD risk)
AnticoagulationProphylactic if serum albumin <20g/L + additional risk factors (immobility, surgery, prior VTE); Therapeutic if VTE/renal vein thrombosis
Infection ProphylaxisPneumococcal, influenza, COVID vaccines; consider varicella if immunosuppressed
DietLow salt, moderate protein

11. Secondary MN — Key Associations

CauseKey Features
SLE (Class V)High ANA/dsDNA, low C3/C4, full-house IF (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q); often mixed Class III/IV + V
MalignancySolid tumours (lung, colon, breast, prostate); older age; anti-THSD7A+; remission with cancer treatment
InfectionsHBV (children, HBeAg+), HCV, syphilis, malaria; treat infection → remission
DrugsNSAIDs, gold, penicillamine, captopril, tiopronin; stop drug → remission
SarcoidosisGranulomatous disease; ACE elevated

12. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Commonest cause adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic) → Membranous Nephropathy
- Primary: anti-PLA2R+ (70–80%), anti-THSD7A+ (2–5%)
- Biopsy: Diffuse GBM thickening + spikes (Jones); subepithelial deposits on EM; IgG4 dominant on IF
- Staging: Ehrenreich-Churg I–IV; early stages = better immunosuppression response
- KDIGO Risk: Low (<4g protein, normal eGFR) → supportive; High (>8g or eGFR <40) → immunosuppress
- First-line immunosuppression: Rituximab (MENTOR trial) — safer than CYC
- Ponticelli: CYC + steroids (alternating monthly × 6mo) — classic but toxic
- Secondary MN screen: ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, hepatitis serology, malignancy screen (if >60)
- Renal vein thrombosis: 20–40% risk; prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20g/L + risk factors
- Spontaneous remission: ~30% (esp. low-risk)

13. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All MN = primary20–30% secondary; always screen (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy if >60)
Anti-PLA2R = 100% specific90–95% specific; false + in secondary (rare)
Subepithelial deposits = only MNAlso post-strep (humps), lupus Class V, infection-related GN
IgG4 dominant = primaryIgG4 dominant in primary; IgG1/3 in secondary (e.g., SLE)
Rituximab = only for relapseNow first-line induction per KDIGO 2024 (MENTOR trial)
All need immunosuppressionLow-risk: supportive only; spontaneous remission 30%
Anticoagulation for everyoneOnly if albumin <20g/L + risk factors (or therapeutic if VTE)
SLE Class V = primary MNSLE = full-house IF, low complement, positive ANA/dsDNA

14. Mnemonics

  • MN = adult nephrotic: Membranous = Most common Adult Nephrotic
  • PLA2R: Phospholipase A2 Receptor = primary MN marker
  • Biopsy: Subepithelial Deposits, Spikes, Start EM = SDS
  • Staging: I = best, IV = worst = 1-2-3-4
  • Risk: Low <4g, Medium 4–8g, High >8g = LMH
  • Rituximab: Rituximab First-line (MENTOR) = RF
  • Secondary screen: ANA, Hepatitis, Malignancy (if >60), Drugs = AHMD

15. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Membranous Nephropathy))

Classification

Primary[Anti-PLA2R/THSD7A]

Secondary[SLE, Cancer, HBV/HCV, Drugs]

Pathogenesis

Podocyte_Antigens[PLA2R, THSD7A]

IgG4_Autoantibodies[In situ immune complexes]

Subepithelial_Deposits[Complement C5b-9]

Podocyte_Injury[Proteinuria]

Biopsy

LM[Diffuse GBM thickening, spikes]

IF[Granular capillary wall, IgG4 dominant]

EM[Subepithelial deposits]

Staging[Ehrenreich-Churg I-IV]

Serology

Anti_PLA2R[Specific, monitors activity]

Anti_THSD7A[Malignancy association]

Management

Supportive[ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, Anticoagulation]

Low_Risk[Conservative]

Immunosuppression[Rituximab (1st line), CYC+steroids, CNI]

```

16. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest cause adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic)
  2. Primary MN autoantibodies (PLA2R, THSD7A)
  3. Biopsy: LM (spikes), IF (IgG4), EM (subepithelial)
  4. KDIGO risk stratification (Low/Medium/High thresholds)
  5. First-line immunosuppression (Rituximab per MENTOR)
  6. Secondary MN screen (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy)
  7. Renal vein thrombosis prophylaxis (albumin <20)
  8. Spontaneous remission rate (~30%)

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
