18. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Clinical presentation, primary vs secondary, anti-PLA2R, biopsy findings, KDIGO risk stratification, Rituximab first-line, renal vein thrombosis prophylaxis
Should Know 🟡Ehrenreich-Churg staging, Ponticelli regimen, CNI regimen, anti-PLA2R-guided therapy, spontaneous remission rate
Nice to Know 🟢THSD7A-malignancy link, IgG subclass significance, complement inhibition trials, novel biomarkers

19. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic adults:

A. Minimal change disease

B. FSGS

C. Membranous nephropathy

D. IgA nephropathy

E. Amyloidosis

  1. Autoantibody most specific for primary membranous nephropathy:

A. Anti-GBM

B. ANCA

C. Anti-PLA2R

D. Anti-dsDNA

E. Cryoglobulins

  1. Immunofluorescence pattern in primary membranous nephropathy:

A. Linear GBM IgG

B. Granular capillary wall IgG (IgG4 dominant)

C. Mesangial IgA

D. Full-house (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)

E. C3 dominant

  1. Electron microscopy hallmark of membranous nephropathy:

A. Subendothelial deposits

B. Mesangial deposits

C. Subepithelial deposits

D. Intramembranous deposits

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. Ehrenreich-Churg Stage I vs Stage IV:

A. Stage I: diffuse GBM thickening; Stage IV: normal GBM

B. Stage I: small deposits, no spikes; Stage IV: advanced sclerosis, few deposits

C. Stage I: subendothelial; Stage IV: subepithelial

D. Stage I: worst prognosis; Stage IV: best

E. No difference

  1. KDIGO 2024 "High Risk" membranous nephropathy criteria:

A. Proteinuria <4g/day, eGFR >60

B. Proteinuria 4–8g/day OR eGFR <60

C. Proteinuria >8g/day OR eGFR <40

D. Any proteinuria with normal eGFR

E. Only anti-PLA2R titre >100

  1. First-line immunosuppression for primary MN per KDIGO 2024 (MENTOR trial):

A. Cyclophosphamide + steroids

B. Rituximab

C. Tacrolimus + steroids

D. Mycophenolate mofetil

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Ponticelli regimen for MN:

A. Rituximab 375mg/m² × 4 weekly

B. Cyclophosphamide + prednisone alternating monthly × 6mo

C. Tacrolimus 0.1mg/kg/day

D. Plasma exchange weekly × 5

E. Mycophenolate 2g/day × 12mo

  1. Prophylactic anticoagulation in MN indicated when:

A. All nephrotic patients

B. Serum albumin <20g/L + additional risk factors

C. Proteinuria >8g/day only

D. Anti-PLA2R positive only

E. Never indicated

  1. Secondary MN screen should include:

A. Anti-PLA2R only

B. ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, hepatitis B/C, malignancy screen (if >60)

C. ANCA only

D. Cryoglobulins only

E. No screen needed

20. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria 6g/day, albumin 18g/L). Biopsy: diffuse GBM thickening, spikes, subepithelial deposits, granular IgG4 on IF. Anti-PLA2R+. Management:

A. Supportive only

B. Rituximab 375mg/m² weekly × 4

C. Ponticelli regimen

D. Tacrolimus + prednisone

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 62-year-old woman, nephrotic syndrome. Biopsy: membranous pattern. Anti-PLA2R negative. Next step:

A. Diagnose primary MN

B. Screen for secondary causes (ANA, hepatitis, malignancy)

C. Start rituximab

D. Ponticelli regimen

E. Conservative only

  1. 45-year-old man, MN, proteinuria 3g/day, eGFR 70, albumin 30g/L. KDIGO risk category:

A. High

B. Medium

C. Low

D. Not classifiable

E. Requires biopsy restaging

  1. Membranous nephropathy with mixed Class III/IV + V on biopsy. Serology: ANA+, anti-dsDNA+, low C3/C4. Diagnosis:

A. Primary MN

B. Lupus nephritis Class V (mixed)

C. Anti-PLA2R negative primary MN

D. Drug-induced MN

E. HBV-associated MN

  1. MN patient develops flank pain, haematuria, acute kidney injury. Ultrasound: absent renal venous flow. Diagnosis:

A. Acute tubular necrosis

B. Renal vein thrombosis

C. Acute interstitial nephritis

D. Pyelonephritis

E. Renal artery thrombosis

  1. MN, albumin 15g/L, history of DVT. Anticoagulation:

A. Prophylactic only

B. Therapeutic (treatment dose)

C. No anticoagulation

D. Aspirin only

E. IVC filter only

  1. Anti-PLA2R titre monitoring in MN:

A. Not useful

B. Correlates with disease activity; guides immunosuppression tapering

C. Only for diagnosis

D. Replaces proteinuria monitoring

E. Only in secondary MN

  1. Spontaneous remission rate in primary MN:

A. <5%

B. 10–15%

C. ~30% (higher in low-risk)

D. 50%

E. >70%

  1. MN with anti-THSD7A positivity. Association:

A. Better prognosis

B. Malignancy (screen required)

C. SLE

D. HBV

E. Drug-induced

  1. Ponticelli regimen — cyclophosphamide dosing:

A. Daily oral 2mg/kg

B. IV 0.5–1g/m² monthly alternating with prednisone × 6mo

C. IV 1g/m² weekly × 6

D. Oral daily × 12mo

E. IV pulse 500mg × 3 days

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest adult nephrotic cause (non-diabetic)?

A: Membranous Nephropathy

  • Q: Primary MN main autoantibody?

A: Anti-PLA2R (70–80%)

  • Q: Secondary MN main autoantibody?

A: Anti-THSD7A (malignancy association)

  • Q: IF in primary MN?

A: Granular capillary wall IgG (IgG4 dominant)

  • Q: EM in MN?

A: Subepithelial deposits (spikes on LM)

  • Q: Ehrenreich-Churg I vs IV?

A: I = small deposits no spikes (best); IV = advanced sclerosis (worst)

  • Q: KDIGO Low Risk MN?

A: Proteinuria <4g/day, normal eGFR → supportive only

  • Q: KDIGO High Risk MN?

A: Proteinuria >8g/day or eGFR <40 → immunosuppression

  • Q: First-line immunosuppression MN (KDIGO 2024)?

A: Rituximab (MENTOR trial)

  • Q: Ponticelli regimen?

A: CYC + pred alternating monthly × 6mo

  • Q: Renal vein thrombosis prophylaxis?

A: Albumin <20g/L + risk factors

  • Q: Therapeutic anticoagulation in MN?

A: Confirmed VTE or renal vein thrombosis

  • Q: Spontaneous remission MN?

A: ~30% (higher in low-risk)

  • Q: Secondary MN screen?

A: ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, Hep B/C, malignancy (if >60), drugs

  • Q: SLE Class V vs Primary MN?

A: SLE = full-house IF, low C3/C4, ANA/dsDNA+

  • Q: Anti-PLA2R monitoring?

A: Correlates with activity; guides taper/retreatment

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Membranous nephropathy = commonest cause adult nephrotic syndrome (non-diabetic)
  2. C — Anti-PLA2R highly specific (90–95%) for primary MN
  3. B — Granular capillary wall IgG, IgG4 dominant in primary
  4. C — Subepithelial deposits (with corresponding GBM spikes)
  5. B — Stage I: small sparse deposits, no spikes; Stage IV: advanced sclerosis
  6. C — High risk: proteinuria >8g/day OR eGFR <40
  7. B — MENTOR trial: Rituximab non-inferior to CYC, safer; KDIGO 2024 first-line
  8. B — Ponticelli: CYC IV monthly alternating with prednisone × 6mo
  9. B — Prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20g/L + risk factors
  10. B — Screen for secondary: ANA, dsDNA, C3/C4, hepatitis, malignancy (if >60)

SBAs

  1. B — Primary MN, high-risk (proteinuria 6g, albumin 18) → Rituximab first-line
  2. B — PLA2R-negative MN → screen for secondary causes (malignancy, SLE, infections)
  3. C — Proteinuria 3g (<4g), eGFR 70, albumin 30 → Low risk (supportive)
  4. B — Mixed Class III/IV+V + ANA/dsDNA+/low complement = Lupus nephritis
  5. B — Flank pain + haematuria + AKI + absent renal venous flow = Renal vein thrombosis
  6. B — Prior DVT = therapeutic anticoagulation indicated
  7. B — Anti-PLA2R titre correlates with disease activity; guides therapy
  8. C — ~30% spontaneous remission (higher in low-risk)
  9. B — Anti-THSD7A associated with malignancy; screen required
  10. B — Ponticelli: CYC IV 0.5–1g/m² monthly alternating with prednisone × 6mo

23. Summary

Membranous Nephropathy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest adult nephrotic (non-diabetic). Primary = anti-PLA2R+ (70–80%), anti-THSD7A+ (malignancy). Biopsy: GBM thickening + spikes (LM), granular capillary wall IgG4 (IF), subepithelial deposits (EM). KDIGO risk: Low (<4g, normal eGFR) → supportive; High (>8g or eGFR<40) → immunosuppress. Rituximab first-line (MENTOR). Secondary screen: ANA, hepatitis, malignancy (>60). Renal vein thrombosis: 20–40%; prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20 + risk factors.

Exam focus: Primary vs secondary, anti-PLA2R, biopsy findings, KDIGO risk, Rituximab vs Ponticelli, complications.

Clinical relevance: Early rituximab improves remission rates; screening avoids missing secondary causes.

topics.title: Fsgs Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
topics.html:
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS (Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
FSGS = commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults. Segmental sclerosis of some glomeruli. Primary (genetic, circulating permeability factor) vs Secondary (adaptive, viral, drug). Primary: steroid-resistant ~60%. Management: high-dose steroids induction, then CNI/MMF/rituximab. Recurrence post-transplant ~30–50% (circulating factor).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify FSGS variants (Columbia classification) and their prognostic significance
  • Distinguish primary vs secondary FSGS
  • Apply treatment algorithms (steroids, CNI, MMF, rituximab)
  • Recognise post-transplant recurrence risk

2. Classification (Columbia Histological Variants)

VariantHistologyPrognosisNotes
CollapsingSegmental/global collapse + podocyte hyperplasiaWorst (rapid progression to ESRD)HIV-associated, pamidronate, interferon
Tip LesionSclerosis at tubular pole (tip)Best (high steroid response)Often nephrotic
CellularEndocapillary hypercellularity + foam cellsIntermediateMay represent early collapsing
PerihilarSclerosis at vascular pole (hilar)IntermediateOften secondary/adaptive (hyperfiltration)
NOS (Not Otherwise Specified)Segmental sclerosis not fitting aboveIntermediateMost common variant

3. Aetiology

CategoryMechanismExamples
Primary (Idiopathic)Circulating permeability factor (unknown), podocyte injuryGenetic (NPHS2, ACTN4, TRPC6, INF2), idiopathic
Secondary — AdaptiveHyperfiltration, reduced nephron massObesity, reduced renal mass, reflux nephropathy, prematurity
Secondary — ViralDirect podocyte infectionHIV (collapsing variant), Parvovirus B19
Secondary — Drug/ToxinPodocyte toxicityPamidronate, interferon, lithium, sirolimus
Secondary — Familial/GeneticPodocyte structural protein mutationsNPHS2 (podocin, AR, childhood), ACTN4 (α-actinin-4, AD, adult), TRPC6, INF2
[!key]
Primary FSGS: Permeability factor, steroid-sensitive ~40%, recurrence post-Tx 30–50%.
Adaptive FSGS: Subnephrotic proteinuria, non-progressive if underlying cause treated.
Collapsing variant: Worst prognosis; HIV, pamidronate, interferon.

4. Clinical Presentation

FeaturePrimary FSGSAdaptive/Secondary FSGS
ProteinuriaNephrotic range (>3.5g/day) commonSubnephrotic (<3.5g) common
Nephrotic Syndrome~70%Rare
Renal ImpairmentCommon at presentationVariable
HypertensionCommonCommon
HaematuriaMicroscopic ~50%Less common
ExtrarenalNoneObesity, HIV, drug history

5. Histopathology

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopySegmental glomerulosclerosis (adhesion to Bowman's capsule); foam cells; variant-specific features
ImmunofluorescenceNon-specific: IgM, C3 in sclerotic areas ("trapped"); no immune complex pattern
Electron MicroscopyDiffuse foot process effacement (>80%); no electron-dense deposits

6. Genetic Testing Indications

IndicationGenes
Childhood-onset SRNSNPHS2 (podocin, AR), WT1, PLCE1
Adult-onset SRNS + family historyACTN4 (AD), TRPC6 (AD), INF2 (AD)
Pre-transplant workupExclude genetic (no recurrence if genetic)
Syndromic featuresWT1 (Denys-Drash, Frasier), LMX1B (nail-patella)
[!key]
Genetic FSGS = no recurrence post-transplant (no circulating factor). NPHS2 = childhood, AR. ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 = adult, AD.

7. Management

Primary FSGS — Nephrotic Syndrome

PhaseRegimen
InductionPrednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 80mg) × 16 weeks → if remission, taper over 6mo
Steroid-Resistant (no remission at 16 weeks)Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus (CNIs) — target trough CsA 150–250, Tac 5–10 ng/mL + low-dose steroids
CNI-Intolerant/ResistantMycophenolate mofetil 2–3g/day + steroids; Rituximab 375mg/m² × 4 weekly (evidence evolving)
RelapseRestart steroids; CNI/rituximab

Adaptive/Secondary FSGS

ApproachDetail
Treat underlying causeWeight loss (obesity), stop offending drug, treat HIV, control BP
ACEi/ARB + SGLT2iReduce proteinuria, slow progression
ImmunosuppressionNot indicated (no permeability factor)

8. Post-Transplant Recurrence

FeatureDetail
Risk30–50% (primary FSGS); collapsing variant highest
TimingImmediate to days-weeks post-Tx (circulating factor)
PresentationSudden nephrotic proteinuria, allograft dysfunction
ProphylaxisPlasmapheresis preemptive (controversial, not standard)
TreatmentPlasmapheresis (removes factor) + rituximab + CNI optimisation
Genetic FSGSNo recurrence (no circulating factor)

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- FSGS = commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults
- Columbia variants: Collapsing (worst), Tip (best steroid response), Perihilar (adaptive/secondary)
- Primary vs Secondary: Primary = nephrotic, permeability factor; Secondary = adaptive (hyperfiltration), viral, drug
- EM: Diffuse foot process effacement >80% (vs segmental in MCD)
- Induction: Pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks (primary)
- Steroid-resistant: CNI (CsA/Tac) + low-dose steroids
- Post-Tx recurrence: 30–50% (primary); no recurrence if genetic
- Collapsing variant: HIV, pamidronate, interferon; worst prognosis
- Genetic: NPHS2 (childhood, AR), ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 (adult, AD)

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All FSGS = primaryAdaptive (obesity, reduced renal mass) = no immunosuppression
Foot process effacement = MCDMCD = diffuse but selective proteinuria; FSGS = diffuse + non-selective
Steroids work for allPrimary: ~40% respond; Adaptive: NO; Genetic: NO
Recurrence = all FSGS post-TxOnly primary (circulating factor); genetic = no recurrence
Collapsing = only HIVAlso pamidronate, interferon, idiopathic
Perihilar = primaryPerihilar = adaptive/secondary (hyperfiltration)
FSGS = always nephroticAdaptive FSGS often subnephrotic

11. Mnemonics

  • Columbia variants: Collapsing, Tip, Cellular, Perihilar, NOS = CTCPN
  • Prognosis: Collapsing Worst, Tip Best = CW/TB
  • Primary FSGS: Permeability Factor, Steroid Resistant ~60% = PFSR
  • Genetic: NPHS2 = Neonatal/Nephrotic Childhood, AR; ACTN4 = Adult AD
  • Post-Tx: Primary FSGS Recurs (30–50%), Genetic No = PR/GN

12. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((FSGS))

Variants

Collapsing[Worst, HIV, pamidronate]

Tip[Best steroid response]

Cellular[Intermediate]

Perihilar[Adaptive/secondary]

NOS[Most common]

Aetiology

Primary[Permeability factor]

Adaptive[Hyperfiltration]

Viral[HIV, Parvovirus]

Drug[Pamidronate, IFN]

Genetic[NPHS2, ACTN4, TRPC6, INF2]

Histology

LM[Segmental sclerosis, foam cells]

IF[Non-specific IgM, C3 in scars]

EM[Diffuse FPE >80%]

Management

Induction[Pred 1mg/kg 16wk]

Steroid_Resistant[CNI + steroids]

Refractory[MMF, Rituximab]

Adaptive[ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, NO immunosuppression]

Transplant

Recurrence[30-50% primary]

Treatment[PLEX + Rituximab]

Genetic[No recurrence]

```

13. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults
  2. Columbia variants: Collapsing (worst), Tip (best), Perihilar (adaptive)
  3. Primary vs Adaptive FSGS features
  4. EM: Diffuse foot process effacement >80%
  5. Induction: Pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks
  6. Steroid-resistant: CNI + low-dose steroids
  7. Post-Tx recurrence: 30–50% primary; no recurrence if genetic
  8. Genetic: NPHS2 (childhood AR), ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 (adult AD)

14. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Columbia variants, primary vs secondary/adaptive, EM findings, steroid induction, CNI for resistance, post-Tx recurrence, genetic implications
Should Know 🟡Specific genetic genes, collapsing variant associations, rituximab role, plasmapheresis for recurrence
Nice to Know 🟢Circulating permeability factor research, suPAR, novel therapies (sparsentan), APOL1 risk variants

16. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults:

A. IgA nephropathy

B. Membranous nephropathy

C. FSGS

D. Minimal change disease

E. Anti-GBM disease

  1. Columbia variant with BEST steroid response:

A. Collapsing

B. Tip lesion

C. Cellular

D. Perihilar

E. NOS

  1. Columbia variant associated with HIV and pamidronate:

A. Tip lesion

B. Collapsing variant

C. Cellular

D. Perihilar

E. NOS

  1. Adaptive FSGS (secondary) — typical proteinuria:

A. Nephrotic range (>3.5g/day)

B. Subnephrotic (<3.5g/day)

C. Always nephrotic

D. No proteinuria

E. Only microscopic haematuria

  1. EM finding in FSGS:

A. Subepithelial deposits

B. Subendothelial deposits

C. Mesangial deposits

D. Diffuse foot process effacement >80%

E. Tubuloreticular inclusions

  1. Primary FSGS induction therapy (nephrotic):

A. CNI first-line

B. Prednisolone 1mg/kg/day × 16 weeks

C. Rituximab first-line

D. MMF + steroids

E. Plasma exchange

  1. Steroid-resistant FSGS — next line:

A. Increase steroids

B. Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus + low-dose steroids

C. Plasma exchange

D. Rituximab alone

E. Transplant referral

  1. Post-transplant FSGS recurrence risk (primary):

A. <5%

B. 10–15%

C. 30–50%

D. 70–80%

E. 100%

  1. Genetic FSGS post-transplant recurrence:

A. Same as primary (30–50%)

B. Higher (50–70%)

C. No recurrence

D. Only if NPHS2

E. Only if ACTN4

  1. Perihilar variant FSGS suggests:

A. Primary idiopathic

B. Adaptive/secondary (hyperfiltration)

C. Drug-induced

D. Genetic

E. Viral

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 35-year-old man, nephrotic syndrome, proteinuria 5g/day. Biopsy: segmental sclerosis at tubular pole, foot process effacement >80%. Variant and prognosis:

A. Collapsing — worst

B. Tip lesion — best steroid response

C. Cellular — intermediate

D. Perihilar — adaptive

E. NOS — intermediate

  1. 45-year-old obese man (BMI 42), proteinuria 1.8g/day, eGFR 65. Biopsy: perihilar sclerosis. Management:

A. High-dose steroids

B. Weight loss, ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i, BP control

C. Cyclosporine

D. Rituximab

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 25-year-old HIV+ man, nephrotic syndrome, AKI. Biopsy: collapsing glomerulopathy. Management:

A. Steroids alone

B. ART optimisation + ACEi/ARB; steroids if severe (controversial)

C. CNI

D. Rituximab

E. Plasma exchange

  1. 12-year-old boy, steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome. Genetic testing before transplant. Gene most likely:

A. ACTN4

B. NPHS2 (podocin)

C. TRPC6

D. INF2

E. APOL1

  1. Adult FSGS, family history, AD inheritance. Gene:

A. NPHS2

B. ACTN4 or INF2 or TRPC6

C. WT1

D. PLCE1

E. APOL1

  1. FSGS post-transplant day 3: sudden proteinuria 8g/day. Native disease: primary FSGS. Immediate management:

A. Increase CNI

B. Plasmapheresis + rituximab

C. Pulse steroids only

D. Biopsy only

E. Switch to mTOR inhibitor

  1. Adaptive FSGS vs Primary FSGS — key distinguishing feature:

A. EM foot process effacement

B. Proteinuria: subnephrotic (adaptive) vs nephrotic (primary)

C. LM segmental sclerosis

D. IF IgM/C3 trapping

E. All above

  1. Pamidronate-associated FSGS variant:

A. Tip

B. Collapsing

C. Cellular

D. Perihilar

E. NOS

  1. Genetic FSGS — no recurrence post-transplant because:

A. CNI prevents it

B. No circulating permeability factor (structural podocyte defect)

C. Rituximab prophylaxis

D. Plasmapheresis prophylaxis

E. Different histology

  1. NPHS2 mutation — inheritance and onset:

A. AD, adult

B. AR, childhood

C. AD, childhood

A. AR, adult

E. X-linked

18. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults?

A: FSGS

  • Q: Columbia variants?

A: Collapsing, Tip, Cellular, Perihilar, NOS

  • Q: Worst prognosis variant?

A: Collapsing

  • Q: Best steroid response variant?

A: Tip lesion

  • Q: Perihilar variant suggests?

A: Adaptive/secondary (hyperfiltration)

  • Q: EM in FSGS?

A: Diffuse foot process effacement >80%

  • Q: Primary FSGS induction?

A: Pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks

  • Q: Steroid-resistant FSGS?

A: CNI (CsA/Tac) + low-dose steroids

  • Q: Adaptive FSGS proteinuria?

A: Subnephrotic (<3.5g/day)

  • Q: Post-Tx recurrence primary FSGS?

A: 30–50%

  • Q: Genetic FSGS post-Tx recurrence?

A: None (no circulating factor)

  • Q: NPHS2 — inheritance, onset?

A: AR, childhood

  • Q: ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 — inheritance, onset?

A: AD, adult

  • Q: Collapsing variant associations?

A: HIV, pamidronate, interferon

  • Q: Post-Tx recurrence treatment?

A: Plasmapheresis + rituximab + CNI optimisation

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — FSGS = commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD in adults
  2. B — Tip lesion = best steroid response, best prognosis
  3. B — Collapsing variant = HIV, pamidronate, interferon; worst prognosis
  4. B — Adaptive FSGS = subnephrotic proteinuria (hyperfiltration mechanism)
  5. D — EM: diffuse foot process effacement >80% (vs MCD also diffuse but clinical difference)
  6. B — Primary FSGS nephrotic: pred 1mg/kg × 16 weeks induction
  7. B — Steroid-resistant: CNI + low-dose steroids next line
  8. C — Post-Tx recurrence 30–50% for primary FSGS
  9. C — Genetic FSGS = no circulating factor = no recurrence
  10. B — Perihilar = adaptive/secondary (obesity, reduced renal mass)

SBAs

  1. B — Tip lesion (tubular pole) = best steroid response
  2. B — Obesity + subnephrotic + perihilar = adaptive FSGS → supportive only
  3. B — HIV collapsing: ART optimisation primary; steroids controversial
  4. B — Childhood SRNS = NPHS2 (podocin) most common (AR)
  5. B — Adult AD FSGS = ACTN4, INF2, TRPC6
  6. B — Post-Tx recurrence: plasmapheresis (removes factor) + rituximab (B-cell depletion)
  7. B — Adaptive = subnephrotic; Primary = nephrotic (key clinical distinction)
  8. B — Pamidronate = collapsing variant
  9. B — Genetic = structural podocyte defect, no circulating factor
  10. B — NPHS2 = AR, childhood onset

20. Summary

FSGS is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest primary glomerular cause of ESRD. Columbia variants: Collapsing (worst, HIV/pamidronate), Tip (best steroid response), Perihilar (adaptive). Primary = nephrotic, permeability factor; Adaptive = subnephrotic, hyperfiltration. EM = diffuse FPE >80%. Induction = pred 1mg/kg × 16wk. Steroid-resistant = CNI + steroids. Post-Tx recurrence 30–50% (primary only); genetic = no recurrence. Genetic: NPHS2 (AR, childhood), ACTN4/INF2/TRPC6 (AD, adult).

Exam focus: Variants prognosis, primary vs adaptive distinction, EM findings, treatment algorithm, post-Tx recurrence, genetics.

Clinical relevance: Early aggressive treatment improves outcomes; genetic testing avoids unnecessary immunosuppression and predicts transplant recurrence.

topics.title: Minimal Change Disease
topics.html:
Topic: Primary Glomerulonephritides — Minimal Change Disease — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — IgA Nephropathy (Berger's Disease)]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — Membranous Nephropathy]], [[Primary Glomerulonephritides — FSGS (Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
MCD = commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (70–90%). "Minimal change" on LM; diffuse foot process effacement on EM; negative IF. Highly steroid-responsive (>90% children, ~80% adults). Relapses common; frequent relapsers need steroid-sparing agents.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise typical presentation (selective proteinuria, nephrotic syndrome)
  • Differentiate MCD from FSGS (clinical, histological)
  • Apply steroid treatment protocols (induction, taper, relapse management)
  • Manage frequent relapsers and steroid-dependent cases
  • Recognise complications (infection, thrombosis, steroid toxicity)

2. Epidemiology & Classification

FeatureDetail
ChildrenCommonest cause of nephrotic syndrome (70–90%); peak 2–6 years; M>F
Adults~10–15% of adult nephrotic syndrome; older than children
Primary (Idiopathic)~90%; T-cell dysregulation → permeability factor
Secondary~10%; drugs (NSAIDs, lithium, gold), infections (syphilis, HBV), malignancy (Hodgkin's), allergies, bee stings, vaccines

3. Pathogenesis

MechanismDetail
T-cell DysregulationCirculating permeability factor (unknown cytokine) → podocyte injury
Key MoleculesReduced nephrin, podocin expression; altered glomerular charge barrier
SelectivitySelective proteinuria (albumin lost, larger proteins retained) — charge/size selectivity preserved
GeneticsMostly sporadic; rare familial (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1 — but these usually present as congenital/SRNS)

4. Clinical Presentation

FeatureChildrenAdults
Age2–6 years (peak)Any age
OnsetAcute (days)Acute/subacute
Nephrotic Syndrome~90% (heavy proteinuria, oedema, hypoalbuminaemia, hyperlipidaemia)~70%
ProteinuriaSelective (albumin selective index <0.1)Selective/non-selective
HaematuriaMicroscopic ~20%Microscopic ~30%
HypertensionRare~30%
Renal FunctionNormalNormal/mildly impaired
ExtrarenalNone (unless secondary)May have secondary cause clues

5. Histopathology

ModalityFindings
Light MicroscopyNormal / minimal change (no proliferation, no sclerosis, no crescents)
ImmunofluorescenceNegative (no immune deposits); occasional IgM/C3 in hyalinosis (non-specific)
Electron MicroscopyDiffuse foot process effacement (FPE) of podocytes (≥80%); no electron-dense deposits
[!key]
Triad of MCD: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM = diagnostic.

6. Differential Diagnosis (MCD vs FSGS)

FeatureMCDFSGS
LMNormalSegmental sclerosis
IFNegativeNon-specific (IgM, C3 in scars)
EMDiffuse FPEDiffuse FPE
ProteinuriaSelectiveNon-selective
Steroid Response>90% (children)~40% (primary)
Relapse RateHigh (50–75%)Lower
Progression to ESRDVery rareCommon
AgeChildren predominantAdults predominant

7. Steroid Treatment Protocols

Children (ISKDC / KDIGO)

PhaseRegimen
InductionPrednisolone 60 mg/m²/day (max 60mg) × 4–6 weeks until remission (proteinuria negative × 3 days)
Taper40 mg/m² alternate days × 4–6 weeks
StopThen stop (total ~12–16 weeks)
RelapsePrednisolone 60 mg/m²/day until remission × 3 days → 40 mg/m² alternate days × 4 weeks → taper

Adults (KDIGO)

PhaseRegimen
InductionPrednisolone 1 mg/kg/day (max 80mg) × 8–16 weeks until remission
TaperReduce by 10mg every 1–2 weeks over 4–6 months
Total Duration~6 months minimum

8. Relapse Classification & Management

CategoryDefinitionManagement
Infrequent Relapser<2 relapses in 6mo or <4 in 12moStandard steroid course each relapse
Frequent Relapser≥2 relapses in 6mo or ≥4 in 12moSteroid-sparing agent: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) 1200–1500 mg/m²/day (1st line); Levamisole (2nd line); Cyclophosphamide (8–12 weeks); Tacrolimus/Cyclosporine; Rituximab
Steroid-DependentRelapse within 2 weeks of stopping steroids / during taperSame as frequent relapser
Steroid-ResistantNo remission after 4 weeks (children) or 8–16 weeks (adults) of daily steroidsRe-evaluate diagnosis (FSGS?); renal biopsy; consider CNI, MMF, rituximab

9. Steroid-Sparing Agents (Evidence-Based)

AgentDoseNotes
MMF600–1000 mg/m² BD (max 3g/day)1st line per KDIGO; good safety; monitor FBC, LFT
Levamisole2.5 mg/kg alternate days2nd line; only in children; immunomodulatory; monitor WBC
Cyclophosphamide2–3 mg/kg/day × 8–12 weeksAlkylator; gonadal toxicity; bladder toxicity; single course
CNI (Tac/CsA)Tac 0.05–0.1 mg/kg/day; CsA 3–5 mg/kg/dayNephrotoxicity; relapse on withdrawal common; monitor levels
Rituximab375 mg/m² weekly × 4 (or 1g × 2 doses)B-cell depletion; sustained remission in ~60–70%; infection risk

10. Complications & Prevention

ComplicationRiskPrevention / Management
Infections (pneumococcal, varicella, TB)High (steroids, immunosuppression, proteinuria → IgG loss)Vaccines: pneumococcal, Hib, MenACWY, varicella (pre-steroids); IVIG if severe/refractory; Penicillin prophylaxis if asplenic/hypoglobulinaemia
Thrombosis (renal vein, DVT, PE)Hypercoagulable (antithrombin III loss, ↑ fibrinogen)Anticoagulation if albumin <20g/L + risk factors; treat VTE
Steroid ToxicityCushingoid, growth failure, osteoporosis, cataracts, glucose intoleranceCalcium/Vit D, height monitoring, BP/glucose monitoring, DEXA
Acute Kidney InjuryHypovolaemia (diuresis), sepsis, nephrotoxinsAvoid over-diuresis; monitor volume
HyperlipidaemiaNephrotic syndromeStatin if persistent; diet

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- MCD = commonest childhood nephrotic syndrome (70–90%)
- Triad: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM
- Selective proteinuria (albumin selective index <0.1)
- Steroid response: >90% children, ~80% adults
- Induction: Children 60 mg/m² × 4–6wk; Adults 1mg/kg × 8–16wk
- Relapse: 50–75% relapse; Frequent relapser = ≥2/6mo or ≥4/12mo
- Steroid-sparing: MMF 1st line (KDIGO); Levamisole (children); CNI; Cyclophosphamide (single course); Rituximab
- Steroid-resistant = NOT MCD → re-biopsy (likely FSGS)
- Complications: Infection (vaccines!), Thrombosis (albumin <20), Steroid toxicity
- Secondary MCD: NSAIDs, lithium, Hodgkin's, infections, allergies

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
MCD = normal EMEM = diffuse foot process effacement (hallmark)
MCD = always steroid-responsive10–15% adults steroid-resistant → re-biopsy for FSGS
Selective proteinuria = only MCDAlso early membranous, early diabetic nephropathy
All relapses need CNIInfrequent relapsers = standard steroids only
Levamisole for adultsLevamisole = children only (2nd line)
CNI = cureCNI = relapse on withdrawal common; not curative
Rituximab = only for refractoryNow used earlier for frequent relapsers (sustained remission)
MCD → ESRDVery rare; progression = likely FSGS misdiagnosis

13. Mnemonics

  • MCD Triad: Normal LM, Negative IF, Diffuse FPE = NL-NIF-DF (or MINimal = Minimal LM, Immuno -, Normal EM... no, EM shows FPE)
  • MCD Kids: MCD = Most Childhood Nephrotic
  • Selective proteinuria: Small Albumin Lost = SALective
  • Treatment: Induction 60→40→stop (kids); Relapse = MMF 1st line
  • Frequent Relapser: FR = Four in 12 / Two in 6 = F4/T2
  • Complications: Infection, Thrombosis, Steroid toxicity = ITS

  1. Commonest childhood nephrotic cause (70–90%)

17. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

20. Flashcards

  • Q: Commonest childhood nephrotic cause?

A: Minimal Change Disease (70–90%)

  • Q: MCD histology triad?

A: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM

  • Q: Proteinuria selectivity in MCD?

A: Selective (albumin selective index <0.1)

  • Q: Steroid response childhood MCD?

A: >90%

  • Q: Steroid induction ISKDC (kids)?

A: Pred 60mg/m²/day × 4–6wk → 40mg/m² alt days × 4–6wk

  • Q: Steroid induction adults?

A: Pred 1mg/kg/day × 8–16wk

  • Q: Frequent relapser definition?

A: ≥2 relapses/6mo OR ≥4/12mo

  • Q: Steroid-sparing 1st line (KDIGO)?

A: MMF

  • Q: Levamisole use?

A: 2nd line, children only

  • Q: Cyclophosphamide course?

A: Single 8–12 week course

  • Q: Steroid-resistant MCD →?

A: Re-biopsy (likely FSGS)

  • Q: MCD complications?

A: Infection, Thrombosis (albumin<20), Steroid toxicity

  • Q: Thrombosis prophylaxis?

A: Albumin <20g/L + risk factors

  • Q: Vaccines in MCD?

A: Pneumococcal, Hib, MenACWY, varicella (pre-steroids if possible)

  • Q: Rituximab in MCD?

A: Sustained remission 60–70% frequent relapsers

21. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — MCD = commonest childhood nephrotic syndrome (70–90%)
  2. B — Triad: Normal LM, Negative IF, Diffuse FPE on EM
  3. B — Selective proteinuria (charge/size barrier relatively preserved)
  4. C — >90% children respond to steroids
  5. B — ISKDC protocol: 60mg/m² daily × 4–6wk → 40mg/m² alternate days × 4–6wk
  6. B — KDIGO: frequent relapser = ≥2/6mo or ≥4/12mo
  7. B — MMF 1st line per KDIGO for frequent relapsers
  8. B — Levamisole = 2nd line, children only
  9. C — Steroid-resistant = NOT MCD; re-biopsy for FSGS
  10. B — Albumin <20 + risk factors → prophylactic anticoagulation; vaccines for all

SBAs

  1. B — Child + selective proteinuria + normal BP/Cr = MCD
  2. B — ISKDC: continue daily until remission × 3 days, then alternate day taper
  3. B — Adult taper: reduce 10mg every 1–2 weeks over 4–6 months
  4. B — Frequent relapser (≥2/6mo) → MMF 1st line
  5. B — Varicella on steroids: IV aciclovir + continue steroids (avoid abrupt stop) + VZIG if recent exposure
  6. B — Prior DVT = therapeutic anticoagulation
  7. B — Steroid-resistant + segmental sclerosis = FSGS
  8. B — Levamisole = 2nd line paediatric only
  9. B — Cyclophosphamide = single 8–12 week course
  10. B — Rituximab: sustained remission 60–70% in frequent relapsers/steroid-dependent

22. Summary

Minimal Change Disease is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: Commonest childhood nephrotic (70–90%). Triad: Normal LM + Negative IF + Diffuse FPE on EM. Selective proteinuria. Steroid response >90% kids, ~80% adults. Induction: kids 60mg/m² × 4–6wk; adults 1mg/kg × 8–16wk. Relapse: 50–75%. Frequent relapser (≥2/6mo) → MMF 1st line (KDIGO). Steroid-resistant = re-bioopsy (FSGS). Complications: infection (vaccines!), thrombosis (albumin<20), steroid toxicity.

Exam focus: Triad, selective proteinuria, steroid protocols, relapse classification, MMF 1st line, steroid-resistant = FSGS, complications.

Clinical relevance: High relapse rate requires steroid-sparing strategy; vaccination critical before immunosuppression.

topics.title: Lupus Nephritis
topics.html:
Topic: Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Lupus Nephritis — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Glomerular Diseases — Overview and Classification]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — ANCA-Associated Vasculitis]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Diabetic Nephropathy]], [[Secondary Glomerulonephritides — Amyloidosis]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Lupus Nephritis = renal manifestation of SLE. Affects 40–60% of SLE patients. ISN/RPS 2003 Class I–VI guides treatment. Class III/IV = proliferative (induction: MMF or CYC + steroids); Class V = membranous (add MMF/CNI); Class VI = advanced sclerosis (no immunosuppression).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Classify lupus nephritis using ISN/RPS 2003 classification (Class I–VI)
  • Correlate histology with clinical presentation and treatment
  • Apply induction and maintenance regimens per class
  • Monitor response and manage flares

2. Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FeatureDetail
Prevalence40–60% of SLE patients develop LN
DemographicsYoung women (9:1 F:M); higher severity in African, Asian, Hispanic ancestry
Risk FactorsMale sex, young age at SLE onset, high anti-dsDNA, low complement, hypertension, delay in treatment
GeneticsHLA-DR2/DR3, complement deficiencies (C1q, C4), FCGR polymorphisms, APOL1 (African ancestry)

3. ISN/RPS 2003 Classification (Light Microscopy)

ClassLight MicroscopyImmunofluorescenceElectron MicroscopyClinical FeaturesTreatment
Class IMinimal mesangialMesangial IgG, IgM, C3, C1qMesangial depositsAsymptomatic, normal renal functionNone (monitor)
Class IIMesangial proliferativeMesangial deposits (full-house)Mesangial depositsMild proteinuria, haematuriaSupportive; steroids if proteinuria >1g
Class IIIFocal (<50% glomeruli) endocapillary/proliferativeGranular: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q (capillary + mesangial)Subendothelial + mesangialActive sediment, proteinuria, haematuria, renal impairmentInduction: MMF or CYC + steroids
Class IVDiffuse (≥50% glomeruli) endocapillary/proliferativeGranular "full-house" (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q)Subendothelial + mesangialSevere: nephritic/nephrotic, active sediment, renal impairmentInduction: MMF or CYC + steroids
Class IV-S / IV-GIV-S: Segmental (>50% tuft); IV-G: Global—Subendothelial + mesangialIV-G worse prognosisSame as Class IV
Class VMembranous (diffuse GBM thickening)Granular capillary wall (IgG, C3)Subepithelial depositsNephrotic syndrome; may overlap III/IV (III+V, IV+V)Class V alone: steroids + MMF/CNI; Mixed: treat as III/IV
Class VIAdvanced sclerosis (>90% globally sclerosed)VariableGlobal sclerosisAdvanced CKD, poor responseNo immunosuppression (renal replacement)

4. Activity & Chronicity Indices

Activity Index (AI)Chronicity Index (CI)
Endocapillary hypercellularityGlomerulosclerosis
Neutrophils/karyorrhexisFibrous crescents
Wire loopsTubular atrophy
Cellular crescentsInterstitial fibrosis
Hyaline deposits
Max AI = 24Max CI = 12
[!key]
High AI = active inflammation = responds to immunosuppression. High CI = irreversible damage = poor response.

5. Clinical Presentation by Class

ClassPresentation
I/IIAsymptomatic, microscopic haematuria, subnephrotic proteinuria (<1g)
IIIProteinuria 1–3g, active sediment (RBC casts), mild renal impairment
IVNephritic/nephrotic, heavy proteinuria (>3g), active sediment, HTN, renal impairment
VNephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, oedema, hypoalbuminaemia); may have active sediment if mixed
VIAdvanced CKD, small kidneys, minimal proteinuria

6. Serology & Monitoring

MarkerSignificance
Anti-dsDNA↑ titre correlates with disease activity (esp. Class III/IV)
Complement (C3, C4)Low C3/C4 = active disease; normalisation = response
Anti-C1qHighly specific for LN; correlates with activity
UrinalysisProteinuria (UPCR), active sediment (RBC casts, WBC casts)
Renal biopsyGold standard for classification; repeat for flare assessment

7. Treatment — Induction (Class III/IV ± V)

RegimenDose/DurationNotes
MMF (Preferred)MMF 2–3 g/day (target 2–3g) + Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day (max 60mg) × 6moALMS trial: MMF non-inferior to CYC; better safety; 1st line per KDIGO/EULAR
Cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus)IV CYC 500 mg × 6 fortnightly (low-dose) + steroidsEuro-Lupus: equally effective, less gonadal toxicity; preferred in Caucasians
Cyclophosphamide (NIH)IV CYC 0.5–1g/m² monthly × 6mo + steroidsHigher dose; more toxicity; used in severe/refractory
Add-onHydroxychloroquine (all SLE), ACEi/ARB, BP control, statinHCQ reduces flares, improves survival
[!key]
Induction 6 months. Assess response at 3mo (proteinuria ↓50%) and 6mo (remission).

8. Treatment — Maintenance (Class III/IV)

AgentDoseDuration
MMF1–2 g/dayMinimum 3 years (longer if high-risk)
Azathioprine1.5–2 mg/kg/dayAlternative if MMF intolerant
CNI (Tac/CsA)Tac 0.05–0.1 mg/kg/dayOption for membranous overlap
PrednisoloneTaper to ≤5–7.5 mg/dayLowest effective dose
Hydroxychloroquine200–400 mg/dayLifelong (reduces flares, thrombosis, damage)

9. Class V (Membranous) LN Treatment

ScenarioTreatment
Pure Class V (nephrotic)Prednisolone + MMF (or CNI) × 6mo induction → MMF maintenance
Mixed III+V / IV+VTreat as Class III/IV (MMF/CYC + steroids)
Subnephrotic proteinuriaACEi/ARB + HCQ + monitor

10. Flare Management

Flare TypeDefinitionManagement
Renal Flare↑Proteinuria >50% from baseline, new active sediment, ↑CrRe-biopsy (class may change); restart induction regimen
Extra-renal FlareArthritis, rash, serositis, cytopeniasTreat per SLE guidelines (steroids, HCQ, immunosuppressants)

11. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- LN affects 40–60% SLE; ISN/RPS Class I–VI guides treatment
- Class III/IV = proliferative → MMF or CYC + steroids (6mo induction)
- MMF 1st line (ALMS trial); Euro-Lupus CYC (low-dose, less toxicity)
- Class V = membranous: Pure = steroids + MMF/CNI; Mixed = treat as III/IV
- Class VI = advanced sclerosis → no immunosuppression
- Full-house IF = IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q (pathognomonic for SLE)
- Activity vs Chronicity Index: AI = treatable; CI = irreversible
- Monitor: anti-dsDNA, C3/C4, UPCR, active sediment
- Maintenance: MMF 1–2g/day × ≥3 years + HCQ lifelong
- Flare: re-biopsy (class may change); restart induction

12. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All LN classes need immunosuppressionClass I/II = supportive; Class VI = no immunosuppression
Class V = always pure membranousOften mixed (III+V, IV+V) → treat as proliferative
CYC = always high-dose NIHEuro-Lupus low-dose (500mg × 6) equally effective, less toxicity
MMF = only maintenanceMMF = 1st line INDUCTION (ALMS trial)
Anti-dsDNA = always correlatesNot 100%; some flares without rise; complement more reliable
Flare = same classRe-biopsy essential — class often changes (e.g., IV → III, III → V)
HCQ = optionalHCQ mandatory all SLE (reduces flares, thrombosis, organ damage)
Maintenance = 1 yearMinimum 3 years (high relapse if stopped early)

13. Mnemonics

  • ISN/RPS Classes: I = Ignorable, II = Mild, III = Focal, IV = Diffuse, V = Membranous, VI = Sclerotic = IMFDMS
  • Full-house IF: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q = III-CC
  • Induction: MMF or CYC + Steroids = MCS
  • Euro-Lupus: Euro = Easy CYC (500mg × 6)
  • Maintenance: MMF ≥ 3 years = M3
  • Monitor: Anti-dsDNA, Complement, UPCR, Sediment = ACUS

14. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Lupus Nephritis))

Classification

Class_I[Minimal mesangial]

Class_II[Mesangial proliferative]

Class_III[Focal proliferative <50%]

Class_IV[Diffuse proliferative ≥50%]

Class_IV_SG[IV-S segmental, IV-G global]

Class_V[Membranous]

Class_VI[Advanced sclerosis >90%]

Histology

LM[ISN/RPS]

IF[Full-house: IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q]

EM[Subendothelial (III/IV), Subepithelial (V)]

Indices[Activity vs Chronicity]

Treatment

Induction[MMF or CYC + Steroids 6mo]

Maintenance[MMF 1-2g 3yr + HCQ lifelong]

Class_V[Pure: MMF/CNI; Mixed: as III/IV]

Class_VI[No immunosuppression]

Monitoring

Anti_dsDNA[Activity]

Complement[C3/C4]

UPCR[Proteinuria]

Sediment[RBC casts]

Flare

Rebiopsy[Class may change]

Restart_Induction[MMF/CYC + steroids]

```

15. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. ISN/RPS Classes I–VI and which need immunosuppression
  2. Full-house IF components
  3. Class III vs IV distinction (focal <50% vs diffuse ≥50%)
  4. Induction: MMF 2–3g/day + steroids × 6mo (1st line)
  5. Euro-Lupus CYC: 500mg × 6 fortnightly
  6. Class V treatment (pure vs mixed)
  7. Class VI = no immunosuppression
  8. Maintenance ≥3 years + HCQ lifelong
  9. Flare = re-biopsy + restart induction

16. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
