GeneticVHL syndrome (3p25 deletion/mutation) → clear cell RCC; HLRCC (fumarate hydratase) → papillary type 2; BHD (FLCN) → chromophobe/oncocytoma
OccupationalTrichloroethylene, cadmium, asbestos

Histological Subtypes

Subtype%GeneticsPrognosis
Clear Cell70–80%VHL loss (3p), PBRM1, SETD2, BAP1Intermediate
Papillary Type 110–15%MET activationBetter
Papillary Type 25–10%Fumarate hydratase (HLRCC), NRF2Worse
Chromophobe5%TP53, PTEN, BHD (FLCN)Better
Collecting Duct<1%SMARCB1, SETD2Aggressive
Unclassified3–5%VariableVariable

Clinical Presentation

FeatureDetail
Classic TriadFlank pain, haematuria, palpable mass (now <10%)
Incidental>50% (CT/US for other reasons)
ParaneoplasticErythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP), hypertension (renin), amyloidosis, Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction)
MetastaticLung (50%), bone, liver, brain, adrenal

Staging (TNM AJCC 8th)

T StageDefinition
T1a≤4 cm, renal only
T1b4–7 cm, renal only
T2a7–10 cm, renal only
T2b>10 cm, renal only
T3aPerinephric fat / renal vein / IVC (infradiaphragmatic)
T3bIVC supradiaphragmatic / IVC wall
T3cIVC wall invasion
T4Beyond Gerota's fascia (adrenal, adjacent organs)
N1Regional nodes
M1Distant mets

Management by Stage

StageTreatment
T1a (≤4 cm)Active surveillance (elderly/comorbid), Ablation (RFA/cryo), Partial nephrectomy (gold standard if feasible)
T1b (4–7 cm)Partial nephrectomy (preferred), Radical nephrectomy
T2 (>7 cm)Radical nephrectomy
T3Radical nephrectomy + IVC thrombectomy if needed
T4Radical nephrectomy + multivisceral resection (if resectable)
Metastatic (M1)Cytoreductive nephrectomy (selected) + Systemic therapy
[!key]
Nephron-sparing (partial) = preferred for T1 if technically feasible — equivalent cancer control, better renal function.

Systemic Therapy (Metastatic RCC)

LineRegimen
1st Line (Clear Cell)IO + TKI: Pembrolizumab + axitinib, Nivolumab + cabozantinib, Avelumab + axitinib
IO + IO: Nivolumab + ipilimumab (intermediate/poor risk IMDC)
2nd LineTKI (cabozantinib, axitinib, lenvatinib+everolimus) if IO-naïve; IO if TKI-naïve
Non-Clear CellTKI (sunitinib, cabozantinib); clinical trials

IMDC Prognostic Model (Metastatic RCC)

FactorPoints
Hb < LLN1
Corrected Ca > ULN1
KPS <80%1
Time from diagnosis to treatment <1yr1
Neutrophils > ULN1
Platelets > ULN1
Risk GroupsFavourable (0), Intermediate (1–2), Poor (≥3)

3. Bladder Cancer

Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FactorDetail
Incidence4th commonest cancer in men; 70–80yo; M:F 3:1
Smoking#1 risk (50% cases); 2–4x risk
OccupationalAromatic amines (benzidine, β-naphthylamine) — dye, rubber, leather, textile, paint
ChemotherapyCyclophosphamide (acrolein), ifosfamide
RadiationPelvic RT
Chronic IrritationStones, indwelling catheter, schistosomiasis (S. haematobium → squamous cell)
GeneticLynch syndrome, RB1 mutation

Classification by Invasion

CategoryDefinition%
NMIBC (Non-Muscle Invasive)Ta, Tis (CIS), T175–80%
MIBC (Muscle Invasive)T2–T420–25%

TNM Staging (Bladder)

StageDefinition
TaNon-invasive papillary
Tis (CIS)Carcinoma in situ (flat, high-grade)
T1Lamina propria invasion (subepithelial connective tissue)
T2Muscularis propria invasion (T2a superficial, T2b deep)
T3Perivesical fat (T3a micro, T3b macro)
T4Adjacent organs (prostate, uterus, vagina, pelvic/abdominal wall)

NMIBC Risk Stratification (EAU/AUA)

Risk GroupCriteriaRecurrence/Progression Risk
LowSingle, primary, Ta, LG, <3cm, no CISRecur 15%, Prog <1%
IntermediateMultiple/recurrent Ta LG, or Ta HG <3cmRecur 30–50%, Prog 5–10%
HighAny T1, any HG, any CIS, multiple/large Ta HGRecur 50–70%, Prog 15–30%

Management

NMIBC

StepTreatment
TURBTComplete resection (including muscle for T1); Re-TURBT mandatory for T1, incomplete, no muscle in specimen
Low RiskSingle post-op instillation (mitomycin C/epirubicin/gemcitabine within 24h)
IntermediateInduction BCG (6 weekly) ± Maintenance BCG (3 weekly at 3,6,12,18,24,30,36mo)
High RiskBCG induction + maintenance (1–3yr); Radical cystectomy if BCG-unresponsive
BCG-UnresponsiveRadical cystectomy (gold standard); Intravesical alternatives (gemcitabine/docetaxel, pembrolizumab, nadofaragene firadenovec)

MIBC

StepTreatment
Localised (T2–T4a, N0)Radical cystectomy + pelvic lymphadenectomy + urinary diversion (ileal conduit, neobladder, continent diversion)
NeoadjuvantCisplatin-based chemo (GC/MVAC) → cystectomy (survival benefit)
AdjuvantIf not given neoadjuvant + high risk (pT3/T4, N+)
Trimodality (Bladder Preservation)Maximal TURBT + concurrent chemoradiation (selected patients)
MetastaticCisplatin-based chemo (GC) → IO maintenance (avelumab); IO if cisplatin-ineligible

4. Upper Tract Urothelial Carcinoma (UTUC)

FeatureDetail
Incidence5–10% of urothelial cancers; 70–80yo
Risk FactorsSmoking, aromatic amines, Balkan nephropathy (aristolochic acid), Lynch syndrome, analgesic nephropathy
PresentationHaematuria (90%), flank pain, UTI, incidental
DiagnosisCT urogram (gold standard), ureteroscopy + biopsy, urine cytology (low sensitivity)
StagingSame TNM as bladder (Ta–T4, N, M)
Risk StratLow: Ta LG, solitary, <2cm; High: T1, HG, CIS, multifocal, >2cm, hydronephrosis
TreatmentRadical nephroureterectomy (RNU) + bladder cuff (gold standard high-risk); Kidney-sparing (ureteroscopic ablation/laser, segmental resection) for low-risk
AdjuvantCisplatin-based chemo if pT3/T4 or N+ (controversial)
SurveillanceHigh risk of bladder recurrence — cystoscopy q3mo × 2yr

5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- RCC = commonest renal cancer; Clear cell 70–80%; VHL mutation
- RCC classic triad (flank pain, haematuria, mass) <10%; incidental >50%
- RCC paraneoplastic: erythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP)
- RCC staging: T1a ≤4cm → partial nephrectomy/ablation/surveillance; T1b 4–7 → partial; T2+ → radical
- Metastatic RCC: IO+TKI 1st line (pembro+axiti, nivo+cabo); IMDC risk stratification
- Bladder Ca: smoking #1; occupational aromatic amines; M>F 3:1
- NMIBC (75%): Ta, Tis, T1; MIBC (25%): T2+
- NMIBC risk: Low (single Ta LG) → single post-op chemo; High (T1/HG/CIS) → BCG induction+maintenance
- BCG-unresponsive → radical cystectomy
- MIBC: neoadjuvant cisplatin chemo → radical cystectomy + diversion
- UTUC: Balkan nephropathy (aristolochic acid); RNU + bladder cuff; high bladder recurrence
- Schistosomiasis → squamous cell bladder cancer

6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All RCC = radical nephrectomyT1a/T1b → partial/ablation/surveillance (nephron-sparing)
Clear cell = only RCCPapillary, chromophobe, collecting duct, unclassified
VHL = only clear cellVHL = clear cell; but also sporadic clear cell have VHL loss
Bladder Ca = only TCCSquamous (schisto), adenocarcinoma, small cell, sarcomatoid
NMIBC = always BCGLow risk = single post-op chemo only; BCG for intermediate/high
BCG = 6 weeks onlyMaintenance BCG (1–3yr) essential for high risk (SWOG/Sylvester)
MIBC = cystectomy onlyNeoadjuvant cisplatin chemo → cystectomy = standard
UTUC = same as bladderRNU + cuff; kidney-sparing for low risk; high bladder recurrence risk
Schistosomiasis = TCCSquamous cell carcinoma (chronic inflammation)
RCC mets = only lungBone, liver, brain, adrenal also common

7. Mnemonics

  • RCC Subtypes: Clear (70%), Papillary (15%), Chromophobe (5%), Collecting Duct (<1%) = CPCCD
  • RCC Paraneoplastic: Erythrocytosis (EPO), Hypercalcaemia (PTHrP), Hypertension (Renin) = EHH
  • RCC Staging: T1a ≤4, T1b 4–7, T2 7–10, T3 fat/vein/IVC, T4 Gerota's = T1T2T3T4
  • RCC Systemic: IO+TKI 1st line; IMDC risk = ITC
  • Bladder Risk: Smoking #1; Aromatic Amines = S#1 AA
  • NMIBC: Ta, Tis, T1 = TTT
  • NMIBC Risk: Low (Ta LG single), Intermediate (multi/recur Ta LG), High (T1/HG/CIS) = LIH
  • BCG: Induction 6wk + Maintenance 1–3yr = IM
  • MIBC: NeoAdjuvant Cisplatin → Cystectomy = NAC
  • UTUC: Radical NephroUreterectomy (RNU) + Bladder Cuff = RNB

8. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Urological Malignancies))

RCC

Subtypes[Clear 70%, Papillary, Chromophobe]

Genetics[VHL loss (3p) - clear cell]

Paraneoplastic[Erythrocytosis, Hypercalcaemia, HTN]

Staging[T1a≤4, T1b4-7, T2>7, T3 fat/vein, T4 Gerota]

Local[Surveillance, Ablation, Partial (T1), Radical (T2+)]

Metastatic[IO+TKI 1st line (Pembro+Axi, Nivo+Cabo); IMDC risk]

Bladder

Risk[Smoking #1; Aromatic amines; Schisto → SCC]

NMIBC[Ta, Tis, T1 = 75%]

NMIBC_Risk[Low (Ta LG single), Int (multi Ta LG), High (T1/HG/CIS)]

NMIBC_Tx[TURBT; Low=post-op chemo; High=BCG Ind+Maint]

BCG_Unresponsive[Cystectomy; Pembrolizumab, Gem/Doce]

MIBC[T2+; NeoAdj Cisplatin → Radical Cystectomy + Diversion]

Metastatic[GC → IO maintenance (Avelumab)]

UTUC

Risk[Smoking, Balkan (aristolochic), Lynch, Analgesic]

Dx[CT Urogram, Ureteroscopy + biopsy]

Rx[RNU + cuff (high risk); Kidney-sparing (low risk)]

Surveillance[Bladder recurrence high = cysto q3mo]

```

9. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. RCC: clear cell 70%, VHL mutation, paraneoplastic (EPO, PTHrP)
  2. RCC staging: T1a ≤4cm → partial/ablation/surveillance; T2+ → radical
  3. Metastatic RCC: IO+TKI 1st line; IMDC risk (favourable/intermediate/poor)
  4. Bladder Ca: smoking #1, aromatic amines, M>F 3:1
  5. NMIBC (75%): Ta, Tis, T1; Risk: Low (Ta LG single) → post-op chemo; High (T1/HG/CIS) → BCG induction + maintenance
  6. BCG-unresponsive → radical cystectomy
  7. MIBC: neoadjuvant cisplatin chemo → radical cystectomy + diversion
  8. UTUC: Balkan nephropathy (aristolochic acid); RNU + cuff; bladder surveillance
  9. Schistosomiasis → squamous cell bladder cancer

10. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴RCC subtypes/VHL, staging & management (partial vs radical), metastatic IO+TKI, bladder risk factors, NMIBC risk stratification & BCG, MIBC neoadjuvant + cystectomy, UTUC RNU
Should Know 🟡Papillary/chromophobe RCC, non-clear cell systemic therapy, bladder preservation (trimodality), UTUC kidney-sparing, surveillance protocols, Lynch syndrome
Nice to Know 🟢Novel IO combinations, HIF-2α inhibitors (belzutifan), ctDNA, adjuvant pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-564), enfortumab vedotin, quality of life outcomes

12. MCQs (10)

  1. Commonest histological subtype of RCC:

A. Papillary

B. Chromophobe

C. Clear cell (70–80%)

D. Collecting duct

E. Oncocytoma

  1. Genetic alteration in clear cell RCC:

A. MET activation

B. VHL loss (3p deletion/mutation)

C. Fumarate hydratase

D. FLCN (BHD)

E. TP53

  1. RCC paraneoplastic syndrome — erythrocytosis mediated by:

A. PTHrP

B. Renin

C. EPO

D. VEGF

E. IL-6

  1. T1a RCC (≤4 cm) — preferred management:

A. Radical nephrectomy

B. Active surveillance / Ablation / Partial nephrectomy

C. Systemic therapy

D. Radiotherapy

E. Active surveillance only

  1. Metastatic clear cell RCC — 1st line systemic therapy:

A. Sunitinib alone

B. IO + TKI (pembrolizumab + axitinib / nivolumab + cabozantinib)

C. Pazopanib alone

D. High-dose IL-2

E. Everolimus alone

  1. Commonest risk factor for bladder cancer:

A. Occupational dyes

B. Smoking (50% cases)

C. Schistosomiasis

D. Cyclophosphamide

E. Pelvic radiation

  1. NMIBC high-risk features:

A. Single Ta LG <3cm

B. Any T1, any HG, any CIS

C. Multiple Ta LG

D. Primary Ta LG <3cm

E. No CIS

  1. BCG-unresponsive NMIBC — gold standard treatment:

A. Repeat BCG

B. Radical cystectomy

C. Intravesical gemcitabine

D. Radiotherapy

E. Surveillance

  1. Muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC) — standard of care:

A. Radical cystectomy alone

B. Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy → radical cystectomy

C. Radiotherapy alone

D. BCG induction + maintenance

E. Intravesical chemo only

  1. Upper tract urothelial carcinoma — associated with Balkan nephropathy:

A. Aristolochic acid

B. Aromatic amines

C. Cyclophosphamide

D. Schistosomiasis

E. Lynch syndrome

13. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, incidental 3.5 cm left renal mass on CT for abdominal pain. Biopsy: clear cell RCC. Management:

A. Radical nephrectomy

B. Partial nephrectomy (nephron-sparing)

C. Active surveillance

D. Ablation (RFA)

E. Systemic therapy

  1. 65-year-old man, metastatic clear cell RCC, IMDC intermediate risk (Hb low, Ca high). 1st line:

A. Sunitinib

B. Pembrolizumab + axitinib OR nivolumab + cabozantinib

C. Nivolumab + ipilimumab

D. Pazopanib

E. Everolimus

  1. 70-year-old man, smoking 40 pack-years, painless haematuria. Cystoscopy: solitary 2cm papillary tumour. TURBT: Ta LG. Management:

A. Radical cystectomy

B. Single post-op intravesical mitomycin C (within 24h)

C. BCG induction + maintenance

D. Repeat TURBT in 6 weeks

E. Surveillance only

  1. 60-year-old man, T1 HG bladder cancer on TURBT, no muscle in specimen. Next step:

A. BCG induction

B. Repeat TURBT (mandatory for T1, no muscle)

C. Radical cystectomy

D. Single post-op chemo

E. Surveillance

  1. 55-year-old woman, CIS on bladder biopsy. BCG induction + maintenance completed. 6mo later: recurrent CIS. BCG-unresponsive. Management:

A. Repeat BCG

B. Radical cystectomy

C. Intravesical gemcitabine

D. Pembrolizumab (if refuses cystectomy)

E. Radiotherapy

  1. 65-year-old man, cT2N0M0 bladder cancer. Fit for surgery, cisplatin-eligible. Optimal management:

A. Radical cystectomy alone

B. Neoadjuvant cisplatin (GC or dose-dense MVAC) → radical cystectomy

C. BCG + chemo

D. Radiotherapy alone

E. Trimodality (TMT) only

  1. 40-year-old man, haematuria, CT urogram: right upper ureteric mass. Ureteroscopy biopsy: HG UTUC. Hydronephrosis present. Management:

A. Ureteroscopic laser ablation

B. Radical nephroureterectomy + bladder cuff excision

C. Segmental ureterectomy

D. BCG instillation

E. Surveillance

  1. UTUC patient post-RNU. Bladder recurrence risk:

A. <5%

B. 10–15%

C. 20–50% (high) — cystoscopy q3mo × 2yr

D. 60–70%

E. Only if CIS present

  1. Schistosomiasis-associated bladder cancer — histology:

A. Urothelial carcinoma

B. Squamous cell carcinoma

C. Adenocarcinoma

D. Small cell carcinoma

E. Sarcomatoid

  1. RCC with IVC tumour thrombus extending above diaphragm (T3b). Management:

A. Radical nephrectomy alone

B. Radical nephrectomy + IVC thrombectomy (cardiac bypass if supradiaphragmatic)

C. Systemic therapy only

D. Radiotherapy

E. Ablation

14. Flashcards

  • Q: RCC commonest subtype?

A: Clear cell (70–80%)

  • Q: RCC genetics?

A: VHL loss (3p) in clear cell

  • Q: RCC paraneoplastic?

A: Erythrocytosis (EPO), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP), HTN (renin)

  • Q: T1a RCC management?

A: Surveillance / ablation / partial nephrectomy

  • Q: Metastatic RCC 1st line?

A: IO+TKI (pembro+axiti, nivo+cabo); IO+IO (nivo+ipi) for int/poor IMDC

  • Q: Bladder Ca #1 risk?

A: Smoking (50%)

  • Q: NMIBC stages?

A: Ta, Tis (CIS), T1

  • Q: NMIBC high risk?

A: T1, HG, CIS

  • Q: NMIBC low risk Tx?

A: Single post-op intravesical chemo

  • Q: NMIBC high risk Tx?

A: BCG induction + maintenance (1–3yr)

  • Q: BCG-unresponsive?

A: Radical cystectomy (gold standard)

  • Q: MIBC standard?

A: Neoadjuvant cisplatin chemo → radical cystectomy

  • Q: UTUC Balkans?

A: Aristolochic acid

  • Q: UTUC treatment?

A: RNU + bladder cuff (high risk); kidney-sparing (low risk)

  • Q: UTUC bladder recurrence?

A: 20–50%; cystoscopy q3mo × 2yr

  • Q: Schistosomiasis bladder Ca?

A: Squamous cell carcinoma

  • Q: IMDC risk factors?

A: Hb↓, Ca↑, KPS<80%, dx→tx<1yr, neutrophils↑, platelets↑

15. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. C — Clear cell = 70–80% of RCC
  2. B — VHL loss (3p) = hallmark of clear cell RCC
  3. C — Erythrocytosis = EPO overproduction
  4. B — T1a ≤4cm = nephron-sparing (partial/ablation/surveillance)
  5. B — IO+TKI = 1st line standard (KEYNOTE-426, CheckMate-9ER)
  6. B — Smoking = 50% of bladder cancer
  7. B — High risk NMIBC = any T1, any HG, any CIS
  8. B — BCG-unresponsive = radical cystectomy (gold standard)
  9. B — Neoadjuvant cisplatin → cystectomy = survival benefit
  10. A — Balkan nephropathy = aristolochic acid

SBAs

  1. B — 3.5cm = T1a → partial nephrectomy (nephron-sparing preferred)
  2. B — IMDC intermediate = IO+TKI (pembro+axiti or nivo+cabo)
  3. B — Ta LG solitary = low risk → single post-op chemo
  4. B — T1 HG + no muscle = repeat TURBT mandatory
  5. B — BCG-unresponsive CIS = radical cystectomy
  6. B — cT2N0 cisplatin-eligible = neoadjuvant GC → cystectomy
  7. B — HG UTUC + hydronephrosis = high risk → RNU + cuff
  8. C — UTUC bladder recurrence 20–50% = intensive cysto surveillance
  9. B — Schistosomiasis = squamous cell carcinoma
  10. B — T3b (IVC supradiaphragmatic) = radical nephrectomy + IVC thrombectomy with cardiac bypass

16. Summary

Urological Malignancies is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: RCC: clear cell 70% (VHL loss); paraneoplastic: erythrocytosis, hypercalcaemia. Staging: T1a ≤4cm → partial/ablation/surveillance; T2+ → radical. Metastatic: IO+TKI 1st line; IMDC risk stratification. Bladder Ca: smoking #1, aromatic amines. NMIBC (75%): Ta/Tis/T1. Risk: Low (Ta LG single) → post-op chemo; High (T1/HG/CIS) → BCG induction + maintenance. BCG-unresponsive → radical cystectomy. MIBC: neoadjuvant cisplatin → radical cystectomy + diversion. UTUC: Balkan = aristolochic acid; RNU + cuff; bladder surveillance q3mo. Schistosomiasis → squamous cell.

Exam focus: RCC subtypes/staging/management, metastatic therapy, bladder risk factors, NMIBC risk stratification, BCG protocol, MIBC neoadjuvant, UTUC management, schistosomiasis histology.

Clinical relevance: Nephron-sparing surgery preserves renal function; IO revolutionised metastatic RCC; BCG maintenance critical for NMIBC; cystectomy morbidity high but curative for MIBC.

topics.title: Male Reproductive Bph Prostate Testicular
topics.html:
Topic: Male Reproductive Tract Disorders (BPH, Prostate Cancer, Testicular Cancer) — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Urinary Incontinence]], [[Urinary Tract Obstruction]], [[Urological Malignancies (RCC, Bladder Cancer, Urothelial Cancer)]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
BPH = commonest benign tumour in men; Prostate cancer = commonest male cancer; Testicular cancer = commonest solid cancer in young men (15–35). Key: BPH = LUTS, PSA screening controversial; Prostate Ca = Gleason grading, TNM staging; Testicular Ca = germ cell (seminoma vs non-seminoma), tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG, LDH).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose and manage Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)
  • Diagnose, stage, and manage Prostate Cancer
  • Diagnose, stage, and manage Testicular Cancer
  • Apply screening, staging, and treatment algorithms
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH)

Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

FeatureDetail
PrevalenceHistological: 50% at 50yo, 80% at 80yo; Clinical: 25–50% symptomatic
PathophysiologyStromal + glandular proliferation in transition zone → urethral compression → BOO
Risk FactorsAge, family history, metabolic syndrome, obesity, sedentary lifestyle

Clinical Presentation (LUTS)

Storage SymptomsVoiding SymptomsPost-Micturition
FrequencyHesitancyPost-void dribbling
UrgencyWeak streamSensation of incomplete emptying
NocturiaIntermittency
Urge incontinenceStraining
Terminal dribbling
[!key]
IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score): 0–7 mild, 8–19 moderate, 20–35 severe. QoL question separate (0–6).

Assessment

ComponentDetails
HistoryIPSS, voiding diary, QoL, sexual function (IIEF), medical history, medications (α-blockers, anticholinergics, diuretics)
ExaminationDRE: enlarged, smooth, firm, non-tender (median sulcus obliterated); abdominal (distended bladder)
InvestigationsPSA (age-adjusted: <3.5 at 50–59, <4.5 at 60–69, <6.5 at 70+), urinalysis, creatinine, eGFR, glucose, Uroflowmetry (Qmax <15 mL/s = obstruction), PVR US
UrodynamicsPressure-flow study if diagnosis unclear, prior surgery, neurogenic

Management

Conservative (Mild IPSS <8, bother low)

MeasureDetails
LifestyleFluid modification (reduce evening), reduce caffeine/alcohol, treat constipation, avoid anticholinergics/decongestants, timed voiding, double voiding
Watchful WaitingAnnual IPSS, DRE, PSA, PVR

Medical (Moderate-Severe IPSS ≥8, bother high)

Drug ClassAgentsMechanismNotes
α-BlockersTamsulosin 0.4mg OD, Alfuzosin 10mg OD, Silodosin 8mg OD, Doxazosin 4mg OD, Terazosinα1A antagonism → prostate/urethral relaxation1st line; rapid onset (days); side effects: dizziness, retrograde ejaculation (tamsulosin/silodosin), hypotension (doxazosin/terazosin)
5-ARIsFinasteride 5mg OD, Dutasteride 0.5mg OD↓ DHT → prostate volume ↓ 20–30%Prostate >30–40g or PSA >1.5; slow onset (6–12mo); reduces AUR/surgery risk; side effects: sexual dysfunction, gynaecomastia
Combinationα-blocker + 5-ARI (e.g., tamsulosin + dutasteride)SynergisticMTOPS/CombAT trials: superior to monotherapy for progression; larger prostates
Phosphodiesterase-5 InhibitorTadalafil 5mg ODSmooth muscle relaxationLUTS + ED; alternative if α-blocker contraindicated
Anticholinergics/β3-AgonistsAdd-on for storage symptoms (OAB)If persistent storage symptoms after α-blockerCaution if high PVR

Surgical (Failed Medical, Complications, Patient Choice)

IndicationProcedure
AUR (acute urinary retention)Trial without catheter (TWOC) after α-blocker; if fails → surgery
Recurrent UTI / Haematuria / Bladder stones / Renal impairmentSurgery
Patient choiceAfter informed discussion
ProcedureDetails
TURP (Transurethral Resection)Gold standard <80–100g; monopolar/bipolar; 85–90% symptom improvement
HoLEP (Holmium Laser Enucleation)Any size; less bleeding, shorter catheter/stay; equivalent to TURP; >100g preferred
PVP (Photoselective Vaporisation)GreenLight laser; <80g; anticoagulation safe
Rezum (Water Vapor Thermal)Minimally invasive; office-based; preserves ejaculation
UroLift (Prostatic Urethral Lift)Mechanical; preserves ejaculation; <70g; less durable
Simple Prostatectomy (Open/Robotic)>100–150g; high morbidity; rarely needed now
[!key]
TURP = gold standard <80–100g; HoLEP = any size, >100g preferred. Bipolar TURP = saline, less TUR syndrome.

Complications

ComplicationManagement
TUR Syndrome (hyponatraemia)Glycine absorption → limit resection time, bipolar saline; treat hyponatraemia
Retrograde Ejaculation~70% TURP; α-blockers also; counsel pre-op
Erectile Dysfunction5–10% TURP; nerve-sparing techniques
StrictureUrethral dilation, urethrotomy
IncontinenceRare (<1%); usually transient; AUS if persistent

3. Prostate Cancer

Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FactorDetail
IncidenceCommonest male cancer (UK/US); >50yo; Black > White > Asian
RiskAge, family history (2–3x if 1st degree), Black ethnicity, BRCA2, Lynch syndrome, diet (high fat, low veg)
ScreeningPSA controversial — ERSPC (↓ mortality 21%), PLCO (no benefit); Shared decision-making (age 50–69, life expectancy >10yr)

Diagnosis

StepMethod
PSA>3–4 ng/mL (age-adjusted); free:total PSA ratio <10–15% ↑ specificity
DRENodule, induration, asymmetry (25% Ca with normal PSA)
MRI (mpMRI)PI-RADS 1–5; PI-RADS 3–5 → targeted biopsy
BiopsyTransperineal (preferred, lower sepsis) or TRUS-guided; systematic + targeted
PathologyGleason Grade Group 1–5 (see below)

Gleason Grading (2014 ISUP)

Grade GroupGleason ScorePatternPrognosis
13+3=6Only pattern 3Very low risk
23+4=7Predominant 3, minor 4Low-intermediate
34+3=7Predominant 4, minor 3Intermediate
48 (4+4, 3+5, 5+3)Pattern 4/5High
59–10 (4+5, 5+4, 5+5)Pattern 5Very high

Staging (TNM AJCC 8th)

T StageDefinition
T1Clinically inapparent (T1a/b TURP incidental, T1c needle biopsy)
T2Confined to prostate (T2a ≤half lobe, T2b >half lobe, T2c both lobes)
T3Extraprostatic extension (T3a microscopic, T3b seminal vesicle invasion)
T4Adjacent structures (bladder neck, rectum, levator, pelvic wall)
N1Regional nodes
M1Distant mets (M1a nodes, M1b bone, M1c other)

Risk Stratification (Localised)

RiskCriteria
Very LowT1c, GG1, PSA <10, <3 cores, ≤50% core involvement, PSA density <0.15
LowT1–T2a, GG1, PSA <10
IntermediateFavourable: T2b–T2c, GG2, or PSA 10–20; Unfavourable: GG3, or >1 intermediate factor
HighT3a, GG4–5, PSA >20
Very HighT3b–T4, GG5, >1 high-risk feature

Management by Risk

Localised

RiskOptions
Very Low / LowActive Surveillance (AS) preferred; Radical prostatectomy (RP), RT (EBRT/brachytherapy)
Intermediate FavourableRP, RT + ADT (4–6mo), AS (selected low-volume GG2)
Intermediate UnfavourableRP, RT + ADT (4–6mo)
High / Very HighRP + PLND, RT + ADT (18–36mo)

Advanced

StageTreatment
Locally Advanced (T3–T4, N0)RT + long-term ADT (18–36mo) ± abiraterone/apalutamide/docetaxel (STAMPEDE)
Node-Positive (N1)ADT + abiraterone/apalutamide/docetaxel; RT to prostate ± nodes
Metastatic Hormone-Sensitive (mHSPC)ADT + novel hormonal (abiraterone, apalutamide, enzalutamide, darolutamide) OR docetaxel
Castration-Resistant (mCRPC)Sequential: novel hormonal → other novel hormonal → docetaxel/cabazitaxel → Lu-177 PSMA → radium-223 (bone-only)
[!key]
ADT = GnRH agonist (leuprolide, goserelin) or antagonist (degarelix, relugolix). Anti-androgens (bicalutamide) for flare prevention. PSA nadir <0.1 post-RT/RP = good.

Follow-Up (Post-Treatment)

ParameterFrequency
PSA3mo × 2yr, 6mo × 5yr, then annually
ImagingRestaging if biochemical recurrence (PSA rise ≥0.2 post-RP; nadir +2 post-RT)
Bone HealthDenosumab/zoledronic acid (on ADT); Ca/Vit D

4. Testicular Cancer

Epidemiology & Risk Factors

FeatureDetail
IncidenceCommonest solid cancer in men 15–35yo; rare >50
RiskCryptorchidism (3–5x, even if orchidopexy), family history, prior testis Ca, GCNIS, White ethnicity
GCNISGerm Cell Neoplasia In Situ (pre-invasive); contralateral biopsy if risk factors

Histology (WHO)

Type%Tumour MarkersRadiosensitivity
Seminoma50–60%β-hCG ↑ (10–20%), LDH ↑, AFP NEVERHighly radiosensitive
Non-Seminoma (NSGCT)40–50%AFP ↑, β-hCG ↑, LDH ↑Radioresistant
Mixed—Treat as non-seminomaTreat as non-seminoma

Non-Seminoma Subtypes

SubtypeFeatures
Embryonal CarcinomaMost aggressive; AFP/β-hCG variable
Yolk Sac TumourAFP ↑↑; children
Choriocarcinomaβ-hCG ↑↑↑; highly vascular, early mets
TeratomaMature/immature; markers normal; chemo-resistant
Somatic MalignancySarcoma, carcinoma in teratoma

Tumour Markers (Essential)

MarkerHalf-LifeSeminomaNon-SeminomaUse
AFP5–7 daysNever elevatedElevated (yolk sac, embryonal)Diagnosis, staging, monitoring
β-hCG24–36 hoursMildly elevated (10–20%)Elevated (choriocarcinoma, embryonal)Diagnosis, staging, monitoring
LDH—Elevated (bulk)Elevated (bulk)Prognostic (IGCCCG)
[!key]
AFP NEVER elevated in pure seminoma. If AFP ↑ = non-seminoma component (mixed) → treat as non-seminoma.

Staging (TNM + S for Markers)

StageDefinition
pTisGCNIS
pT1Tumour confined to testis/epididymis (no vascular/lymphatic invasion)
pT2Vascular/lymphatic invasion or tunica vaginalis
pT3Spermatic cord invasion
pT4Scrotal invasion
NRetroperitoneal nodes (pelvic nodes rare)
MDistant (M1a non-regional nodes/lung; M1b other)
SMarker level (S0 normal, S1 mild, S2 moderate, S3 high)

IGCCCG Prognostic Groups (Metastatic)

GroupSeminomaNon-Seminoma
GoodAny primary, no non-pulmonary visceral mets, normal AFPTestis/retroperitoneal primary, no non-pulmonary visceral mets, AFP <1000, β-hCG <5000, LDH <1.5×ULN
IntermediateAny primary, non-pulmonary visceral mets, normal AFPTestis/retro, no non-pulm visceral, AFP 1000–10000, β-hCG 5000–50000, LDH 1.5–10×ULN
PoorNone (seminoma never poor)Mediastinal primary, or non-pulmonary visceral mets, or AFP >10000, β-hCG >50000, LDH >10×ULN

Management

Primary Treatment (All Stages)

StepSeminomaNon-Seminoma
1. Radical Inguinal OrchiectomyStandard (high inguinal, cord ligation); NO trans-scrotalStandard; testis-sparing if bilateral/solitary + small

Stage I

SeminomaNon-Seminoma
Surveillance (preferred; 80% no relapse)Surveillance (preferred low-risk pT1; 70% no relapse)
Adjuvant Carboplatin (AUC 7 × 1–2 cycles)Adjuvant BEP × 1 cycle (pT2–4, LVI+)
Adjuvant RT (para-aortic ± iliac; 20–30Gy) — declining useRPLND (nerve-sparing; staging/therapeutic)

Stage II (Nodal)

SeminomaNon-Seminoma
IIA/IIB (<3cm): RT (para-aortic ± pelvic; 30–36Gy)IIA/IIB: RPLND (nerve-sparing) or BEP × 3 cycles
IIC/III (>3cm): Chemo (BEP × 3–4, or EP × 4)IIC/III: BEP × 3–4 (or VIP)

Stage III (Metastatic)

SeminomaNon-Seminoma
Good/Intermediate: BEP × 3–4 (or EP × 4)Good: BEP × 3; Intermediate/Poor: BEP × 4 (or VIP/HDCT)
Residual mass: PET-CT (if >3cm) → observe if negative; resect if positiveResidual mass: RPLND (residual >1cm or markers normalised + mass)

Chemotherapy Regimens

RegimenComposition
BEPBleomycin 30U D1,8,15 + Etoposide 100mg/m² D1–5 + Cisplatin 20mg/m² D1–5 q3wk
EPEtoposide + Cisplatin (if bleomycin contraindicated)
VIPEtoposide + Ifosfamide + Cisplatin (salvage/poor risk)
TIPPaclitaxel + Ifosfamide + Cisplatin (salvage)
HDCT + ASCTHigh-dose carboplatin/etoposide + auto-SCT (relapsed/refractory)

Post-Chemo Residual Mass

SeminomaNon-Seminoma
Residual >3cm: PET-CT (6–8wk post-chemo)Residual >1cm: RPLND (mandatory if markers normalised)
PET+ve → salvage chemo/resectionRPLND → pathology: necrosis (70%), teratoma (20%), viable (10%)
PET-ve → observeTeratoma = chemotherapy-resistant; viable = needs further chemo

5. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- BPH: transition zone enlargement → LUTS; IPSS scoring; DRE = smooth, enlarged
- BPH Medical: α-blockers (tamsulosin) 1st line; 5-ARI (finasteride) if prostate >30–40g/PSA >1.5
- BPH Surgical: TURP gold standard <80–100g; HoLEP any size/>100g
- Prostate Ca: commonest male cancer; Gleason Grade Groups 1–5; PI-RADS MRI → targeted biopsy
- Prostate Ca Risk: Very Low/Low → Active Surveillance; Intermediate → RP/RT+ADT; High → RP+PLND/RT+ADT
- Metastatic HSPC: ADT + novel hormonal (abi/apa/enz/daro) OR docetaxel
- Testicular Ca: 15–35yo commonest solid cancer; Seminoma 50–60% (radiosensitive, β-hCG↑, AFP never); NSGCT 40–50% (AFP↑, β-hCG↑)
- Orchiectomy: high inguinal, NO trans-scrotal; sentinel node not routine
- Stage I Seminoma: Surveillance > Carboplatin > RT; NSGCT: Surveillance > BEP×1 > RPLND
- Metastatic: Good/Int seminoma = BEP×3–4; NSGCT Good = BEP×3, Int/Poor = BEP×4
- Residual mass: Seminoma >3cm PET-CT; NSGCT >1cm RPLND
- Tumour markers: AFP never in pure seminoma; β-hCG in both; LDH prognostic

6. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
PSA = prostate cancerPSA ↑ in BPH, prostatitis, retention, instrumentation; free:total ratio <10% ↑ specificity
All prostate Ca = surgeryLow/very low risk = Active Surveillance (preferred)
Gleason 3+4 = 4+3Grade Group 2 (3+4) vs 3 (4+3) — different prognosis
Testicular Ca = trans-scrotal biopsyNO trans-scrotal (scrotal violation → upstage); High inguinal orchiectomy
Seminoma = AFP elevatedAFP NEVER in pure seminoma; if AFP ↑ = non-seminoma component
All Stage I = chemoSurveillance preferred (seminoma 80% cure, NSGCT 70% cure pT1)
Seminoma poor riskSeminoma NEVER poor risk (IGCCCG)
Residual mass seminoma = RPLNDSeminoma residual >3cm = PET-CT; NSGCT >1cm = RPLND
BEP = 3 cycles alwaysGood risk NSGCT = BEP×3; Int/Poor = BEP×4
Teratoma = chemo-sensitiveTeratoma = CHEMO-RESISTANT → surgery (RPLND)
GCNIS = biopsy routineContralateral biopsy only if risk factors (cryptorchidism, atrophy, infertility)

7. Mnemonics

  • BPH Zone: Transition Zone = TZ
  • LUTS: Storage (freq, urg, noct), Voiding (hesit, weak, intermit), Post-mict (dribble, incomplete) = SVP
  • IPSS: 0–7 Mild, 8–19 Mod, 20–35 Sev = 0-7-19-35
  • BPH Meds: αlpha-blockers (fast), 5-ARI (slow, big prostate) = α5
  • TURP: TUR Syndrome = hyponatraemia (glycine); Bipolar = saline
  • Prostate Ca Grade: GG1=6, GG2=3+4, GG3=4+3, GG4=8, GG5=9-10 = 1-2-3-4-5
  • Prostate Ca Risk: Very Low, Low, Intermed, High, Very High = VLIHVH
  • Testicular Ca Age: 15–35 = 135
  • Seminoma Markers: AFP Never, β-hCG sometimes, LDH bulk = ANβL
  • Orchiectomy: High Inguinal, NO Trans-scrotal = HINT
  • IGCCCG Good NSGCT: AFP <1000, β-hCG <5000, LDH <1.5×ULN = AβL
  • Residual Mass: Seminoma >3cm PET; NSGCT >1cm RPLND = SP-NR

8. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Male Reproductive Tract Disorders))

BPH

Pathophysiology[Transition zone → BOO]

LUTS[IPSS: Storage, Voiding, Post-mict]

DRE[Smooth, enlarged, median sulcus gone]

Medical[α-blockers (tamsulosin) 1st; 5-ARI if >30-40g/PSA>1.5; Combo]

Surgical[TURP <80-100g gold; HoLEP >100g; UroLift/Rezum]

Complications[TUR syndrome (hyponatraemia), Retrograde ejac, Stricture]

Prostate_Cancer

Screening[PSA shared decision; MRI PI-RADS → targeted bx]

Gleason[GG1=6, GG2=3+4, GG3=4+3, GG4=8, GG5=9-10]

Staging[T1-4, N1, M1a/b/c]

Risk[VLow/Low=AS; Int=RP/RT+ADT; High=RP+PLND/RT+ADT(18-36mo)]

Advanced[mHSPC: ADT+Novel hormonal/Docetaxel; mCRPC: Sequential]

Testicular_Cancer

Age[15-35yo commonest solid]

Histology[Seminoma 50-60% (radiosensitive); NSGCT 40-50%]

Markers[Seminoma: AFP NEVER, β-hCG↑, LDH; NSGCT: AFP↑, β-hCG↑, LDH]

Orchiectomy[High inguinal, cord ligation, NO trans-scrotal]

Stage_I[Seminoma: Surveillance > Carboplatin; NSGCT: Surveillance > BEPx1 > RPLND]

Metastatic[IGCCCG: Good/Int/Poor; Seminoma never Poor]

Chemo[BEP x3 Good NSGCT; BEP x4 Int/Poor; EP if bleo contraindicated]

Residual[Seminoma >3cm PET-CT; NSGCT >1cm RPLND]

Salvage[HDCT+ASCT, TIP, VeIP]

```

9. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. BPH: transition zone, LUTS (IPSS), DRE smooth enlarged
  2. BPH meds: tamsulosin 1st line; finasteride if >30–40g/PSA>1.5
  3. BPH surgery: TURP <80–100g gold; HoLEP >100g
  4. Prostate Ca: Gleason Grade Groups 1–5 (3+3=6, 3+4=7, 4+3=7, 8, 9–10)
  5. Prostate Ca risk: VLow/Low → AS; Intermediate → RP/RT+ADT; High → RP+PLND/RT+ADT
  6. mHSPC: ADT + novel hormonal (abi/apa/enz/daro) OR docetaxel
  7. Testicular Ca: 15–35yo; Seminoma 50–60% (AFP NEVER, radiosensitive); NSGCT 40–50% (AFP↑)
  8. Orchiectomy: high inguinal, NO trans-scrotal
  9. Stage I: Seminoma Surveillance > Carboplatin; NSGCT Surveillance > BEP×1 > RPLND
  10. Metastatic: IGCCCG Good/Int seminoma BEP×3–4; NSGCT Good BEP×3, Int/Poor BEP×4
  11. Residual: Seminoma >3cm PET-CT; NSGCT >1cm RPLND
  12. Tumour markers: AFP NEVER in pure seminoma; β-hCG both; LDH prognostic

10. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

11. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴BPH LUTS/IPSS/DRE, α-blockers/5-ARI/combo, TURP/HoLEP; Prostate Ca Gleason/Grade Groups, risk stratification, AS, RP/RT+ADT, mHSPC ADT+novel hormonal; Testicular Ca age/histology/markers, orchiectomy, stage I management, IGCCCG, BEP cycles, residual mass management
Should Know 🟡PI-RADS/MRI, PI-RADS 3 management, PSA kinetics, ADT side effects/bone health, abiraterone/apalutamide/enzalutamide/darolutamide, RPLND nerve-sparing, PET-CT in seminoma, HDCT+ASCT salvage, GCNIS biopsy
Nice to Know 🟢Focal therapy (HIFU, cryo), PSMA PET/CT, Lu-177 PSMA, novel biomarkers (miRNA, ctDNA), quality of life, fertility preservation, testicular prosthesis, cost-effectiveness

12. MCQs (10)

  1. BPH arises from which prostatic zone?

A. Peripheral zone

B. Transition zone

C. Central zone

D. Anterior fibromuscular stroma

E. All zones equally

  1. First-line medical therapy for BPH/LUTS:

A. Finasteride

B. Tamsulosin (α-blocker)

C. Dutasteride

D. Tadalafil

E. Oxybutynin

  1. TURP syndrome is caused by:

A. Hypernatraemia

B. Hyponatraemia (glycine absorption)

C. Hyperkalaemia

D. Hypocalcaemia

E. Hypoglycaemia

  1. Gleason Grade Group 3 corresponds to:

A. 3+3=6

B. 3+4=7

C. 4+3=7

D. 4+4=8

E. 4+5=9

  1. Very low / low risk localised prostate cancer — preferred management:

A. Radical prostatectomy

B. Radiotherapy

C. Active Surveillance

D. ADT alone

E. Focal therapy

  1. Metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC) — standard of care:

A. ADT alone

B. ADT + novel hormonal (abiraterone/apalutamide/enzalutamide/darolutamide) OR docetaxel

C. ADT + radiotherapy

D. Docetaxel alone

E. Radium-223

  1. Commonest solid cancer in men aged 15–35:

A. Prostate cancer

B. Testicular cancer

C. Lung cancer

D. Colorectal cancer

E. Lymphoma

  1. Pure seminoma — tumour marker NEVER elevated:

A. β-hCG

B. LDH

C. AFP

D. Both β-hCG and AFP

E. All can be elevated

  1. Radical orchiectomy for testicular cancer — approach:

A. Trans-scrotal

B. High inguinal (cord ligation at internal ring)

C. Laparoscopic transperitoneal

D. Scrotal with testis delivery

E. Trans-scrotal with frozen section

  1. Good risk metastatic non-seminoma (IGCCCG) — chemotherapy:

A. BEP × 4 cycles

B. BEP × 3 cycles

C. EP × 4 cycles

D. VIP × 4 cycles

E. Carboplatin AUC 7 × 2

13. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 65-year-old man, LUTS (IPSS 18), prostate 45g on DRE, PSA 2.8. Best initial medical therapy:

A. Finasteride 5mg

B. Tamsulosin 0.4mg OD

C. Tamsulosin + dutasteride

D. Tadalafil 5mg

E. Oxybutynin

  1. 70-year-old man, IPSS 22, prostate 60g, PSA 4.5, failed tamsulosin 3 months. Next step:

A. Add dutasteride

B. Add dutasteride (prostate >30–40g, PSA >1.5) or switch to combination

C. Switch to silodosin

D. Refer for TURP immediately

E. Add tadalafil

  1. 55-year-old man, PSA 6.2, DRE: right lobe nodule. mpMRI: PI-RADS 4 lesion. Targeted biopsy: GG2 (3+4=7) in 2/12 cores, 20% involvement. PSA density 0.12. Risk group:

A. Very low

B. Low

C. Intermediate favourable

D. Intermediate unfavourable

E. High

  1. Same patient, chooses radical prostatectomy. Pathology: pT2c, GG2, negative margins, pN0. PSA at 3 months: 0.05 ng/mL. Significance:

A. Biochemical recurrence

B. PSA nadir <0.1 = favourable; continue surveillance

C. Incomplete resection

D. Need adjuvant RT

E. Need ADT

  1. 75-year-old man, metastatic prostate cancer to bone, PSA 150, fit. mHSPC — 1st line:

A. ADT alone

B. ADT + abiraterone (or apalutamide/enzalutamide/darolutamide) OR docetaxel

C. Docetaxel alone

D. Radium-223

E. Sipuleucel-T

  1. 22-year-old man, painless right testicular lump 3cm. AFP 1200, β-hCG 50, LDH 300. Ultrasound: solid intratesticular mass. Management:

A. Trans-scrotal biopsy

B. Radical inguinal orchiectomy (high inguinal, cord ligation)

C. Scrotal ultrasound guided biopsy

D. MRI pelvis

E. Serum markers only

  1. Same patient, orchiectomy: mixed germ cell tumour (seminoma + embryonal). Staging CT: 2cm retroperitoneal node. pT2N1M0S1. Treatment:

A. Surveillance

B. BEP × 3 cycles (good risk NSGCT)

C. BEP × 4 cycles

D. RT to retroperitoneum

E. RPLND

  1. 30-year-old man, pure seminoma, 4cm retroperitoneal node post-orchiectomy. Markers normalising. Treatment:

A. Surveillance

B. RT (para-aortic ± pelvic) OR Carboplatin AUC 7 × 2

C. BEP × 4

D. RPLND

E. High-dose chemo

  1. Non-seminoma, post-chemo residual retroperitoneal mass 2.5cm, markers normal. Management:

A. PET-CT

B. RPLND (mandatory if >1cm and markers normal)

C. Surveillance

D. Radiotherapy

E. Further BEP cycle

  1. Post-chemo residual mass in seminoma 4cm, markers normal. PET-CT: SUV 1.2 (background). Management:

A. RPLND

B. Observe (PET-negative)

C. Salvage chemotherapy

C. Radiotherapy

E. RPLND + adjuvant chemo

  1. Testicular cancer — contralateral testis biopsy (GCNIS) indicated if:

A. All patients

B. Risk factors: cryptorchidism, atrophy (<12mL), infertility, prior GCNIS

C. Only seminoma

D. Only non-seminoma

E. Never indicated

14. Flashcards

  • Q: BPH zone?

A: Transition zone

  • Q: BPH 1st line drug?

A: Tamsulosin (α-blocker)

  • Q: 5-ARI indication?

A: Prostate >30–40g or PSA >1.5

  • Q: TURP syndrome?

A: Hyponatraemia (glycine absorption)

  • Q: Gleason GG1–5?

A: 1=3+3=6, 2=3+4=7, 3=4+3=7, 4=8, 5=9–10

  • Q: Prostate Ca very low/low risk?

A: Active Surveillance

  • Q: mHSPC 1st line?

A: ADT + novel hormonal (abi/apa/enz/daro) OR docetaxel

  • Q: Testicular Ca peak age?

A: 15–35yo

  • Q: Seminoma markers?

A: AFP NEVER, β-hCG sometimes, LDH bulk

  • Q: NSGCT markers?

A: AFP↑, β-hCG↑, LDH↑

  • Q: Orchiectomy approach?

A: High inguinal, NO trans-scrotal

  • Q: Stage I seminoma?

A: Surveillance > Carboplatin > RT

  • Q: Stage I NSGCT?

A: Surveillance > BEP×1 > RPLND

  • Q: IGCCCG Good NSGCT?

A: AFP<1000, β-hCG<5000, LDH<1.5×ULN

  • Q: BEP cycles Good NSGCT?

A: BEP × 3

  • Q: BEP cycles Int/Poor NSGCT?

A: BEP × 4

  • Q: Residual seminoma >3cm?

A: PET-CT

  • Q: Residual NSGCT >1cm?

A: RPLND

  • Q: Teratoma chemo?

A: RESISTANT → surgery

  • Q: GCNIS biopsy?

A: Only if risk factors (cryptorchidism, atrophy, infertility)

15. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — BPH = transition zone enlargement
  2. B — α-blockers (tamsulosin) = 1st line for LUTS
  3. B — TURP syndrome = hyponatraemia from glycine absorption
  4. C — GG3 = 4+3=7 (predominant pattern 4)
  5. C — Very low/low risk = Active Surveillance preferred
  6. B — mHSPC = ADT + novel hormonal (STAMPEDE, LATITUDE, etc.) OR docetaxel
  7. B — Testicular cancer = commonest solid cancer 15–35yo
  8. C — AFP NEVER in pure seminoma
  9. B — High inguinal orchiectomy with cord ligation
  10. B — Good risk NSGCT = BEP × 3

SBAs

  1. B — IPSS 18 (moderate), prostate 45g, PSA 2.8 → tamsulosin 1st line
  2. B — Failed α-blocker, prostate >30g, PSA >1.5 → add 5-ARI (dutasteride)
  3. C — GG2, <50% cores, PSA density <0.15 → intermediate favourable
  4. B — PSA nadir <0.1 post-RP = favourable
  5. B — mHSPC = ADT + novel hormonal (abiraterone etc.) OR docetaxel
  6. B — High inguinal orchiectomy = standard; NO trans-scrotal
  7. B — pT2N1M0S1 = Stage IIA/IIB good risk NSGCT → BEP × 3
  8. B — Stage IIA/IIB seminoma = RT or carboplatin
  9. B — NSGCT residual >1cm + normal markers = RPLND
  10. B — Seminoma residual >3cm PET-negative = observe
  11. B — Contralateral biopsy only if risk factors (cryptorchidism, atrophy, infertility)

16. Summary

Male Reproductive Tract Disorders is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: BPH: transition zone → LUTS; IPSS; tamsulosin 1st line; finasteride if >30–40g/PSA>1.5; TURP <80–100g gold, HoLEP >100g. Prostate Ca: Gleason Grade Groups 1–5; Risk: VLow/Low → AS; Intermediate → RP/RT+ADT; High → RP+PLND/RT+ADT(18–36mo); mHSPC: ADT + novel hormonal (abiraterone/apalutamide/enzalutamide/darolutamide) OR docetaxel. Testicular Ca: 15–35yo; Seminoma 50–60% (AFP NEVER, radiosensitive); NSGCT 40–50% (AFP↑); High inguinal orchiectomy (NO trans-scrotal); Stage I: Surveillance > Carboplatin (sem) / BEP×1/RPLND (NSGCT); Metastatic: IGCCCG Good/Int seminoma BEP×3–4; NSGCT Good BEP×3, Int/Poor BEP×4; Residual: Seminoma >3cm PET-CT; NSGCT >1cm RPLND; Teratoma = chemo-resistant.

Exam focus: BPH management algorithm, prostate cancer Gleason/Grade Groups/risk stratification/AS, mHSPC treatment, testicular cancer age/histology/markers, orchiectomy approach, stage I management, IGCCCG groups, BEP cycles, residual mass protocols.

Clinical relevance: Active surveillance avoids overtreatment in prostate cancer; testicular cancer highly curable with multidisciplinary approach; BPH very common, stepwise management improves QoL.

topics.title: Priapism
topics.html:
Topic: Priapism — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Male Reproductive Tract Disorders (BPH, Prostate Cancer, Testicular Cancer)]], [[Sickle Cell Disease]], [[Anticoagulation]], [[Neurology]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
Priapism = prolonged penile erection (>4 hours) unrelated to sexual stimulation. UROLOGICAL EMERGENCY. Types: Ischaemic (low-flow, 95%) = corporal hypoxia → urgent aspiration/irrigation ± shunting; Non-ischaemic (high-flow, 5%) = arterial fistula → conservative/embolisation. Stuttering = recurrent ischaemic episodes. Sickle cell disease = most common cause in children/young men. PDE5 inhibitors, anticoagulants, antipsychotics = drug causes.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate ischaemic vs non-ischaemic priapism (clinical, blood gas, Doppler)
  • Apply time-critical management for ischaemic priapism (aspiration, irrigation, phenylephrine, shunting)
  • Manage non-ischaemic priapism conservatively/embolisation
  • Recognise sickle cell priapism specifics (exchange transfusion, hydroxyurea prophylaxis)
  • Identify drug-induced causes and management
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP emergency urology vignettes

2. Classification

TypeMechanismBlood FlowAgeUrgency
Ischaemic (Low-flow)Venous occlusion → corporal hypoxia/acidosisLow/StagnantAll (peak 30–40, sickle cell: children)Emergency (>4hr = permanent damage)
Non-ischaemic (High-flow)Arterial-lacunar fistula (trauma) → unregulated arterial inflowHighPost-perineal traumaSemi-elective
Stuttering (Recurrent Ischaemic)Recurrent self-limiting ischaemic episodesIntermittentSickle cell, idiopathicPrevent progression

3. Ischaemic Priapism (Low-Flow) — Emergency

Aetiology

CategoryCauses
Idiopathic~30–50%
Sickle Cell DiseaseMost common in children/adolescents (HbSS, HbSC, HbSβ-thal)
Drug-InducedPDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil), Anticoagulants (warfarin, heparin, DOACs), Antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine, chlorpromazine), Antidepressants (trazodone, SSRIs), Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin), Cocaine, Alcohol
MalignancyLeukaemia, metastatic infiltration
NeurologicalSpinal cord injury, herniated disc, tumours
InfectionsSepsis, UTI

Clinical Presentation

FeatureIschaemicNon-ischaemic
PainSevere, progressivePainless or mild
ErectionRigid corporal bodies, glans soft (glans not involved)Non-rigid, tumescent, glans may be engorged
Duration>4 hours (often hours–days)Hours to days
Corpus spongiosum/glansSoft (not involved)May be engorged
Penile DopplerLow/absent cavernosal flowHigh arterial flow
Cavernosal Blood GaspH <7.25, pO₂ <30, pCO₂ >60 (acidosis)Normal arterial values
Glans soft + rigid corpora = ischaemic priapism (corpus spongiosum spared).

Cavernosal Blood Gas (Diagnostic)

ParameterIschaemicNormal Arterial
pH<7.25 (acidosis)7.35–7.45
pO₂<30 mmHg (hypoxia)80–100 mmHg
pCO₂>60 mmHg (hypercapnia)35–45 mmHg

4. Management — Ischaemic Priapism (Algorithm)

Stepwise Approach (Time-Critical)

TimeIntervention
0–4 hoursConservative: analgesia, ice packs, gentle exercise, oral terbutaline/sudafed (α-agonist)
>4 hoursEMERGENCY: Corporal aspiration + irrigation + intracavernosal phenylephrine
Failed aspirationSurgical shunt (distal → proximal)
Sickle CellHydration, oxygenation, analgesia, exchange transfusion ± aspiration/shunt

1. Corporal Aspiration & Irrigation

StepDetail
AnaesthesiaPenile block (dorsal nerve) or local infiltration
Aspiration19G butterfly needle at 2 & 10 o'clock (corpora cavernosa) — aspirate 20–50mL dark blood
IrrigationNormal saline 50–100mL per side until effluent clear
Phenylephrine100–500 μg/mL (dilute 1mg in 100–200mL NS) — inject 1mL q3–5min × max 1mg (monitor BP/HR)
RepeatAspirate/irrigate q10–15min if detumescence incomplete
Phenylephrine: α₁-agonist → corporal smooth muscle contraction. Monitor BP/HR (hypertension, bradycardia, arrhythmia). Contraindicated in severe CAD, uncontrolled HTN.

2. Surgical Shunt (If Aspiration Fails @ 1 hour)

Shunt TypeDescriptionSuccess Rate
Distal (Glans-Corporal)Winter (biopsy punch 3–5mm at glans-corpus junction), Ebbehøj (incision)60–80%
Al-Ghorab (excision of tunica at glans-corpus)70–85%
Proximal (Corporospongiosal / Cavernosospongiosal)Quackels (corpus-spongiosum fistula), Grayhack (corpus-spongiosum with saphenous vein graft)70–90%
Eberhard (cavernosospongiosal with graft)70–85%
Sequence: Distal first (Winter/Ebbehøj/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal if distal fails.

3. Sickle Cell Priapism Specifics

InterventionDetail
HydrationIV fluids (1.5–2x maintenance)
Oxygen100% O₂ (reduce sickling)
AnalgesiaOpioids (avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment)
Exchange TransfusionTarget HbS <30%, Hb >10g/dL — if aspiration fails or recurrent
HydroxyureaProphylaxis for recurrent/stuttering
Aspiration/ShuntSame as above; lower threshold for exchange

5. Non-Ischaemic Priapism (High-Flow)

Aetiology

  • Perineal/penile trauma (straddle injury, pelvic fracture) → arterial-lacunar fistula
  • Iatrogenic (penile injection, surgery)

Clinical

  • Painless/tender, non-rigid tumescence
  • Often post-trauma history
  • Doppler: High arterial flow (arterialised blood gas)

Management

ApproachDetail
Observation25–50% spontaneous resolution
Selective Arterial EmbolisationDefinitive (superselective angiography + coils/particles) — 90% success
Surgical LigationIf embolisation fails/unavailable
CompressionPerineal pressure (temporary)

6. Stuttering (Recurrent Ischaemic) Priapism

  • Recurrent self-limiting episodes (<4hr each)
  • High risk of progression to fulminant ischaemic priapism
  • Aetiology: Sickle cell (most common), idiopathic
  • Prophylaxis: Hydroxyurea (sickle cell), PDE5 inhibitors (some evidence), GnRH agonists, Baclofen, Terbutaline

7. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- Priapism >4hr = urological emergency.
- Ischaemic (95%) = rigid corpora, soft glans, painful, low flow, acidic blood gas (pH<7.25, pO₂<30).
- Non-ischaemic (5%) = painless, non-rigid, high flow, post-trauma.
- Ischaemic >4hr: Aspiration + irrigation + phenylephrine (emergency).
- Phenylephrine: 100–500 μg/mL, 1mL q3–5min, max 1mg; monitor BP/HR.
- Failed aspiration → shunt: Distal (Winter/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal.
- Sickle cell: Hydration, O₂, analgesia, exchange transfusion (HbS<30%), hydroxyurea prophylaxis.
- Non-ischaemic: Post-trauma, painless, non-rigid → embolisation (90% success).
- Stuttering: Recurrent ischaemic → hydroxyurea prophylaxis.

8. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
All priapism = ischaemic5% = non-ischaemic (high flow, post-trauma, painless)
Glans rigid in ischaemicGlans SOFT in ischaemic (corpus spongiosum spared); glans rigid in non-ischaemic
Ice packs first for ischaemicIce OK adjunct but aspiration/irrigation is definitive after 4hr
Phenylephrine = no monitoringMonitor BP/HR (hypertension, bradycardia, arrhythmia)
Sickle cell = same as idiopathicLower threshold for exchange transfusion; hydroxyurea prophylaxis
Non-ischaemic = emergency surgeryObservation/embolisation first; surgery if embolisation fails
All priapism >4hr = shuntAspiration/irrigation/phenylephrine FIRST (60–80% success)
Oral terbutaline = definitiveOnly adjunct <4hr; >4hr = aspiration
Glans involvement = ischaemicGlans soft = ischaemic; glans engorged = non-ischaemic
Priapism in children = only sickle cellAlso idiopathic, trauma, drugs, leukaemia

9. Mnemonics

  • Ischaemic features: Rigid corpus, Soft glans, Painful, Acidic blood = RSPA
  • Blood gas ischaemic: pH Low, pO₂ Low, pCO₂ High = Low Low High
  • Ischaemic stepwise: Aspiration → Irrigation → Phenylephrine → Shunt = AIPS
  • Phenylephrine: Phenylephrine = Pressure monitor (BP/HR)
  • Shunt sequence: Distal (Winter/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal = DP
  • Sickle cell: Hydration, Oxygen, Analgesia, Exchange = HOAE

10. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Priapism))

Ischaemic[Low-Flow 95%]

Features[Rigid corpora, Soft glans, Painful, pH<7.25, pO2<30]

Causes[Sickle cell, Drugs (PDE5i, antipsychotics, anticoagulants), Idiopathic, Leukaemia]

Timeline[>4hr = Emergency]

Rx

Aspiration[19G needle, aspirate dark blood]

Irrigation[Saline until clear]

Phenylephrine[100-500mcg/mL, q3-5min, max 1mg, MONITOR BP/HR]

Shunt[Distal (Winter/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal]

Sickle[Hydration, O2, Analgesia, Exchange Transfusion (HbS<30%)]

Non_Ischaemic[High-Flow 5%]

Features[Non-rigid, Painless, High flow, Post-trauma]

Rx[Observation → Embolisation (90%) → Surgery]

Stuttering[Recurrent Ischaemic]

Rx[Hydroxyurea, PDE5i, GnRH agonists, Terbutaline]

```

11. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. Ischaemic vs non-ischaemic differentiation (rigid/soft glans, pain, flow, blood gas)
  2. Ischaemic blood gas (pH<7.25, pO₂<30, pCO₂>60)
  3. Time threshold (>4hr = emergency)
  4. Aspiration → Irrigation → Phenylephrine → Shunt
  5. Phenylephrine dose (100–500 μg/mL, max 1mg) + BP/HR monitoring
  6. Shunt sequence: Distal (Winter/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal
  7. Sickle cell: Exchange transfusion (HbS<30%)
  8. Non-ischaemic: Embolisation (90%)
  9. Stuttering: Hydroxyurea prophylaxis

12. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

13. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴Ischaemic vs non-ischaemic, >4hr emergency, aspiration/irrigation/phenylephrine, shunt sequence, sickle cell exchange transfusion, non-ischaemic embolisation
Should Know 🟡Phenylephrine monitoring, distal vs proximal shunt indications, stuttering prophylaxis, drug-induced causes, long-term erectile dysfunction risk
Nice to Know 🟢Novel shunts (biodegradable), gene therapy for sickle cell, pharmacokinetic models for phenylephrine, quality of life outcomes

14. MCQs (10)

  1. Key distinguishing feature between ischaemic and non-ischaemic priapism:

A. Duration

B. Glans involvement (soft in ischaemic, engorged in non-ischaemic)

C. Age of onset

D. Pain severity

E. Time of day

  1. Cavernosal blood gas in ischaemic priapism:

A. pH 7.40, pO₂ 80, pCO₂ 40

B. pH <7.25, pO₂ <30, pCO₂ >60

C. pH 7.30, pO₂ 50, pCO₂ 45

D. pH 7.35, pO₂ 60, pCO₂ 50

E. pH 7.20, pO₂ 100, pCO₂ 30

  1. Time threshold for emergency intervention in ischaemic priapism:

A. 2 hours

B. 4 hours

C. 6 hours

D. 8 hours

E. 12 hours

  1. First-line emergency management for ischaemic priapism >4 hours:

A. Oral terbutaline

B. Corporal aspiration + irrigation + intracavernosal phenylephrine

C. Immediate surgical shunt

D. Ice packs only

E. Observation

  1. Phenylephrine concentration for intracavernosal injection:

A. 1–10 μg/mL

B. 100–500 μg/mL

C. 1–5 mg/mL

C. 10 mg/mL

E. 100 mg/mL

  1. Maximum total dose of intracavernosal phenylephrine:

A. 200 μg

B. 500 μg

C. 1 mg

D. 2 mg

E. 5 mg

  1. Sickle cell priapism — definitive disease-modifying intervention for recurrent episodes:

A. Repeated aspiration

B. Hydroxyurea prophylaxis

C. Chronic transfusion

D. PDE5 inhibitors

E. GnRH agonists

  1. Non-ischaemic priapism — definitive management after failed observation:

A. Aspiration

B. Selective arterial embolisation

C. Distal shunt

C. Phenylephrine

E. Oral terbutaline

  1. Stuttering priapism — most common association:

A. Trauma

B. Sickle cell disease

C. PDE5 inhibitors

D. Anticoagulation

E. Spinal cord injury

  1. Glans penis in ischaemic priapism:

A. Rigid

B. Soft (spared)

C. Partially rigid

D. Fluctuant

E. Necrotic

15. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 28-year-old man, sickle cell disease (HbSS), 5-hour painful erection. Corpora rigid, glans soft. Cavernosal gas: pH 7.15, pO₂ 15, pCO₂ 75. First intervention:

A. Oral hydration + analgesia

B. Corporal aspiration + irrigation + phenylephrine

C. Winter shunt

D. Exchange transfusion first

E. Observation

  1. 35-year-old man, 6-hour erection after sildenafil use. Rigid corpora, soft glans, severe pain. Failed 2 rounds of aspiration/phenylephrine. Next step:

A. Increase phenylephrine dose

B. Distal shunt (Winter or Al-Ghorab)

C. Proximal shunt

D. Exchange transfusion

D. Observation

  1. 10-year-old boy, HbSS, 3-hour painful erection. Corpora rigid, glans soft. Cavernosal gas: pH 7.20. Initial management:

A. Ice packs

B. Aspiration + irrigation + phenylephrine + hydration + O₂ + analgesia

C. Exchange transfusion immediately

D. Winter shunt

E. Oral terbutaline

  1. 40-year-old man, straddle injury 2 weeks ago, now painless non-rigid erection. Doppler: high arterial flow. Management:

A. Aspiration + phenylephrine

B. Observation → selective arterial embolisation if persistent

C. Winter shunt

D. Exchange transfusion

E. Ice packs

  1. Sickle cell patient with recurrent stuttering priapism (≥3 episodes/month). Best prophylaxis:

A. Daily PDE5 inhibitor

B. Hydroxyurea (titrate to MTD)

C. Chronic exchange transfusion

D. GnRH agonist

E. Terbutaline PRN

  1. Ischaemic priapism >4hr — phenylephrine injection protocol:

A. 1mg bolus once

B. 1mL of 100–500mcg/mL q3–5min × max 1mg, monitor BP/HR

C. 5mg bolus

D. Continuous infusion

E. 100mcg/mL once

  1. Non-ischaemic priapism post-trauma — definitive treatment:

A. Aspiration

B. Superselective arterial embolisation (90% success)

C. Distal shunt

D. Phenylephrine

E. Oral alpha-blocker

  1. Sickle cell priapism >4hr, failed aspiration/phenylephrine. Next step:

A. Proximal shunt

B. Exchange transfusion (target HbS<30%) ± distal shunt

C. Observation

D. High-dose steroids

E. PDE5 inhibitor

  1. Priapism in child — most common cause:

A. Trauma

B. Sickle cell disease

C. PDE5 inhibitor

D. Antipsychotic

E. Leukaemia

  1. Monitoring during intracavernosal phenylephrine:

A. Serial cavernosal gas

B. Blood pressure and heart rate (hypertension, bradycardia, arrhythmia)

C. Urine output

D. Temperature

E. Oxygen saturation

16. Flashcards

  • Q: Ischaemic vs non-ischaemic key diff?

A: Glans soft (ischaemic) vs engorged (non-ischaemic)

  • Q: Ischaemic blood gas?

A: pH<7.25, pO₂<30, pCO₂>60

  • Q: Emergency threshold?

A: >4 hours

  • Q: First-line ischaemic >4hr?

A: Aspiration + irrigation + phenylephrine

  • Q: Phenylephrine concentration?

A: 100–500 μg/mL

  • Q: Phenylephrine max dose?

A: 1 mg total

  • Q: Phenylephrine monitoring?

A: BP and HR

  • Q: Shunt sequence?

A: Distal (Winter/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal

  • Q: Sickle cell exchange target?

A: HbS <30%

  • Q: Non-ischaemic definitive?

A: Arterial embolisation

  • Q: Stuttering prophylaxis?

A: Hydroxyurea

  • Q: Glans in ischaemic?

A: Soft

17. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — Corpus spongiosum/glans spared in ischaemic (venous occlusion of corpora cavernosa only); engorged in non-ischaemic (arterial inflow).
  2. B — Ischaemic = acidosis (pH<7.25), hypoxia (pO₂<30), hypercapnia (pCO₂>60).
  3. B — >4 hours = emergency intervention threshold to prevent permanent fibrosis/impotence.
  4. B — Stepwise: aspiration → irrigation → phenylephrine (not shunt first).
  5. B — Standard dilution: 1mg phenylephrine in 100–200mL NS = 100–500 μg/mL.
  6. C — Max cumulative dose 1mg (prevent systemic α₁-effects: hypertension, bradycardia, arrhythmia).
  7. B — Hydroxyurea ↑ HbF → ↓ sickling → reduces stuttering episodes (proven in sickle cell).
  8. B — Non-ischaemic = arterial fistula → superselective embolisation (coils/particles) = 90% success.
  9. B — Stuttering priapism most commonly associated with sickle cell disease.
  10. B — Ischaemic = corpora cavernosa rigid, glans soft (corpus spongiosum not involved).

SBAs

  1. B — Sickle cell, >4hr, rigid corpora/soft glans, acidic gas = emergency aspiration/irrigation/phenylephrine.
  2. B — Failed 2 rounds of aspiration/phenylephrine → distal shunt (Winter/Al-Ghorab).
  3. B — Paediatric sickle cell: aspiration/irrigation/phenylephrine + aggressive hydration/O₂/analgesia; exchange if fails.
  4. B — Non-ischaemic (painless, high flow, post-trauma): observation first, then embolisation.
  5. B — Stuttering sickle cell priapism: hydroxyurea prophylaxis (↑ HbF, ↓ sickling).
  6. B — Standard protocol: 1mL of 100–500mcg/mL q3–5min, max 1mg total, continuous BP/HR monitoring.
  7. B — Superselective arterial embolisation = gold standard for non-ischaemic (90% success).
  8. B — Sickle cell + failed medical Rx → exchange transfusion (HbS<30%) ± surgical shunt.
  9. B — Paediatric priapism: sickle cell disease is #1 cause.
  10. B — Phenylephrine α₁-effects: hypertension, reflex bradycardia, arrhythmia → monitor BP/HR.

18. Summary

Priapism is a Must Know 🔴 urological emergency.

Ischaemic (Low-flow, 95%): Rigid corpora, soft glans, painful, blood gas pH<7.25, pO₂<30, pCO₂>60. Causes: sickle cell, drugs (PDE5i, antipsychotics, anticoagulants), idiopathic. >4hr = emergency.

Stepwise Rx: Aspiration + irrigation + phenylephrine (100–500 μg/mL, q3–5min, max 1mg, monitor BP/HR) → if fails → Distal shunt (Winter/Al-Ghorab) → Proximal shunt.

Sickle Cell: Hydration, O₂, analgesia, exchange transfusion (HbS<30%), hydroxyurea prophylaxis for stuttering.

Non-ischaemic (High-flow, 5%): Post-trauma, painless, non-rigid, high flow → embolisation (90% success).

Stuttering: Recurrent ischaemic episodes → hydroxyurea prophylaxis.

Exam focus: Ischaemic vs non-ischaemic differentiation, >4hr emergency, aspiration/irrigation/phenylephrine protocol, phenylephrine monitoring, shunt sequence, sickle cell exchange target, non-ischaemic embolisation, glans soft in ischaemic.

topics.title: Renal Replacement Therapy Dialysis
topics.html:
Topic: Renal Replacement Therapy — Dialysis (HD, PD), Indications, Complications — Nephrology & Urology
Style: Sea Knowledge study infographic
Audience: FCPS / MRCP exam prep

Related: [[Acute Kidney Injury (AKI)]], [[Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)]], [[Kidney Transplantation (Indications, Workup, Immunosuppression, Complications)]], [[Blood Tests in Renal Disease]], [[Nephrology and Urology MOC]]

[!important]
RRT = life-sustaining therapy for ESRD (G5) or severe AKI (AEIOU). Modalities: Intermittent Haemodialysis (IHD), Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT), Peritoneal Dialysis (PD). Indications: AEIOU. Vascular access: AVF (gold standard), AVG, CVC. Complications: access-related, metabolic, infections, cardiovascular.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Identify indications for RRT (AEIOU mnemonic)
  • Compare HD, CRRT, PD modalities
  • Understand vascular access types and complications
  • Manage dialysis complications (intradialytic hypotension, disequilibrium, access issues)
  • Apply to FCPS/MRCP clinical scenarios

2. Indications for RRT (AEIOU Mnemonic)

LetterIndicationDetails
AAcidosispH <7.2, HCO₃⁻ <12–15 mmol/L refractory to medical therapy
EElectrolytesK+ >6.5 mmol/L or ECG changes refractory to medical therapy
IIngestionToxic alcohols (methanol, ethylene glycol), lithium, salicylates, metformin
OOverloadPulmonary oedema refractory to diuretics; pericardial effusion/tamponade
UUraemiaPericarditis, encephalopathy, neuropathy, bleeding diathesis, refractory nausea/vomiting
[!key]
AEIOU = absolute indications for urgent RRT. Chronic ESRD (G5) = planned RRT start.

3. Dialysis Modalities Comparison

FeatureIHD (Intermittent HD)CRRT (CVVH/CVVHDF)PD (Peritoneal Dialysis)
Schedule3–4h × 3/weekContinuous 24/7Continuous (CAPD) or nightly (APD)
SettingHospital/satellite unitICUHome
HaemodynamicsLarge fluid shifts → hypotension riskStable (slow fluid removal)Stable
ClearanceHigh (intermittent)Lower (continuous)Lowest (continuous)
AnticoagulationHeparin/citrate (circuit)Citrate (regional) / heparinNone
Solute RemovalDiffusion dominantConvection (CVVH) / Mixed (CVVHDF)Diffusion
Fluid RemovalUltrafiltration (UF)UF (controlled)UF (glucose gradient)
IndicationsStable ESRD, AKI (if stable)Haemodynamically unstable AKI, sepsis, fluid overloadESRD (patient choice), children, vascular access failure
AccessAVF/AVG/CVCCVC (large bore)PD catheter (Tenckhoff)

4. Haemodialysis (IHD) — Detailed

Vascular Access

TypeDescriptionMaturationLongevityComplications
AVF (Arteriovenous Fistula)Radiocephalic (wrist) → Brachiocephalic (elbow) → Brachiobasilic (upper arm)6–8 weeksYears (gold standard)Stenosis, thrombosis, aneurysm, steal syndrome
AVG (Arteriovenous Graft)PTFE loop (usually upper arm)2–3 weeks1–3 yearsHigher infection/thrombosis than AVF
CVC (Central Venous Catheter)Tunnelled (Permanent) or Non-tunnelled (Temporary)ImmediateWeeks–monthsInfection (CRBSI), thrombosis, stenosis, fibrin sheath
[!key]
AVF = gold standard (radiocephalic first). CVC = last resort (high infection). Fistula First initiative.

HD Prescription (Typical)

ParameterTypical Value
Blood Flow (Qb)300–400 mL/min
Dialysate Flow (Qd)500–800 mL/min
Duration3–4 hours × 3/week
DialyserHigh-flux (β2-microglobulin clearance)
AnticoagulationUnfractionated heparin (bolus + infusion) or citrate (regional)
DialysateK+ 1–2 mmol/L (adjust), Ca 1.25–1.5 mmol/L, Bicarbonate 32–35 mmol/L, Glucose 5.5 mmol/L

HD Adequacy

MeasureTarget
spKt/V (single-pool)≥1.2 per session (minimum); ≥1.4 preferred
URR (Urea Reduction Ratio)≥65% (preferably ≥70%)
eKt/V≥1.0 (equilibrated)
FrequencyMonthly

5. Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy (CRRT)

Modalities

ModalityMechanismBest For
CVVH (Continuous Veno-Venous Haemofiltration)Convection (solvent drag)Middle molecules, fluid removal
CVVHD (Continuous Veno-Venous Haemodialysis)DiffusionSmall molecules
CVVHDF (Continuous Veno-Venous Haemodiafiltration)Convection + DiffusionMost common — balanced clearance

Prescription

ParameterTypical Value
Blood Flow100–200 mL/min
Effluent Flow (Dose)20–25 mL/kg/h (KDIGO: 20–25 mL/kg/h)
AnticoagulationRegional citrate (preferred) — target post-filter ionised Ca 0.25–0.35 mmol/L; Systemic heparin if citrate contraindicated
Filter Life24–72 hours (goal >24h)

Indications for CRRT over IHD

  • Haemodynamic instability (vasopressors, shock)
  • Severe fluid overload (need precise UF control)
  • Raised ICP (avoid rapid osmolar shifts)
  • Sepsis (cytokine clearance — evidence limited)
  • Lack of vascular access for IHD (large CVC for CRRT)

6. Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)

Types

TypeDescription
CAPD (Continuous Ambulatory PD)4 exchanges/day (manual); dwell 4–6h day, 8–10h night
APD (Automated PD)Cycler at night (8–10h); 1–2 daytime dwells (last fill)

PD Catheter & Solutions

ComponentDetails
CatheterTenckhoff (double cuff); inserted laparoscopically/open/percutaneously; 2-week break-in before full use
SolutionsGlucose-based (1.36%, 2.27%, 3.86%) — osmotic agent; Icodextrin (7.5%) — long dwell (colloid osmotic); Bicarbonate/lactate buffer
Dwell PhasesFill → Dwell → Drain

PD Adequacy

MeasureTarget
Weekly Kt/V≥1.7
Weekly Creatinine Clearance≥45 L/week/1.73m²
Residual Renal FunctionCritical — preserves urine output, volume control, middle molecule clearance

PD Complications

ComplicationManagement
PeritonitisCloudy effluent + abdominal pain; Cell count >100/μL (neutrophils >50%); Empiric IP antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime/aminoglycoside); Culture-directed; Catheter removal if refractory/fungal/multiple
Exit-site/Tunnel InfectionTopical + systemic antibiotics; catheter removal if tunnel
HerniaInguinal, umbilical, incisional; surgical repair
Ultrafiltration FailureHigh transporter (fast glucose absorption); icodextrin for long dwell; consider HD
Encapsulating Peritoneal Sclerosis (EPS)Rare, late; bowel obstruction; high mortality; stop PD

7. Dialysis Complications

Intradialytic Hypotension (IDH)

FeatureManagement
DefinitionSBP drop >20 mmHg or MAP drop >10 mmHg + symptoms
CausesRapid UF, autonomic dysfunction, antihypertensives, low dialysate Ca, acetate buffer (historical)
PreventionSodium profiling, cooled dialysate (35–35.5°C), midodrine, reduce UF rate, avoid pre-dialysis antihypertensives
TreatmentStop UF, Trendelenburg, IV saline bolus (100–200mL)

Dialysis Disequilibrium Syndrome (DDS)

FeatureManagement
MechanismRapid urea clearance → cerebral oedema (osmotic gradient)
RiskFirst HD, severe uraemia (Cr >800), elderly, children
SymptomsHeadache, nausea, confusion, seizures, coma
PreventionGentle first HD (low Qb, short duration, high dialysate Na, mannitol)
TreatmentStop HD, hypertonic saline, mannitol, airway protection

Access Complications

ComplicationAVF/AVGCVC
ThrombosisThrombectomy, thrombolysis, revisionThrombolysis (alteplase), exchange
StenosisAngioplasty (balloon), stentingFibrin sheath stripping, exchange
InfectionRare (superficial)CRBSI (blood cultures ×2, catheter-tip); remove if refractory/Staph aureus/fungal
AneurysmSurgical repair if large/painfulN/A
Steal SyndromeIschaemic hand; banding, revisionN/A

Metabolic & Other

ComplicationManagement
HyperkalaemiaLow-K+ dialysate (1mmol/L), insulin/dextrose pre-HD, avoid high-K+ foods
HypokalaemiaHigher K+ dialysate (2–3mmol/L), supplement
Dialysis Amyloidosisβ2-microglobulin deposition (carpal tunnel, cysts, arthropathy); high-flux dialysers reduce
CrampsReduce UF rate, hypertonic saline, quinine (prophylactic)
Itch (Pruritus)Optimise PO4, Ca; gabapentin, UVB, montelukast, difelikefalin (KOR agonist)

8. HD vs PD — Patient Factors

FactorFavours HDFavours PD
Vascular AccessGood vessels for AVFFailed access / no vessels
HaemodynamicsStableUnstable (but PD also ok)
Residual Renal FunctionLess criticalCritical (preserves RRF)
Home EnvironmentNot suitableSuitable for home
Patient PreferenceUnit-based, professional careAutonomy, flexibility, travel
Abdominal SurgeryNo issueAdhesions → contraindicated
ObesityHD often easierPD possible but UF harder
Heart FailureHD may worsenPD better volume control

9. High-Yield FCPS/MRCP Points

[!important]
- AEIOU = absolute RRT indications (Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia)
- IHD = 3x/week, 3–4h; AVF gold standard (radiocephalic); CVC high infection
- CRRT = haemodynamically unstable; citrate anticoagulation preferred; dose 20–25 mL/kg/h
- PD = CAPD (4 exchanges) or APD (cycler); Tenckhoff catheter; peritonitis = cloudy effluent + pain
- Adequacy: IHD spKt/V ≥1.2, URR ≥65%; PD weekly Kt/V ≥1.7, CrCl ≥45 L/week
- IDH: prevent with sodium profiling, cool dialysate, midodrine; treat: stop UF, IV saline
- DDS: first HD, severe uraemia; prevent: gentle HD (low Qb, short, high Na dialysate)
- Peritonitis (PD): cloudy effluent + pain; cell count >100/μL; empiric IP vancomycin + ceftazidime
- Access: AVF > AVG > CVC (Fistula First); CVC = CRBSI risk
- Anticoagulation: HD = heparin/citrate; CRRT = citrate preferred; PD = none
- PD preserves residual renal function — key advantage

10. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

TrapCorrection
CRRT = only for AKIAlso for ESRD with instability; fluid overload
PD = only for young/healthyAlso for elderly; better QoL for some
CVC = acceptable long-term accessCVC = last resort; high infection, stenosis; aim for AVF
IDH = just give salinePrevention better: sodium profiling, cool dialysate, midodrine, reduce UF
DDS = only first HDCan occur in any session if aggressive; prevent with gentle HD
Citrate anticoagulation = no monitoringMonitor post-filter ionised Ca (0.25–0.35), acid-base (metabolic alkalosis risk)
PD adequacy = only Kt/VResidual renal function critical — include in total Kt/V
Peritonitis = always remove catheterRemove if refractory, fungal, Staph aureus, multiple episodes
AVF ready immediately6–8 weeks maturation; use CVC meanwhile
All patients need same HD doseLarger patients need more; spKt/V ≥1.2 per session

11. Mnemonics

  • AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolytes (K+), Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia = AEIOU
  • Access Hierarchy: AVF → AVG → CVC = AA C (Fistula First)
  • CRRT Dose: 20–25 mL/kg/h effluent = 20-25
  • IDH Prevention: Sodium profiling, Cool dialysate, Midodrine, Reduce UF = SCMR
  • DDS Prevention: Gentle HD (low Qb, short, high Na) = GH
  • PD Peritonitis: Cloudy Effluent + Pain = CEP (cell count >100, IP abx)
  • CRRT Anticoagulation: Citrate Regional = CR (target post-filter iCa 0.25–0.35)
  • IHD Adequacy: spKt/V ≥1.2, URR ≥65% = sU

12. Mind Map

```mermaid

mindmap

root((Renal Replacement Therapy))

Indications

AEIOU[Acidosis, Electrolytes, Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia]

Chronic[ESRD G5]

Modalities

IHD[3x/week, AVF gold, Heparin/Citrate]

CRRT[Continuous, Unstable, Citrate, 20-25 mL/kg/h]

PD[CAPD/APD, Tenckhoff, Preserves RRF]

IHD

Access[AVF 1st, AVG, CVC last]

Prescription[Qb 300-400, Qd 500-800, 3-4h]

Adequacy[spKt/V >=1.2, URR >=65%]

Complications[IDH, DDS, Access, Amyloidosis]

CRRT

Types[CVVH, CVVHD, CVVHDF]

Dose[20-25 mL/kg/h effluent]

Anticoagulation[Citrate preferred]

Indications[Unstable, Fluid overload, Raised ICP]

PD

Types[CAPD (4ex), APD (cycler)]

Catheter[Tenckhoff, 2wk break-in]

Adequacy[wk Kt/V >=1.7, CrCl >=45, RRF critical]

Complications[Peritonitis (cloudy+pain), Hernia, EPS, UF failure]

Complications

IDH[Prevent: Na profiling, Cool, Midodrine]

DDS[Gentle HD: low Qb, short, high Na]

Access[Thrombosis, Stenosis, Infection, Steal]

Metabolic[K, PO4, Amyloidosis, Itch]

```

13. 24-Hour Recall Prompts

  1. AEIOU indications for urgent RRT
  2. HD: 3x/week, AVF gold standard (radiocephalic), CVC last resort
  3. CRRT: haemodynamically unstable AKI; citrate anticoagulation; 20–25 mL/kg/h
  4. PD: CAPD (4 exchanges) or APD (cycler); Tenckhoff catheter; preserves RRF
  5. Adequacy: IHD spKt/V ≥1.2, URR ≥65%; PD wk Kt/V ≥1.7, CrCl ≥45
  6. IDH prevention: sodium profiling, cool dialysate, midodrine, reduce UF
  7. DDS prevention: gentle first HD (low Qb, short, high Na dialysate)
  8. PD peritonitis: cloudy effluent + pain; cell count >100; IP vancomycin + ceftazidime
  9. Access hierarchy: AVF > AVG > CVC
  10. Anticoagulation: HD heparin/citrate; CRRT citrate; PD none
  11. Citrate monitoring: post-filter ionised Ca 0.25–0.35

14. 7-Day / 15-Day / 30-Day Revision Tracker

DayDateRecall (1-5)Notes
1
7
15
30

15. Must Know / Should Know / Nice to Know

PriorityContent
Must Know 🔴AEIOU, HD/CRRT/PD modalities & indications, AVF hierarchy, adequacy targets, IDH/DDS prevention, PD peritonitis, anticoagulation, access complications
Should Know 🟡CRRT modalities (CVVH/DF/HDF), PD types (CAPD/APD), citrate monitoring, steal syndrome, amyloidosis, icodextrin, EPS
Nice to Know 🟢Novel sorbents (AST-120), wearable dialysis, home HD, incremental PD, cost-effectiveness, QoL outcomes, SGLT2i in dialysis

16. MCQs (10)

  1. AEIOU mnemonic for RRT indications — "E" stands for:

A. Edema

B. Electrolytes (K+ >6.5 or ECG changes)

C. Encephalopathy

D. Euvolemia

E. ESRD

  1. Gold standard vascular access for haemodialysis:

A. CVC

B. AVG

C. AVF (radiocephalic)

D. Femoral catheter

E. Subclavian catheter

  1. CRRT preferred anticoagulation (KDIGO):

A. Unfractionated heparin

B. Regional citrate

C. LMWH

D. Argatroban

E. No anticoagulation

  1. CRRT effluent dose target (KDIGO):

A. 10–15 mL/kg/h

B. 20–25 mL/kg/h

C. 30–35 mL/kg/h

D. 40–45 mL/kg/h

E. 50 mL/kg/h

  1. PD peritonitis — diagnostic criteria:

A. Abdominal pain only

B. Cloudy effluent + abdominal pain + cell count >100/μL (neutrophils >50%)

C. Fever only

D. Positive culture only

E. Cloudy effluent only

  1. PD peritonitis — empiric intraperitoneal antibiotics:

A. Vancomycin alone

B. Vancomycin + ceftazidime (or aminoglycoside)

C. Ceftazidime alone

D. Piperacillin-tazobactam

E. Meropenem

  1. Intradialytic hypotension (IDH) — prevention:

A. Increase UF rate

B. Hot dialysate

C. Sodium profiling, cooled dialysate (35–35.5°C), midodrine

D. Stop antihypertensives permanently

E. Increase blood flow

  1. Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome (DDS) — high risk patients:

A. Chronic HD patients

B. First HD, severe uraemia (Cr >800), elderly, children

C. Only diabetic patients

D. Only CRRT patients

E. Only PD patients

  1. spKt/V target for thrice-weekly HD:

A. ≥1.0

B. ≥1.2 (prefer ≥1.4)

C. ≥1.6

D. ≥2.0

E. ≥0.8

  1. CVC vs AVF — major disadvantage of CVC:

A. Better clearance

B. Higher infection rate (CRBSI)

C. Longer maturation

D. Lower cost

E. No stenosis risk

17. SBA Questions (10)

  1. 55-year-old man, septic shock, AKI (Cr 450), oliguric, fluid overloaded, on noradrenaline. RRT modality:

A. IHD

B. CRRT (CVVHDF)

C. PD

D. SLED

E. Conservative

  1. 60-year-old diabetic, ESRD, suitable vessels, wants autonomy, travels frequently. Best modality:

A. In-centre HD

B. PD (APD)

C. Home HD

D. Satellite HD

E. PD (CAPD)

  1. New HD patient, first session, Cr 900, urea 60. Prescription to prevent DDS:

A. Qb 400, 4h, standard dialysate Na

B. Qb 200, 2h, high dialysate Na (150–160), consider mannitol

C. Qb 300, 3h, low dialysate Na

D. Standard prescription

E. CVVHDF instead

  1. HD patient, 2h into session, SBP drops 90/50, dizzy, cramps. UF rate 1.5L/h. Immediate action:

A. Increase dialysate Na

B. Stop UF, Trendelenburg, IV saline 100–200mL

C. Give midodrine IV

D. Stop HD, give albumin

E. Reduce blood flow

  1. PD patient, cloudy effluent, abdominal pain, effluent WBC 500/μL (80% neutrophils). Empiric IP antibiotics:

A. Vancomycin alone

B. Vancomycin + ceftazidime

C. Ceftazidime alone

D. Gentamicin alone

E. Oral ciprofloxacin

  1. CRRT with regional citrate — target post-filter ionised calcium:

A. 0.5–0.7 mmol/L

B. 0.25–0.35 mmol/L

C. 0.8–1.0 mmol/L

D. 1.0–1.2 mmol/L

E. Same as systemic

  1. HD patient with AVF, hand pain, pallor, paraesthesia on dialysis. Diagnosis:

A. Steal syndrome

B. Steal syndrome (access flow > cardiac output to hand)

C. Carpal tunnel

D. Ulnar neuropathy

E. Access infection

  1. PD patient, long dwell (night), poor UF. High transporter on PET. Solution:

A. Increase glucose concentration

B. Icodextrin 7.5% for long dwell

C. Shorten dwell time

D. Switch to HD

E. Add extra exchange

  1. HD adequacy — spKt/V 1.0 (target ≥1.2). Action:

A. Increase dialysate flow only

B. Increase time, blood flow, or dialyser surface area (K); check access recirculation

C. Increase UF

C. Decrease frequency

E. Switch to PD

  1. AVF maturation — minimum time before cannulation:

A. 2 weeks

B. 6–8 weeks

C. 4 weeks

D. 10 weeks

E. 12 weeks

18. Flashcards

  • Q: AEIOU indications?

A: Acidosis, Electrolytes (K+), Ingestion, Overload, Uraemia

  • Q: HD vascular access hierarchy?

A: AVF (radiocephalic) > AVG > CVC

  • Q: CRRT dose?

A: 20–25 mL/kg/h effluent

  • Q: CRRT anticoagulation?

A: Regional citrate preferred

  • Q: PD types?

A: CAPD (4 manual exchanges), APD (cycler nightly)

  • Q: PD adequacy?

A: Weekly Kt/V ≥1.7, CrCl ≥45 L/week, RRF critical

  • Q: PD peritonitis diagnosis?

A: Cloudy effluent + pain + cell count >100/μL (neutrophils >50%)

  • Q: PD peritonitis empiric IP abx?

A: Vancomycin + ceftazidime (or aminoglycoside)

  • Q: IDH prevention?

A: Sodium profiling, cool dialysate (35.5°C), midodrine, reduce UF

  • Q: DDS high risk?

A: First HD, severe uraemia, elderly, children

  • Q: DDS prevention?

A: Gentle HD (low Qb, short, high Na dialysate, mannitol)

  • Q: spKt/V target HD?

A: ≥1.2 (prefer ≥1.4)

  • Q: Access infection highest?

A: CVC (CRBSI)

  • Q: Citrate target post-filter iCa?

A: 0.25–0.35 mmol/L

  • Q: AVF maturation?

A: 6–8 weeks

19. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — E = Electrolytes (hyperkalaemia)
  2. C — AVF radiocephalic = gold standard
  3. B — Regional citrate preferred for CRRT
  4. B — KDIGO: 20–25 mL/kg/h effluent
  5. B — Cloudy effluent + pain + cell count >100 (neutrophils >50%)
  6. B — IP vancomycin + ceftazidime (covers Gram+ and Gram-)
  7. C — Sodium profiling, cool dialysate, midodrine prevent IDH
  8. B — First HD, severe uraemia, elderly, children
  9. B — spKt/V ≥1.2 (prefer 1.4)
  10. B — CVC = high CRBSI risk

SBAs

  1. B — Septic shock + fluid overload = CRRT (haemodynamically unstable)
  2. B — Autonomy + travel = APD (PD)
  3. B — First HD + severe uraemia = gentle HD (low Qb, short, high Na, mannitol)
  4. B — IDH = stop UF, Trendelenburg, IV saline bolus
  5. B — Cloudy effluent + high WBC = peritonitis; IP vancomycin + ceftazidime
  6. B — Citrate regional anticoagulation: post-filter iCa 0.25–0.35
  7. B — Hand ischaemia on HD with AVF = steal syndrome
  8. B — High transporter = poor long-dwell UF; icodextrin (colloid osmotic) for long dwell
  9. B — spKt/V low = increase K (time, Qb, dialyser); check recirculation
  10. B — AVF needs 6–8 weeks maturation

20. Summary

Renal Replacement Therapy is a Must Know 🔴 topic.

Key takeaway: AEIOU = urgent RRT indications. HD: 3x/week, AVF gold standard (radiocephalic), CVC last resort; spKt/V ≥1.2. CRRT: haemodynamically unstable; citrate anticoagulation; 20–25 mL/kg/h. PD: CAPD/APD; Tenckhoff catheter; preserves RRF; weekly Kt/V ≥1.7; peritonitis = cloudy effluent + pain + >100 WBC; IP vancomycin + ceftazidime. IDH prevention: sodium profiling, cool dialysate, midodrine. DDS prevention: gentle first HD. Access: AVF > AVG > CVC. Citrate: post-filter iCa 0.25–0.35.

Exam focus: AEIOU, modality selection, AVF hierarchy, adequacy targets, IDH/DDS, PD peritonitis, citrate anticoagulation, access complications, steal syndrome, icodextrin for high transporters.

Clinical relevance: Modality choice impacts survival, QoL, healthcare costs; Fistula First reduces CVC complications; PD preserves residual function.

topics.title: Kidney Transplantation
topics.html:
